target: The study aimed to look at the use of a camera on the tip of a curved blade to diagnose and remove objects in the throat that shouldn’t be eaten.

source: Method: In total, 1572 patients diagnosed with throat FBs at the Department of Otolaryngology of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were retrospectively analysed.
target: We studied 1572 people who had objects in the throat that shouldn't be eaten.

source: The covariables collected were the time from FB ingestion to admission, age, sex, duration of admission, and site of impaction.
target: We wrote down the time between eating the object and coming to the hospital, age, gender, how long the patient was in the hospital, and where the object was stuck.

source: Result: The most common FBs were fish bones, which accounted for 1446 (91.98%) of 1572 FBs.
target: The most common objects stuck were fish bones, which made up 1446 (91.98%) of 1572 objects.

source: Among all 1572 FBs, 1004 (63.87%) were successfully removed by video laryngoscopy without complications.
target: Among all 1572 objects, 1004 (63.87%) were successfully removed using a camera on the tip of a curved blade without problems.

source: A shorter duration of admission was associated with a higher diagnostic rate under video laryngoscopy.
target: When a camera on the tip of a curved blade was used to diagnose the object, people left the hospital sooner.

source: The diagnostic rate of sharp FBs was significantly higher than that of non-sharp FBs.
target: Diagnosing sharp objects was higher than that of non-sharp objects.

source: The most common sites of throat FBs were the tongue root (42.29%), epiglottic vallecula (19.40%), tonsil (18.21%), and piriform fossa (10.65%).
target: The most common place that objects were stuck in the throat were the far back and bottom of the tongue (42.29%), the space between the back of the tongue and the windpipe entrance (19.40%), the tonsil (18.21%), and the bottom of the throat (10.65%).

source: Conclusion: Video laryngoscopy is a powerful tool for the diagnosis and treatment of throat FBs, allowing for identification of rare locations of FBs as well as refractory FBs.
target: We conclude that using a camera on the tip of a curved blade is a strong tool for the diagnosis and removal of objects in the throat that shouldn't be eaten, allowing for the finding of uncommon locations of objects and of objects not easily removed.

source: Objective: This study was designed to explore the clinical application of video laryngoscopy in the diagnosis and treatment of throat foreign bodies (FBs).
target: This work explored the use of video viewing of the voice box with a small device to identify and treat throat foreign bodies (FBs) or substances.

source: Method: In total, 1572 patients diagnosed with throat FBs at the Department of Otolaryngology of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were retrospectively analysed.
target: 1572 total patients with throat foreign substances at the Department of Otolarynology of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were analysed.

source: The covariables collected were the time from FB ingestion to admission, age, sex, duration of admission, and site of impaction.
target: Time from foreign substance consumption to hospital admission, age, sex, duration of admission, and site of blockage were measured.

source: Result: The most common FBs were fish bones, which accounted for 1446 (91.98%) of 1572 FBs.
target: The most common foreign substance was fish bones, seen in  91.98% of patients.

source: Among all 1572 FBs, 1004 (63.87%) were successfully removed by video laryngoscopy without complications.
target: Among all 1572 foreign substances, 1004 (63.87%) were removed by video throat treatment with a small device without issues.

source: A shorter duration of admission was associated with a higher diagnostic rate under video laryngoscopy.
target: A shorter hospital stay was linked to higher identification rate by video viewing of the voice box with a small device.

source: The diagnostic rate of sharp FBs was significantly higher than that of non-sharp FBs.
target: The identification rate of sharp foreign substances was higher than that of non-sharp ones.

source: The most common sites of throat FBs were the tongue root (42.29%), epiglottic vallecula (19.40%), tonsil (18.21%), and piriform fossa (10.65%).
target: The most common sites of throat foreign substances were areas near the tonsil and bottom of the tongue and throat.

source: Conclusion: Video laryngoscopy is a powerful tool for the diagnosis and treatment of throat FBs, allowing for identification of rare locations of FBs as well as refractory FBs.
target: Video viewing of the voice box with a small device is powerful for identifying and treating throat foreign substances. It allows identification of rare locations of foreign substances and stubborn ones.

source: Achalasia is an esophageal motility disorder characterized by aberrant peristalsis and insufficient relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.
target: Achalasia describes a disorder in which your esophagus is unable to move food and liquids down into your stomach.

source: Patients most commonly present with dysphagia to solids and liquids, regurgitation, and occasional chest pain with or without weight loss.
target: People often have difficulty swallowing solids and liquids, spitting up undigested or partially digested food from the stomach, and occasional chest pain with or without weight loss.

source: High-resolution manometry has identified 3 subtypes of achalasia distinguished by pressurization and contraction patterns.
target: Measuring the strength and muscle coordination of your esophagus when you swallow led to the finding of 3 subtypes of achalasia that have different pressure and contraction patterns.

source: Endoscopic findings of retained saliva with puckering of the gastroesophageal junction or esophagram findings of a dilated esophagus with bird beaking are important diagnostic clues.
target: A lot of saliva and puckering where the esophagus is connected to the stomach, seen using a flexible tube with a camera, or a widened esophagus and narrowing where the esophagus is connected to the stomach, that looks like a bird's beak, found by X-raying the esophagus suggest achalasia.

source: In this American College of Gastroenterology guideline, we used the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation process to provide clinical guidance on how best to diagnose and treat patients with achalasia.
target: In this guideline, we used a popular method to guide doctors on how best to diagnose and treat people with achalasia.

source: Dysphagia is common but may be underreported.
target: Trouble swallowing is common but may be underreported.

source: Specific symptoms, rather than their perceived location, should guide the initial evaluation and imaging.
target: Specific symptoms, not the area where they are thought to come from, should guide the first exam and visual tests.

source: Obstructive symptoms that seem to originate in the throat or neck may actually be caused by distal esophageal lesions.
target: Symptoms of difficulty breathing that seem to come from the throat or neck may actually be caused by damage in the lower esophagus.

source: Oropharyngeal dysphagia manifests as difficulty initiating swallowing, coughing, choking, or aspiration, and it is most commonly caused by chronic neurologic conditions such as stroke, Parkinson disease, or dementia.
target: Difficulty swallowing that happens in the mouth or the throat shows up as difficulty starting swallowing, coughing, choking, or something entering the airway or lungs by accident. It is most often caused by long-term brain conditions such as stroke, Parkinson's, or memory, language, and thinking loss.

source: Symptoms should be thoroughly evaluated because of the risk of aspiration.
target: Symptoms should be looked at closely due to the risk of something entering the airway or lungs by accident.

source: Patients with esophageal dysphagia may report a sensation of food getting stuck after swallowing.
target: People with problems that happen during swallowing may describe a feeling of food getting stuck after swallowing.

source: This condition is most commonly caused by gastroesophageal reflux disease and functional esophageal disorders.
target: Problems that happen during swallowing is most often caused by stomach-related reflux diseases and disorders of the esophagus with symptoms like heartburn and chest pain.

source: Eosinophilic esophagitis is triggered by food allergens and is increasingly prevalent; esophageal biopsies should be performed to make the diagnosis.
target: A long-term allergic condition of the esophagus is set off by food allergens and is more and more common. To diagnose this condition, a small part of the esophagus should be removed for examination.

source: Esophageal motility disorders such as achalasia are relatively rare and may be overdiagnosed.
target: Dysfunction of the esophagus that causes difficulty swallowing such as a disorder in which your esophagus is unable to move food and liquids down into your stomach are pretty rare and may be overdiagnosed.

source: Opioid-induced esophageal dysfunction is becoming more common.
target: Dysfunction of the esophagus caused by opioids is becoming more common.

source: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy is recommended for the initial evaluation of esophageal dysphagia, with barium esophagography as an adjunct.
target: Using a flexible tube with a camera to look at the esophagus, stomach and part of the small intestine is used to look at difficulty swallowing that happens in the mouth and throat, together with barium and X-rays.

source: Esophageal cancer and other serious conditions have a low prevalence, and testing in low-risk patients may be deferred while a four-week trial of acid-suppressing therapy is undertaken.
target: Cancer of the esophagus and other serious conditions are not common, and low-risk people may take acid reducers for 4 weeks before undergoing testing.

source: Many frail older adults with progressive neurologic disease have significant but unrecognized dysphagia, which significantly increases their risk of aspiration pneumonia and malnourishment.
target: Many weak older adults with progressive deterioration in functioning have serious but unseen difficulty swallowing, which really increases their risk of pneumonia (lung infection) caused by something entering the airway or lungs by accident and risk of lacking nutrients in the body.

source: In these patients, the diagnosis of dysphagia should prompt a discussion about goals of care before potentially harmful interventions are considered.
target: In these people, the diagnosis of difficulty breathing should lead to a conversation about what is important to the person before suggesting possibly harmful treatments.

source: Speech-language pathologists and other specialists, in collaboration with family physicians, can provide structured assessments and make appropriate recommendations for safe swallowing, palliative care, or rehabilitation.
target: People who treat communication and swallowing disorders and other specialists, working with family doctors, can evaluate and make suggestions for safe swallowing, care for people living with a serious illness, or rehab.

source: The local, systemic, and referred causes of finger pain are generally recognizable by historical features and physical examination findings, although radiographs and laboratory evaluation are often required to support the diagnostic impression.
target: The causes of finger pain can usually be found by learning about the duration and severity of the problem and doing a physical examination. However, x-rays and lab tests are often used to identify the cause of the pain.

source: Most minor traumatic causes of finger pain require only conservative management, including immobilization followed by exercise.
target: Most minor traumatic injuries that cause finger pain only need non-invasive (no surgery) therapy, such as resting followed by exercise.

source: Infectious causes of finger pain include cellulitis, tendinitis, paronychia, felon, and infectious emboli, which generally require antibiotics with or without drainage.
target: Finger pain caused by an infection (germs that build in the body causing illness) usually require medicines that fight bacteria called antibiotics. A process called drainage that removes extra fluid from a wound is sometimes used with antibiotics.

source: Certain patients with finger pain resulting from infection should be referred to a hand surgeon.
target: Sometimes patients with finger pain from an infection will see a hand surgeon.

source: Vascular and ischemic causes of finger pain represent true emergencies, because tissue viability is dependent on prompt intervention.
target: Limited or no blood flow to or through the tissues in the finger can cause finger pain, are signs of a true emergency, and require immediate attention to prevent more damage to the tissues.

source: Whereas any sensory neuropathy may present with finger pain, carpal tunnel syndrome is among the most common.
target: Patients with nerve damage may have finger pain. Carpal tunnel syndrome (pain or tingling in the hand or arm caused by a pinched wrist nerve) is a common example of pain caused by nerve damage.

source: Systemic rheumatic disease, such as rheumatoid arthritis or vasculitis, may begin with finger pain.
target: Diseases that can cause your immune system to attack your joints, muscles, bones and organs (called systemic rheumatic diseases) can begin with finger pain.

source: In addition, such pain may be the first manifestation of a serious systemic illness, as in hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy.
target: In addition, finger pain may be the first sign of a more serious illness, such as in hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (a rare disease with irritation around the bone and enlarged fingertips).

source: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy is an example of referred pain, presumably by way of neural mechanisms.
target: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (a disease causing long-lasting pain in one or more limbs) is an example of when an injury in one part to the body causes pain in a different part of the body.

source: Certain infectious, traumatic, and ischemic causes of finger pain must be diagnosed promptly to avoid significant morbidity; depending upon the cause of the symptoms, referral to a hand surgeon, rheumatologist, or neurologist may be appropriate.
target: Certain infections, traumatic injuries, and blood flow problems that cause finger pain must be identified quickly to prevent long-term or more serious damage. In some cases, going to a hand surgeon, a rheumatologist (doctor who treats arthritis and other joint, muscle, and bone diseases), or a neurologist (brain and nerve doctor) is needed.

source: Symptomatic and functional improvement may also be hastened by the input of an occupational therapist.
target: Progress in reducing pain and improving movement in the finger may come faster with the help of an occupational therapist, people who help patients with injuries, illnesses, and disabilities build or restore their abilities to do everyday tasks in life.

source: The local, systemic, and referred causes of finger pain are generally recognizable by historical features and physical examination findings, although radiographs and laboratory evaluation are often required to support the diagnostic impression.
target: Causes of finger pain are usually recognizable by past characteristics and findings from a physical checkup. However, radiographs (images created usually by x-rays) and other lab tests are usually required to support the identification.

source: Most minor traumatic causes of finger pain require only conservative management, including immobilization followed by exercise.
target: Most minor causes of finger pain need only mild treatment, like restraining the finger from moving and exercise.

source: Infectious causes of finger pain include cellulitis, tendinitis, paronychia, felon, and infectious emboli, which generally require antibiotics with or without drainage.
target: Causes of finger pain from bacteria include cellulitis (a bacterial skin infection), tendinitis (an irritation of the tendon), paronychia (a bacterial nail infection), felon (a bacterial fingertip infection), and emboli (a blood vessel blockage). These causes generally require antibacterials alomg with or without fluid drainage of the finger.

source: Certain patients with finger pain resulting from infection should be referred to a hand surgeon.
target: Some patients with finger pain from bacteria should be sent to a hand surgeon.

source: Vascular and ischemic causes of finger pain represent true emergencies, because tissue viability is dependent on prompt intervention.
target: Causes of finger pain related to blood vessels represent real emergencies that depend on quick treatment.

source: Whereas any sensory neuropathy may present with finger pain, carpal tunnel syndrome is among the most common.
target: When any nerve damage leads to finger pain, a pinched nerve in the wrist is usually the most common.

source: Systemic rheumatic disease, such as rheumatoid arthritis or vasculitis, may begin with finger pain.
target: Full-body autoimmune diseases (diseases in which infection-preventing cells attack healthy cells), which may cause joint pain or irritation in the blood vessels, might start with finger pain.

source: In addition, such pain may be the first manifestation of a serious systemic illness, as in hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy.
target: Also, finger pain may be the first sign of a serious full-body illness, as in hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy, a rare disease with irritation around the bone and joints and enlarged fingertips.

source: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy is an example of referred pain, presumably by way of neural mechanisms.
target: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (a disease causing long-lasting limb pain) is an example of pain felt in an area away from its source, possibly by nerve signaling issues.

source: Certain infectious, traumatic, and ischemic causes of finger pain must be diagnosed promptly to avoid significant morbidity; depending upon the cause of the symptoms, referral to a hand surgeon, rheumatologist, or neurologist may be appropriate.
target: Some bacteria-related, injury-related, and blood vessel-related causes of finger pain must be checked quickly to avoid serious harm. Depending on the cause, help from a hand surgeon, rheumatologist (a doctor who treats musculoskeletal issues), or neurologist (a doctor who treats nerve-related issues) may be needed.

source: Symptomatic and functional improvement may also be hastened by the input of an occupational therapist.
target: Recovery may be quickened with occupational therapists that help patients perform everyday activities.

source: Objective: To identify through case study the presentation and possible pathophysiological cause of complex regional pain syndrome and its preferential response to stellate ganglion blockade.
target: The objective of this case report (a summary of an individual patient's symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment) is to identify the sign or symptoms and causes of complex regional pain syndrome (a form of ongoing pain that affects the arm or leg) and if a pain treatment called stellate ganglion blockade (an injection of medication into nerves at the front of the neck that can relieve pain) has any effect on the pain.

source: Setting: Complex regional pain syndrome can occur in an extremity after minor injury, fracture, surgery, peripheral nerve insult or spontaneously and is characterised by spontaneous pain, changes in skin temperature and colour, oedema, and motor disturbances.
target: Complex regional pain syndrome can occur in the arms or legs after a minor injury, fracture, surgery, or nerve damage. It appears as sudden, random pain, changes in skin temperatures and color, swelling in ankles, feet or legs, and uncontrollable movements in the body.

source: Pathophysiology is likely to involve peripheral and central components and neurological and inflammatory elements.
target: Disease-related physical changes are likely to involve outer and central parts, nerve-related elements, and inflammatory elements, the body's natural response to injury or infection.

source: There is no consistent approach to treatment with a wide variety of specialists involved.
target: There is no consistent approach to treatment with a wide variety of specialists involved.

source: Diagnosis can be difficult, with over-diagnosis resulting from undue emphasis placed upon pain disproportionate to an inciting event despite the absence of other symptoms or under-diagnosed when subtle symptoms are not recognised.
target: Diagnosis can be difficult. Sometimes it is over-diagnosed (diagnosis of a medical condition that would never have caused any symptoms or problems) from too much attention on the pain even though other symptoms are not present. Sometimes it can be under-diagnosed (diagnosed less often than its occurrence) when less obvious symptioms are not recognized.

source: The International Association for the Study of Pain supports the use of sympathetic blocks to reduce sympathetic nervous system overactivity and relieve complex regional pain symptoms.
target: A professional medical association for the study of pain supports the kind of pain block used in stellate ganglion blockade to reduce sensory nerves and relieve pain sympoms.

source: Educational reviews promote stellate ganglion blockade as beneficial.
target: Educational reviews promote stellate ganglion blockade as beneficial.

source: Three blocks were given at 8, 10 and 13 months after the initial injury under local anaesthesia and sterile conditions.
target: Three blocks (injections) were given at 8, 10 and 13 months after the initial injury under local anaesthesia and clean conditions.

source: Physiotherapeutic input was delivered under block conditions to maximise joint and tissue mobility and facilitate restoration of function.
target: Physical therapy was provided after the patient received an injection to allow more movement of the joint and tissues and to help restore use of the injured finger.

source: Conclusion: This case demonstrates the need for practitioners from all disciplines to be able to identify the clinical characteristics of complex regional pain syndrome to instigate immediate treatment and supports the notion that stellate ganglion blockade is preferable to upper limb intravenous regional anaesthetic block for refractory index finger pain associated with complex regional pain syndrome.
target: This case shows that healthcare providers need to be able to identify clinical signs of complex regional pain syndrome to start treatment immediately. It also shows that stellate ganglion blockade is a preferable method to an IV of local anaesthetic (one-time injection of medicine that numbs a small area of the body) in the upper arm to help finger pain associated with complex regional pain syndrome.

source: Background and aims: In previous studies, we successfully applied Low Level Laser Therapy (LLLT) in patients with non-specific chronic pain of the shoulder joint and lower back.
target: To treat patients with chronic shoulder and low back pain, previous studies have successfully used a treatment called low level laser therapy (LLLT), a laser light used at a low level and applied to the skin of the body to reduce pain or inflammation and help heal wounds, tissues, and nerves.

source: The purpose of the present study was to assess the effectiveness of LLLT for chronic joint pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.
target: The purpose of this current study is to find out if LLLT also helps patients with chronic joint pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.

source: Subjects and methods: Nine male and 15 female patients with chronic joint pain of the elbow, wrist, or fingers, who were treated at the rehabilitation outpatient clinic at our hospital from April, 2007 to March, 2009 were enrolled in the study.
target: This study included 9 male and 15 female patients with chronic joint pain in the elbow, wrist, or fingers.

source: Each tender point and three points around it were irradiated with laser energy.
target: The tender point (specific area of pain) and three points surrounding the pain were exposed to the LLLT therapy light.

source: Each point was irradiated twice for 20 s per treatment, giving a total of three minutes for all 4 points.
target: Each area was exposed two times for 20 seconds per treatment, giving a total of three minutes for all 4 points.

source: Patients visited the clinic twice a week, and were evaluated after four weeks of treatment.
target: Patients visited the clinic twice a week, and were evaluated after 4 weeks of treatment with LLLT (laser therapy).

source: Pain was evaluated with a Visual Analogue Scale (VAS).
target: A tool called the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) was used to measure pain by asking patients to report how intense their pain is on a scale.

source: Statistical analysis of the VAS scores after laser irradiation was performed with Wilcoxon's signed rank sum test, using SPSS Ver.17.
target: The study analyzed VAS scores (how patients described their pain) after they received the LLLT laser treatment.

source: Results: All VAS scores were totaled and statistically analyzed.
target: All VAS scores that document pain intensity were totaled and analyzed.

source: The average VAS score before irradiation was 59.2±12.9, and 33.1±12.2 after the irradiation, showing a significant improvement in VAS score (p<0.001) after treatment.
target: There was significant improvement in how much pain patients felt after they received the treatment.

source: The treatment effect lasted for about one and a half days in the case of wrist pain, epicondylitis lateralis (tennis elbow), and carpal tunnel syndrome.
target: The effect of the laser treatment lasted for about one and half days for patients with wrist pain, tennis elbow, and carpal tunnel syndrome (pinched nerve in the wrist).

source: In other pain entities, it lasted for about three to fifteen hours.
target: In patients with other types of pain, the effect lasted for about 3-15 hours.

source: No change in the range of motion (ROM) was seen in any of the 24 subjects.
target: There was no change in how far patients could move or stretch the injured part of the body after the treatment.

source: Conclusion: We concluded that LLLT at the wavelength and parameters used in the present study was effective for chronic pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.
target: This study found that low level laser therapy was helpful for chronic pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.

source: Background and aims: In previous studies, we successfully applied Low Level Laser Therapy (LLLT) in patients with non-specific chronic pain of the shoulder joint and lower back.
target: In previous studies, we used Low Level Laser Therapy (LLLT), therapy with applying light on the body's surface, in patients with non-specific, long-lasting pain of the shoulder joint and lower back.

source: The purpose of the present study was to assess the effectiveness of LLLT for chronic joint pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.
target: This work measures the success of this specific light therapy for lasting joint pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.

source: Subjects and methods: Nine male and 15 female patients with chronic joint pain of the elbow, wrist, or fingers, who were treated at the rehabilitation outpatient clinic at our hospital from April, 2007 to March, 2009 were enrolled in the study.
target: Nine male and fifteen female patients with long-lasting joint pain of the elbow, wrist, or fingers were treated at our hospital's recovery clinic from April, 2007 to March, 2009. These patients were employed in the study.

source: Patients visited the clinic twice a week, and were evaluated after four weeks of treatment.
target: Patients visited the clinic twice a week and were checked after four weeks of treatment.

source: Pain was evaluated with a Visual Analogue Scale (VAS).
target: Pain was measured with a specific pain scale.

source: The average VAS score before irradiation was 59.2±12.9, and 33.1±12.2 after the irradiation, showing a significant improvement in VAS score (p<0.001) after treatment.
target: The average pain score noticeably improved after treatment.

source: The treatment effect lasted for about one and a half days in the case of wrist pain, epicondylitis lateralis (tennis elbow), and carpal tunnel syndrome.
target: The treatment effect lasted for about one and a half days regarding wrist pain, tennis elbow, and carpal tunnel syndrome, which involves pinched nerves in the wrist.

source: In other pain entities, it lasted for about three to fifteen hours.
target: In other pain types, the treatment effect lasted for about three to fifteen hours.

source: No change in the range of motion (ROM) was seen in any of the 24 subjects.
target: No change in the range of motion was seen in any of the 24 patients.

source: Conclusion: We concluded that LLLT at the wavelength and parameters used in the present study was effective for chronic pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.
target: LLLT at the settings used in this study was useful for long-lasting pain of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.

source: Upper extremity neuropathic pain states greatly impact patient functionality and quality of life, despite appropriate surgical intervention.
target: Pain felt in the arm that is caused by damaged nerves or a weakening nervous sytem is called neuropathic pain and can greatly impact movement and quality of life, even after a patient has surgery.

source: This article focuses on the advanced therapies that may improve pain care, including advanced treatment strategies that are available.
target: This article focuses on the new therapies that may improve pain care, including new treatment plans that are available.

source: The article also surveys therapies on the immediate horizon, such as spinal cord stimulation, peripheral nerve stimulation, and dorsal root ganglion spinal cord stimulation.
target: This article examines new and upcoming therapies, such as spinal cord simulation (electrical treatment to the spinal cord), peripheral nerve simulation (electrical treatment to the nerves), and dorsal root ganglion spinal cord stimulation (electrical treatment to spine cells).

source: As these therapies evolve, so too will their placement within the pain care algorithm grounded by a foundation of evidence to improve patient safety and management of patients with difficult neuropathic pain.
target: As these therapies evolve, so too will their placement within pain care plans.

source: Upper extremity neuropathic pain states greatly impact patient functionality and quality of life, despite appropriate surgical intervention.
target: Nerve-related, upper hand pain greatly affects patient performance and quality of life, even with proper treatment.

source: This article focuses on the advanced therapies that may improve pain care, including advanced treatment strategies that are available.
target: This article looks at leading treatments that may improve pain care, including advanced treatments that are available.

source: The article also surveys therapies on the immediate horizon, such as spinal cord stimulation, peripheral nerve stimulation, and dorsal root ganglion spinal cord stimulation.
target: This article also reviews upcoming therapies, like spinal cord electrical stimulation, peripheral or outer nerve stimulation, and dorsal root ganglion spinal cord stimulation (electrical treatment for specific cells in the spinal cord).

source: As these therapies evolve, so too will their placement within the pain care algorithm grounded by a foundation of evidence to improve patient safety and management of patients with difficult neuropathic pain.
target: As these therapies improve, so will their use in pain care grounded by evidence to improve patient safety and monitoring of patients with complex nerve-related pain.

source: Background: Hand osteoarthritis (OA) is a prevalent joint disease that may lead to pain, stiffness and problems in performing hand-related activities of daily living.
target: Hand osteoarthritis is a common joint disease that may lead to pain, stiffness and problems in performing hand-related activities of daily living.

source: Currently, no cure for OA is known, and non-pharmacological modalities are recommended as first-line care.
target: Currently, no cure for osteoarthritis is known, and using treatment methods that do not involve medications for pain are recommended as first-line care.

source: A positive effect of exercise in hip and knee OA has been documented, but the effect of exercise on hand OA remains uncertain.
target: A positive effect of exercise in hip and knee osteoarthritis has been documented, but the effect of exercise on hand osteoarthritis remains uncertain.

source: Authors' conclusions: When we pooled results from five studies, we found low-quality evidence showing small beneficial effects of exercise on hand pain, function and finger joint stiffness.
target: Researchers reviewed results from 5 studies and found low-quality evidence (the findings are difficult to interpret or are considered weak) showing small helpful effects of exercise on hand pain, function and finger joint stiffness.

source: Estimated effect sizes were small, and whether they represent a clinically important change may be debated.
target: The relationship between hand osteoarthritis and exercise were small in these studies, and it is not clear if any medical practices should change based on these 5 studies.

source: One study reported quality of life, and the effect is uncertain.
target: One study reported quality of life, and the effect is uncertain.

source: Three studies reported on adverse events, which were very few and were not severe.
target: Three studies reported on unexpected medical problems, which were very few and were not severe.

source: When patients present with acute or chronic hand and/or finger pain after an injury, try placing a pencil first over and then under the proximal phalanx of the finger that is generating the pain.
target: When a patient has sudden or ongoing hand and/or finger pain after an injury, a hand therapist may place a pencil over and under the first bone of the finger (that starts at the base from the hand) that is causing the pain.

source: Ask the patient to flex and extend the fingers several times.
target: The doctor will then ask the patient to flex and extend the fingers several times.

source: Putting the affected metacarpal phalangeal (MP) joint more relatively flexed or extended than the other MP joints will often take away the pain with active movement with the pencil in place.
target: Putting the first knuckle (the joint where the finger connects with the hand) in a more flexed and extended position than the other knuckles will often take away the pain with active movement when the pencil is in place.

source: When this happens, our hand therapist builds a relative motion splint that simulates the effect of the pencil.
target: The hand therapist will then build a relative motion splint (a piece of sturdy material to support injured bones and allow some movement) that will have the same effect of the pencil.

source: These are very functional splints that patients wear 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
target: These types of splints support initial movement and can be worn 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

source: Most people can work with these splints on.
target: Most people can work with these splints on.

source: Background: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is characterized by pain, functional disability, poor quality of life (QoL), high socioeconomic impact, and annual costs of over $56 billion in the United States.
target: Rheumatoid arthritis is a disease where the immune system attacks healthy cells in the body by mistake. It can cause pain, decrease movement, lead to poor quality of life, impact the social and economic parts of communities, and cost over $56 billion in the United States every year.

source: Acupuncture (AC) is widely in use; however, studies show severe methodological shortcomings, did not consider the functional diagnosis for the allocation of acupoints and their results showed no differences between verum and control groups.
target: Acupuncture involves pricking the skin or tissues with thin needles at key points in the body to reduce pain and is widely used. However, studies show problems in the methods used to measure acupuncture.

source: Objective: The authors aimed to objectively assess the safety and efficacy of AC treatments for RA.
target: The aim of this current study is to evaluate the safety and the performance of acupuncture treatments on rheumatoid arthritis.

source: Methods: 105 RA patients with a functional diagnosis of a "Pivot syndrome" or "Turning Point syndrome" were randomly assigned to (1) verum-AC (verum acupoints), (2) control-AC (sham acupoints-points outside of the conduits/meridians and of the extra-conduits), or (3) waiting list (each group n = 35).
target: In this study, 105 patients with rheumatoid arthritis and a specific diagnosis were randomly assigned to either the acupuncture group, the sham acupuncture group (where the patient is pricked with thin needles but at different, less key points of the body), or a waiting list group. Each group had 35 patients.

source: AC groups experienced the exact same number, depth, and stimulation of needles.
target: The acupuncture groups (both normal and sham) experienced the exact same number, depth, and stimulation of needles.

source: Assessments took place before and 5 min after AC with follow-ups over 4 weeks.
target: A physical and visual exam of patients took place before and 5 minutes after acupuncture treatment with follow-up visits over 4 weeks.

source: Results: (1) Verum-AC significantly improved self-reported pain (Z = -5.099, p < 0.001) and pressure algometry (Z = -5.086, p < 0.001); hand grip strength (Z = -5.086, p < 0.001) and arm strength (Z = -5.086, p < 0.001); health status improved significantly (p < 0.001, Z = -4.895); QoL improved significantly in 7/8 survey domains; and number of swollen joints (Z = -2.862, p = 0.004) and tender joints (Z = -3.986, p < 0.001) significantly decreased.
target: Patients in the acupuncture group showed signficantly improved pain and pain sensitivity, hand grip and arm strength, as well as improved health status. Quality of life significantly improved in 7 out of 8 parts of a survey. The number of swollen joints and tender joints signficantly decreased.

source: (2) Control-AC showed no significant changes, except in self-reported pain improvement.
target: In the sham acupuncture group (the group that received needle pricks but outside the key points of the body) did not have any significant changes in their condition except in pain improvement.

source: (3) Waiting list group showed an overall worsening.
target: The waiting list group showed an overall worsening.

source: Conclusion: This is the first double-blind controlled study on AC in RA of the hand that objectively and specifically assesses positive effects supporting its integration in rheumatology.
target: This is the first double-blind controlled study (where neither the patients nor the researcher knows which treatment participants are getting) on acupuncture for rheumatoid arthritis of the hand. The study shows positive effects supporting acupuncture's inclusion in other treatments for rheumatology (diseases in the joints, muscles, tendons, ligaments).

source: Acupoint allocation according to Chinese Medicine functional diagnoses is extremely relevant to assess AC effectiveness in a patient group primarily defined by a "western" medicine diagnosis.
target: The site of the acupuncture needle is very important to determine acupuncture effectiveness.

source: Based on clear allocation criteria for acupoints, the authors minimized the possible bias of unspecific and suggestive effects on the control group, showed the specific effects of the points chosen, improved efficacy, and identified an evidence base for AC.
target: Because the authors in this study clearly defined the placement of the acupuncture needles on the body, they reduced possible bias, showed how the placement of the needles affects the body, improved performance of acupuncture, and identified evidence to support use of acupuncture.

source: Pain and a loss of feeling in your thumb, index finger, middle finger, and part of your ring finger may be a sign of carpal tunnel syndrome.
target: Pain and a loss of feeling in the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and part of the ring finger may be a sign of carpal tunnel syndrome (pinched nerves in the wrist).

source: This syndrome and the pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness in your hand that result from it are caused by pressure on the median nerve as it travels through the carpal tunnel.
target: This syndrome and the pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness in the hand is caused by pressure on the main nerve at the front of the forearm as it travels through the carpal tunnel, a narrow passageway at the base of the hand that is made of ligaments and bone.

source: Guidelines published in the May 2019 issue of JOSPT make recommendations, based on best practices from the published literature, for evaluating, diagnosing, and treating carpal tunnel syndrome.
target: Guidelines from the May 2019 issue of Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy, a scientific journal, make recommendations for evaluating, diagnosing, and treating carpal tunnel syndrome.

source: For you as a patient, these guidelines outline the best rehabilitation treatment options based on the scientific research.
target: For patients, these guidelines outline the best rehabilitation treatment options (care that can help people regain, keep, or improve abilities needed for daily life) based on the scientific research.

source: Ultimately, the best care is a combination of the leading science, the clinical expertise of your health care provider, and your input as the patient.
target: Ultimately, the best care is a combination of three things: 1) the leading science, 2) the knowledge, experience, and skills of a patient's health care provider, and 3) the patient's own input.

source: These guidelines help inform the first step in that process.
target: These guidelines help inform the first step in that process.

source: Practical Advice: Physical therapists are well trained to assess and evaluate people with carpal tunnel syndrome.
target: Physical therapists are people who help injured or ill people improve movement and manage pain. They are well trained to help people with carpal tunnel syndrome.

source: Although some patients (anywhere from 28% to 62%) recover without treatment, others (from 32% to 58%) get worse.
target: Although some patients (anywhere from 28% to 62%) recover without treatment, others (from 32% to 58%) get worse.

source: A key to nonsurgical treatment shown to help those with carpal tunnel syndrome is the use of a night brace; a night brace should hold your wrist in a neutral position and only be worn for short-term symptom relief.
target: A key to nonsurgical treatment shown to help those with carpal tunnel syndrome is the use of a night brace; a night brace should hold the wrist in a neutral position (where the joints are not being bent) and only be worn for short-term symptom relief.

source: If you have mild to moderate carpal tunnel syndrome, stretching exercises and the night brace can help, as can manual therapy of your cervical spine and upper extremity performed by a therapist.
target: For patients with mild to moderate carpal tunnel syndrome, stretching exercises and the night brace can help. Manual therapy (hands-on therapy without using a machine or device) of the neck and arms performed by a therapist can also help.

source: Education on the proper setup of your computer, especially the mouse, and how hard you strike the keyboard may also help control your symptoms of pain and loss of feeling.
target: Education on the proper setup of computers, especially the mouse, and how hard patients should strike the keyboard may also help control symptoms of pain and loss of feeling.

source: The literature review for these guidelines found that low-level laser therapy, thermal ultrasound, iontophoresis, and magnets provided no consistent benefit in treating carpal tunnel syndrome.
target: Research shows that low-level laser therapy (a laser light used at a low level applied to the skin to reduce pain and help the body heal), thermal ultrasound (a device that provides heat to tissues to increase circulation and reduce pain), iontophoresis (a procedure that uses an electrical current to deliver medicine through the skin), and magnets provided no consistent benefit in treating carpal tunnel syndrome.

source: If nonsurgical treatment does not help, you may need surgery.
target: If nonsurgical treatment does not help, surgery may be needed.

source: Your physical therapist can help guide your recovery, decreasing your symptoms.
target: A physical therapist can help guide a patient's recovery and decrease symptoms.

source: This compilation presents a comprehensive review of the literature on common chronic pain conditions of the hand.
target: This report presents a thorough review of the literature on common chronic pain conditions of the hand.

source: It briefly presents these common conditions with their biological background, diagnosis, and common management options.
target: It briefly presents common conditions with their biological background, diagnosis, and common options to treat and manage pain.

source: It then presents and compares the latest literature available for injection techniques to treat these diagnoses and compares the available evidence.
target: The report also shows and compares the latest literature available for injection methods to treat these diagnoses and compares the available evidence.

source: Results: Hand pain is a common condition with 9.7% prevalence in men and 21.6% in women and can cause significant morbidity and disability.
target: Hand pain is a common condition occuring in 9.7% of men and 21.6% of women and can cause significant illness and disability.

source: It also carries a significant cost to the individuals and the healthcare system, totaling in $4 billion dollars in 2003.
target: It is also costly to the individuals and the healthcare system, totaling in $4 billion dollars in 2003.

source: Injection therapy is an alternative when conservative treatment fails.
target: Injection therapy (a treatment that involves inserting a needle into the skin to deliver medicine and reduce pain) is an option when other non-surgical treatments fail.

source: Osteoarthritis is the most common chronic hand pain syndrome and affects about 16% of the population.
target: Osteoarthritis (the wearing down of flexible tissue at the end of bones) is the most common chronic hand pain condition and affects about 16% of the population.

source: Its mechanism is largely mechanic, and as such, there is controversy if steroid injections are of benefit.
target: There is disagreement on whether steroid injections are helpful to osteoarthritis.

source: Hyaluronic acid (HA) appears to provide substantial relief of pain and may increase functionality.
target: Injections of hyaluronic acid (a natural lubricant that can relieve pain) appear to provide significant relief of pain and may increase movement.

source: More studies of HA are required to make a definite judgment on its efficacy.
target: More studies of hyaluronic acid are required before deciding how well it works.

source: Similarly, steroid ganglion cyst injection may confer little benefit.
target: Similarly, steroid injection to reduce a ganglion cyst (a swelling or bump on the wrist joint) may have little benefit.

source: Carpal tunnel syndrome is a compressive neuropathy, and only temporarily relieved with injection therapy.
target: Injection therapy provides only short-term relief for carpal tunnel syndrome (pinched nerves in the wrist).

source: US-guidance provides significant improvement and, while severe cases may still require surgery, can provide a valuable bridge therapy to surgery when conservative treatment fails.
target: While severe cases of hand pain may still require surgery, injection therapy may be an additional treatment step before surgery.

source: Similar bridging treatments and increased efficacy under US-guidance are effective for stenosing tenosynovitis ("trigger finger"), though, interestingly, inflammatory background is associated with decreased effect in this case.
target: Similar treatments under US-guidance are effective for a condition called trigger finger where the finger gets locked in a bent position.

source: When the etiology of the pain is inflammatory, such as in RA, corticosteroid (CS) injections provide significant pain relief and increased functionality.
target: When the cause of the pain is inflammation (the body's response to injury or infection often causing swelling, pain, or redness), corticosteroid or steroid injections provide significant pain relief and increased movement.

source: They do not, however, change the course of disease (unlike DMARDs).
target: Corticosteroid injections do not, however, change the course of disease.

source: Another such example is De-Quervain tenosynovitis that sees good benefit from CS injections, and an increased efficacy with US-guidance, and similarly are CS injections for gout.
target: Other conditions such as gout (a type of arthritis that causes sudden pain or swelling, often in the big toe) and De-Quervain tenosynovitis (pain in the tendons in the thumb) may get some benefit from corticosteroid injections.

source: For Raynaud's phenomenon, Botox injections have encouraging results, but more studies are needed to determine safety and efficacy, as well as the possible difference in effect between primary and secondary Raynaud's.
target: Botox injections show encouraging results for Raynaud's phenomenon (spasms caused by slow blood flow in the fingers and other extremities), but more studies are needed to determine safety and how well the injections work.

source: Conclusions: Chronic hand pain is a prevalent and serious condition and can cause significant morbidity and disability and interferes with independence and activities of daily living.
target: Chronic hand pain is a common and serious condition, can cause significant illness and disability, and interferes with independence and the activities of daily living.

source: Conservative treatment remains the first line of treatment; however, when first-line treatments fail, steroid injections can usually provide benefit.
target: Careful, non-invasive treatment remains the first line of treatment; however, when first-line treatments fail, steroid injections can usually provide benefit.

source: In some cases, Hyaluronic acid or Botox may also be beneficial.
target: In some cases, Hyaluronic acid or Botox may also be helpful.

source: US-guidance is increasing in hand injection and almost ubiquitously provides safer, more effective injections.
target: US-guidance is expanding in hand injections and provides safer, more effective injections.

source: Hand surgery remains the alternative for refractory pain.
target: Hand surgery remains the alternative for pain that is not responding to multiple treatments.

source: Importance: Hand osteoarthritis is a musculoskeletal problem that is associated with hand pain, stiffness, functional limitation, decreased grip strength, and reduced quality of life.
target: Hand osteoarthritis is a problem that is associated with hand pain, stiffness, inability to perform normal functional movements, decreased grip strength, and reduced quality of life.

source: Objective: To evaluate the effectiveness of nighttime orthoses on the second or third finger of the dominant hand in controlling pain in women with symptomatic osteoarthritis (OA) in the interphalangeal joint.
target: A nighttime orthosis is a device or brace worn at night that supports weak or damaged muscles and limits motion of the wrist. The objective of this study is to evaluate how helpful using nighttime orthoses on the second or third finger of the dominant hand is in controlling pain in women who have osteoarthritis in the hinge joint of the fingers.

source: Design: Randomized controlled trial.
target: This study used a randomized controlled trial, a type of study that randomly assigns participants to one of two groups: the intervention group that is receiving the treatment or the comparison group not receiving treatment.

source: Setting: Outpatient clinic.
target: The study took place in an outpatient clinic away from a hospital.

source: Participants: Fifty-two women with symptomatic OA and presence of Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes, allocated randomly to the intervention group or the control group.
target: Fifty-two women with osteoarthritis and who also had bony bumps (also called nodes) on the finger joints closest to the fingernail and in the middle of the finger were randomly assigned to the intervention group or the comparison group.

source: Intervention: The intervention group used a nighttime orthosis on the second or third finger of the dominant hand.
target: The intervention group used a nighttime orthosis on the second or third finger of the dominant hand.

source: Both groups participated in an educational session.
target: Both groups participated in an educational session.

source: Outcomes and measures: The following parameters were measured: pain (numerical rating scale, Australian/Canadian Osteoarthritis Hand Index), grip and pinch strength, function (Cochin Hand Functional Scale), and manual performance (Moberg Pick Up Test).
target: The following were measured in both groups: pain (using a numbered scale), grip and pinch strength, function (measuring the ability to move and use the hand for daily activities), and manual performance (using a timed test that involves picking up, holding, and operating small objects).

source: Results: The intervention group showed a statistically significant improvement in pain (p < .001) and hand function.
target: The intervention group (group that used the orthoses) showed a significant improvement in pain and hand function.

source: The improvement in pain correlated with Cochin Hand Functional Scale scores and the absence of Bouchard's nodes in the third finger, which are predictors of the best prognosis for treatment with a nighttime orthosis.
target: The improvement in pain matched with measures of movement and the absence of nodes in the middle of the finger on the third finger.

source: Conclusions and relevance: This study demonstrates that nighttime orthoses are effective in reducing pain and lead to improvement in hand function in women with hand OA.
target: This study shows that nighttime orthoses are effective in reducing pain and lead to improvement in hand function in women with hand osteoarthritis.

source: They are therefore specifically recommended for nonpharmacological treatment of hand OA.
target: They are therefore specifically recommended for treatment of hand osteoarthritis that does not involve medications.

source: What this article adds: Orthoses can be considered, together with manual exercises and joint protection, as an intervention to reduce symptoms and improve hand function in people with hand OA.
target: Orthoses can be considered, together with manual exercises and joint protection, as a way to reduce symptoms and improve hand function in people with hand osteoarthritis.

source: This study is an important step in empowering occupational therapists to determine appropriate and effective intervention for clients with OA.
target: This study is an important step in helping occupational therapists (people who help patients with injuries, illnesses, and disabilities build or restore their abilities to perform the daily tasks of life) find appropriate and useful treatments for clients with osteoarthritis.

source: Importance: Hand osteoarthritis is a musculoskeletal problem that is associated with hand pain, stiffness, functional limitation, decreased grip strength, and reduced quality of life.
target: Hand osteoarthritis is a musculoskeletal problem involving hand pain, stiffness, practical limitation, decreased grip strength, and reduced quality of life.

source: Objective: To evaluate the effectiveness of nighttime orthoses on the second or third finger of the dominant hand in controlling pain in women with symptomatic osteoarthritis (OA) in the interphalangeal joint.
target: The objective is to measure the use of nighttime braces on the second or third finger of the dominant hand in controlling pain in women with typical osteoarthritis (OA) in the finger knuckles.

source: Participants: Fifty-two women with symptomatic OA and presence of Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes, allocated randomly to the intervention group or the control group.
target: Fifty-two women with typical OA and bony lumps in the finger knucles were randomly assigned to the treatment group or the control group.

source: Intervention: The intervention group used a nighttime orthosis on the second or third finger of the dominant hand.
target: The treatment group used a nighttime brace on the second or third finger of the dominant hand.

source: Both groups participated in an educational session.
target: Both groups took part in an informational session.

source: Outcomes and measures: The following parameters were measured: pain (numerical rating scale, Australian/Canadian Osteoarthritis Hand Index), grip and pinch strength, function (Cochin Hand Functional Scale), and manual performance (Moberg Pick Up Test).
target: Pain, grip and pinch strength, funciton, and manual performance were measured.

source: Results: The intervention group showed a statistically significant improvement in pain (p < .001) and hand function.
target: The treatment group showed a noticeable improvement in pain and hand function.

source: The improvement in pain correlated with Cochin Hand Functional Scale scores and the absence of Bouchard's nodes in the third finger, which are predictors of the best prognosis for treatment with a nighttime orthosis.
target: The improvement in pain paralleled functionality test scores and the loss of bony lumps in the third finger. They are predictors of the best recovery for treatment with a nighttime brace.

source: Conclusions and relevance: This study demonstrates that nighttime orthoses are effective in reducing pain and lead to improvement in hand function in women with hand OA.
target: Nighttime braces are useful in reducing pain and improving hand function in women with hand osteoarthritis.

source: They are therefore specifically recommended for nonpharmacological treatment of hand OA.
target: They are recommended for non-medication treatment of hand osteoarthritis.

source: What this article adds: Orthoses can be considered, together with manual exercises and joint protection, as an intervention to reduce symptoms and improve hand function in people with hand OA.
target: Braces can be used, along with hand exercises and joint protection, to reduce symptoms and improve hand function in people with hand osteoarthritis.

source: This study is an important step in empowering occupational therapists to determine appropriate and effective intervention for clients with OA.
target: This study can help occupational therapists, who help patients perform everyday activities, choose useful treatments for those with osteoarthritis.

source: Treatment of PDH deficiency rarely influences the course of the disease.
target: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency or shortage is a rare disorder caused by genetic changes, and its progression can rarely be stopped by treatment.

source: It is usually possible to reverse or minimize systemic lactic acid accumulation by giving a high fat/low carbohydrate "ketogenic" diet, but this does not alleviate the neurological symptoms as structural damage in the brain is present from before birth and many patients do not have significant metabolic problems.
target: It is usually possible to reverse or minimize some symptoms by giving a high fat/low carbohydrate "ketogenic" diet, but this does not help with delays in mental and motor development because damage in the brain is present before birth.

source: There is some evidence that dichloroacetate (which inhibits the specific PDH kinase and thereby activates any residual functioning complex) will also reduce the metabolic disturbance in some patients, but, again, this is rarely accompanied by any objective improvement in neurological performance.
target: A chemical in the body called dichloroacetate may also reduce the disturbance on metabolism in some patients, but, again, this is rarely accompanied by any improvement in mental and motor development.

source: A more favorable outcome can be expected in the extremely rare patients with a thiamine responsive form of the disease and, for this reason, a short therapeutic trial of thiamine is worth trying in all cases.
target: In rare cases, a vitamin called thiamine may respond to pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency, and so a short course of thiamine is worth trying for all cases.

source: We determined the ability of self-complementary adeno-associated virus (scAAV) vectors to deliver and express the pyruvate dehydrogenase E1alpha subunit gene (PDHA1) in primary cultures of skin fibroblasts from 3 patients with defined mutations in PHDA1 and 3 healthy subjects.
target: This study focuses on the ability of self-complementary adeno-associated virus (scAAV) vectors (a gene therapy tool that introduces genetic material using a modified virus to create a normal copy of a damaged gene) to deliver the pyruvate dehydrogenase gene called PDHA1 (a gene involved in the chemical process to create energy for the body) and create proteins. Cell samples of connective tissue from 3 patients with mutations in PDHA1 and 3 healthy patients were collected for the gene therapy process.

source: Cells were transduced with scAAV vectors containing the cytomegalovirus promoter-driven enhanced green fluorescent protein (EGFP) reporter gene at a vector:cell ratio of 200.
target: Cells are given genetic material with scAAV vectors containing a gene that creates proteins.

source: The transduction efficiency of scAAV2 and scAAV6 vectors was 3- to 5-fold higher than that of the other serotypes, which were subsequently used to transduce fibroblasts with wild-type PDHA1 cDNA under the control of the chicken beta-action (CBA) promoter at a vector:cell ratio of 1000.
target: Two types of vectors, the scAAV2 and scAAv6, were found to be the most effective way to deliver the genetic material into cells.

source: Both vectors increased E1alpha expression 40-60% in both control and patient cells, and increased PDH activity in two patient cell lines.
target: Both vectors increase the production of gene proteins in patients with the damaged genes and in patients with healthy cells.

source: We also used dichloroacetate (DCA) to maximally activate PDH through dephosphorylation of E1alpha.
target: Dichloroacetate, a drug used to treat genetic mitochondrial diseases (diseases where the miochondria cannot create enough energy for cells), is also used to activate pyruvate dehydrogenase, (an enzyme used in metabolism to create energy).

source: Exposure of transduced patient fibroblasts to DCA increased PDH activity up to 90% of the activity measured in untreated control cells.
target: The use of dichloroacetate did increase productivity of pyruvate dehydrogenase in cells with damaged genes involved in metabolism.

source: DCA also increased expression of E1alpha protein and, to variable extents, that of other components of the PDH complex in both non-transduced and transduced cells.
target: Dichloroacetate did increase production of proteins from certain energy-related genes and activity in other parts of pyruvate dehydrogenase (a protein involved in the chemical process that creates energy for the body).

source: These data suggest that a combined gene delivery and pharmacological approach may hold promise for the treatment of PDH deficiency.
target: This study shows that a combination of gene therapy and drugs may be a promising treatment for people with pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency or shortage.

source: Mitochondria are the energy-producing organelles of the cell, generating ATP via oxidative phosphorylation mainly by using pyruvate derived from glycolytic processing of glucose.
target: Mitochondria are the parts of cells that produce energy for the body.

source: Ketone bodies generated by fatty acid oxidation can serve as alternative metabolites for aerobic energy production.
target: Ketone bodies are particles made from breaking down fats and can serve as an alternative to generate energy.

source: The ketogenic diet, which is high in fat and low in carbohydrates, mimics the metabolic state of starvation, forcing the body to utilize fat as its primary source of energy.
target: The ketogenic diet, which is high in fat and low in carbohydrates, forces the body to use fat as its main source of energy.

source: The ketogenic diet is used therapeutically for pharmacoresistant epilepsy and for "rare diseases" of glucose metabolism (glucose transporter type 1 and pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency).
target: The ketogenic diet is used to help drug resistant epilepsy (seizures that cannot be conrolled with medicine) and for rare diseases that impact metabolism and the process for changing sugar into energy.

source: As metabolic reprogramming from oxidative phosphorylation toward increased glycolysis is a hallmark of cancer cells; there is increasing evidence that the ketogenic diet may also be beneficial as an adjuvant cancer therapy by potentiating the antitumor effect of chemotherapy and radiation treatment.
target: There is increasing evidence that the ketogenic diet may also be helpful as an additional therapy for cancer.

source: Objectives: Our aime was to study the short- and long-term effects of ketogenic diet on the disease course and disease-related outcomes in patients with pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency, the metabolic factors implicated in treatment outcomes, and potential safety and compliance issues.
target: A ketogenic diet is a low carb, high-fat diet that can help create fuel for the body when glucose levels are low. The aim of this study is to find the short- and long-term effects of a ketogenic diet on disease progression and disease-related outcomes (changes in health) in patients with pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency, a rare disease that impacts the nervous system. The effects of a ketogenic diet on the body's chemical process to turn food into energy and potential safety issues are also studied.

source: Methods: Pediatric patients diagnosed with pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency in Sweden and treated with ketogenic diet were evaluated.
target: Child patients diagnosed with pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency in Sweden and treated with a ketogenic diet are evaluated in this study.

source: Study assessments at specific time points included developmental and neurocognitive testing, patient log books, and investigator and parental questionnaires.
target: The study includes different tests on development and brain function, recording assessments in patient log books, and a series of questions for both researchers and parents to answer.

source: A systematic literature review was also performed.
target: A review of all relevant research on this topic is also performed.

source: Results: Nineteen patients were assessed, the majority having prenatal disease onset.
target: Nineteen patients were included in the study. For most of the patients, the disease developed during pregnancy.

source: Patients were treated with ketogenic diet for a median of 2.9 years.
target: Patients were treated with ketogenic diet for about 2.9 years, but this time varied.

source: All patients alive at the time of data registration at a median age of 6 years.
target: All patients alive at the time of data registration were about 6 years old, but the age did vary.

source: The treatment had a positive effect mainly in the areas of epilepsy, ataxia, sleep disturbance, speech/language development, social functioning, and frequency of hospitalizations.
target: The treatment has a positive effect mainly in the areas of epilepsy (a disorder that causes seizures), ataxia (loss of control of movement), sleep disturbance, speech/language development, social functioning (the ability to engage with others), and the number of hospitalizations.

source: It was also safe-except in one patient who discontinued because of acute pancreatitis.
target: It is also safe-except in one patient who had to stop because of acute pancreatitis, where the pancreas becomes swollen over a short period of time.

source: Poor dietary compliance was associated with relapsing ataxia and stagnation of motor and neurocognitive development.
target: Not staying on the diet regularly is connected with returning ataxia (loss of control of movement) and slowing or stopping of the strengthening of bones and muscles and brain development.

source: Conclusion: Ketogenic diet was an effective and safe treatment for the majority of patients.
target: Ketogenic diet is an effective and safe treatment for the majority of patients.

source: Treatment effect was mainly determined by disease phenotype and attainment and maintenance of ketosis.
target: How effective treatment is was mostly determined by physical traits or characteristics of patients and by if the body reaches and maintains ketosis, a process where the body burns stored fat for energy instead of glucose.

source: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency (PDCD) is a rare neurodegenerative disorder associated with abnormal mitochondrial metabolism.
target: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency (PDCD) or shortage is a rare disorder that can impact metabolism and lead to problems in the brain and nerves.

source: Structural brain abnormalities are common in PDCD.
target: Structural brain abnormalities are common in PDCD.

source: A case of a patient with PDCD with an unusual presentation is described.
target: A case of a patient with PDCD is described.

source: A 20-month-old boy with hypotonia and developmental delay, presented with hypoxia and respiratory distress due to bronchiolitis.
target: A 20-month-old boy with hypotonia (low muscle tone) and developmental delay, was seen by doctors and also had hypoxia (low levels of oxygen in tissues) and trouble breathing due to bronchiolitis (a lung infection).

source: During hospitalisation, he was prescribed PediaSure® feeds.
target: While in the hospital, he was prescribed PediaSure® feeds, a child formula or nutritional food product.

source: Two days after starting these feeds, he developed respiratory arrest requiring intubation.
target: Two days after starting these feeds, he stopped breathing and needed a tube inserted in his throat to help him breathe.

source: After stabilisation and a period of compulsory fasting, subsequent feeding with PediaSure® resulted in the recurrence of lactic acidosis.
target: After being stable and fasting (no food or drinks), feeding with PediaSure® resulted in the recurrence of lactic acidosis (lactic acid build up that can cause nausea, vomiting, and breathing problem).

source: Brain MRI was normal.
target: The MRI (scanned image) of the brain was normal.

source: Skeletal muscle biopsy confirmed PDCD.
target: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency (PDCD) was confirmed by viewing the muscle tissue under a microscope.

source: The most common cause of PDCD is a mutation in the X-linked PDHA1 gene.
target: The most common cause of PDCD is a mutation in one of the genes called PDHA1 (a gene that encodes a building block of PCD).

source: The severity of PDCD can range from neonatal death to more delayed onset of symptoms as in our index case.
target: The seriousness of PDCD can range from infant death to more delayed onset of symptoms as in the child case described here.

source: Normal brain MRI is reported in only 2% of patients with PDCD.
target: A normal brain MRI is reported in only 2% of patients with PDCD.

source: There is no effective treatment for PDCD.
target: There is no effective treatment for PDCD.

source: In patients with proximal muscle weakness and feeding intolerance with glucose-containing feeds, the presence of lactic acidosis should raise the suspicion of PDCD irrespective of the patient's age and normal MRI.
target: In patients with muscle weakness and who are unable to eat foods with glucose, the presence of lactic acidosis should raise the suspicion of PDCD regardless of the patient's age and normal MRI.

source: Diets low in carbohydrates and proteins and enriched in fat stimulate the hepatic synthesis of ketone bodies (KB).
target: Diets low in carbohydrates and proteins but high in fat stimulate the liver to make fat-related energy molecules called ketone bodies (KB).

source: These molecules are used as alternative fuel for energy production in target tissues.
target: Ketone bodies are molecules used as alternate fuel to produce energy for tissues in the body.

source: The synthesis and utilization of KB are tightly regulated both at transcriptional and hormonal levels.
target: Making and using ketone bodies is carefully controlled within the body at the molecule and hormone level.

source: The nuclear receptor peroxisome proliferator activated receptor α (PPARα), currently recognized as one of the master regulators of ketogenesis, integrates nutritional signals to the activation of transcriptional networks regulating fatty acid β-oxidation and ketogenesis.
target: One hormone, known as the master regulator of the process to develop ketone bodies, helps activate the cell networks that control fatty acids and ketogenesis, the process that creates ketone bodies.

source: New factors, such as circadian rhythms and paracrine signals, are emerging as important aspects of this metabolic regulation.
target: New factors, such as circadian rhythms (the natural cycle of mental and physical changes in the body over 24 hours) and paracrine signals (cell signaling to communicate with other cells nearby), are becoming important aspects of this metabolic regulation that controls and monitors cell energy stores.

source: However, KB are currently considered not only as energy substrates but also as signaling molecules.
target: However, ketone bodies are currently considered not only as an energy supply but also as signaling molecules that send messages to other cells or parts of the body.

source: Ketogenic diets (KD) are currently used to treat different forms of infantile epilepsy, also caused by genetic defects such as Glut1 and Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Deficiency Syndromes.
target: Ketogenic diets are currently used to treat different forms of infantile epilepsy (the onset of seizures in infancy), which is also caused by genetic defects.

source: However, several researchers are now focusing on the possibility to use KD in other diseases, such as cancer, neurological and metabolic disorders.
target: However, several researchers are now focusing on the possibility to use ketogenic diets in other diseases, such as cancer, brain-related and metabolic disorders.

source: Nonetheless, clear-cut evidence of the efficacy of KD in other disorders remains to be provided in order to suggest the adoption of such diets to metabolic-related pathologies.
target: Nonetheless, clear-cut evidence of the performance of ketogenic bodies in other disorders is needed in order to suggest using such diets.

source: Diets low in carbohydrates and proteins and enriched in fat stimulate the hepatic synthesis of ketone bodies (KB).
target: Diets low in carbs and proteins and high in fat quicken the creation of liver ketone bodies (KB) (particles made from fat breakdown).

source: These molecules are used as alternative fuel for energy production in target tissues.
target: KB are used for energy creation in certain body parts.

source: The synthesis and utilization of KB are tightly regulated both at transcriptional and hormonal levels.
target: The creation and use of KB from fat breakdown are monitored both at the cellular and multi-cellular level.

source: The nuclear receptor peroxisome proliferator activated receptor α (PPARα), currently recognized as one of the master regulators of ketogenesis, integrates nutritional signals to the activation of transcriptional networks regulating fatty acid β-oxidation and ketogenesis.
target: The nuclear receptor peroxisome proliferator activated receptor α (PPARα) is a major regulator of the creation of KB made from fat breakdown. It combines diet signals to the start up of cellular systems monitoring fat breakdown and KB creation.

source: New factors, such as circadian rhythms and paracrine signals, are emerging as important aspects of this metabolic regulation.
target: New factors, like sleep-wake patterns and cellular signals, may strongly influence this metabolic monitoring.

source: However, KB are currently considered not only as energy substrates but also as signaling molecules.
target: However, KB are not just energy particles but also signaling particles.

source: β-hydroxybutyrate has been identified as class I histone deacetylase inhibitor, thus establishing a connection between products of hepatic lipid metabolism and epigenetics.
target: β-hydroxybutyrate (a particle made from fat breakdown) blocks some cellular processes. This creates a connection between products of liver fat breakdown and changes in gene expression without changes to the DNA sequence.

source: Ketogenic diets (KD) are currently used to treat different forms of infantile epilepsy, also caused by genetic defects such as Glut1 and Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Deficiency Syndromes.
target: Ketogenic diets (KD) or low-carb diets are used to treat different forms of infatile epilepsy, a disorder in babies that disturbs brain and nerve activity and causes seizures. Infantile epilepsy is also caused by flaws in gene expression.

source: However, several researchers are now focusing on the possibility to use KD in other diseases, such as cancer, neurological and metabolic disorders.
target: However, researchers are now focusing on using KD in other diseases, such as cancer, nerve-related and metabolic disorders.

source: Nonetheless, clear-cut evidence of the efficacy of KD in other disorders remains to be provided in order to suggest the adoption of such diets to metabolic-related pathologies.
target: More evidence is needed to use KD diets to treat diseases.

source: Objectives: To report 2 additional cases of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency with reversible deep gray matter lesions following initiation of ketogenic diet and to perform a literature review of serial imaging in patients with pyruvate dehydrogenase complex.
