target: At more than 28 days after full recovery, 59 out of 174 are negative (with no virus detected), and 25 out of 69 are positive (with virus detected), but 13 of these are between 35 and 48 days.

source: Neutralizing antibody titers of 160 or greater were found in 63.6%.
target: Additional tests are done to learn more about the level of antibodies in the blood.

source: Serial plasma collection showed decline in neutralizing antibody titers and IgA levels (P < .05), probably denoting a "golden period" for CCP collection (≤28 days after joining the program); IgA might have an important role as neutralizing antibody.
target: Blood plasma collection showed a decline in antibodies that attach to the virus and keep it from entering cells.

source: Donor's weight, days between disease onset and serial plasma collection, and IgG and IgM levels are important predictors for neutralizing antibody titer.
target: The donor's weight, days between the start of the disease and repeated plasma collection, and antibody levels are important for predicting possible antibody concentration that can keep the virus from entering cells.

source: Conclusions: RT-PCR +ve cases are still detected in 36.2% within 28 to 48 days after recovery.
target: In conclusion, a positive test that finds genetic traces of the virus are still detected in 36.2% of cases within 28 to 48 days after recovery.

source: High anti-nucleocapsid protein IgG levels may be used as a surrogate marker to neutralizing antibody.
target: High levels of certain antibody proteins may be used to identify antibodies that can neutralize the virus and make it inactive.

source: Many studies have shown that re-positive tests for SARS-CoV-2 by RT-PCR in recovered COVID-19 patients are very common.
target: Many studies have shown that testing positive again for (or having) coronavirus in recovered Covid-19 patients (patients recovered from the serious viral, lung infection of Covid-19) is very common.

source: We aim to conduct this review to summarize the clinical and epidemiological characteristics of these patients and discuss the potential explanations for recurrences, the contagiousness of re-detectable positive SARS-CoV-2 virus, and the management of COVID-19 patients after discharge from hospital.
target: This study aims to summarize medical and other key characteristics of these patients. The summary will discuss explanations for the return of the virus in recovered patients, how contagious people are after they have recovered from the virus but test positive again, and the care of Covid-19 patients after going home from the hospital.

source: The proportion of re-positive tests in discharged COVID-19 patients varied from 2.4 to 69.2% and persisted from 1 to 38 days after discharge, depending on population size, age of patients, and type of specimens.
target: The percentage who re-tested as positive in discharged Covid-19 patients is between 2.4 to 69.2%. Retesting as positive continued from 1 to 38 days after discharge, or release, from the hospital, depending on population size, age, and the type of samples used to test.

source: Currently, several causes of re-positive tests for SARS-CoV-2 in recovered COVID-19 patients are suggested, including false-negative, false-positive RT-PCR tests; reactivation; and re-infection with SARS-CoV-2, but the mechanism leading to these re-positive cases is still unclear.
target: Currently, several causes of re-positive tests for coronavirus in recovered Covid-19 patients are suggested, including incorrect negative or positive test results; reactivation (when someone appears to have recovered but still has traces of the virus in the body); and being re-infected with coronavirus, but the process leading to these re-positive tests is still unclear.

source: The prevention of re-positive testing in discharged patients is a fundamental measure to control the spread of the pandemic.
target: Preventing patients who have recovered and are sent home from testing positive again is an important step to control the spread of the pandemic.

source: In order to reduce the percentage of false-negative tests prior to discharge, we recommend performing more than two tests, according to the standard sampling and microbiological assay protocol.
target: To limit the number of false-negatives tests (when a person tests negative but are actually positive for the virus) before being sent home, study researchers suggest doing more than 2 tests.

source: In addition, specimens should be collected from multiple body parts if possible, to identify SARS-CoV-2 viral RNA before discharge.
target: In addition, samples should be collected from multiple body parts if possible, to identify coronavirus DNA before discharge from the hospital.

source: Further studies should be conducted to develop novel assays that target a crucial region of the RNA genome in order to improve its sensitivity and specificity.
target: More studies should be done to develop new tests that target a crucial part of the genes from the virus in order to improve its accuracy.

source: Knowledge of the sensitivities of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) antibody tests beyond 35 days after the clinical onset of COVID-19 is insufficient.
target: There is not enough information on how well tests for coronavirus (which causes Covid-19 - a breathing-related illness) can correctly diagnose a positive case (which signals presence of the virus) beyond 35 days after the start of Covid-19.

source: We aimed to describe positivity rate of SARS-CoV-2 assays employing three different measurement principles over a prolonged period.
target: This study aims to describe the percentage of positive coronavirus tests out of all tests, also called positivity rate, using three different measurements over a long period of time.

source: Two hundred sixty-eight samples from 180 symptomatic patients with COVID-19 and a reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) test followed by serological investigation of SARS-CoV-2 antibodies were included.
target: Researchers took 268 samples from 180 Covid-19 patients with symptoms. These samples are tested using an RT-PCR test that finds traces of genetic material of the virus and a blood test to look for antibodies (proteins used by the immune system to fight infection).

source: We conducted three chemiluminescence (including electrochemiluminescence assay (ECLIA)), four enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and one lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) test formats.
target: Researchers used 3 electrochemiluminescence assay (ECLIA) test formats that can look for multiple types of antibodies in the blood, 4 enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) tests that test for antibodies for the coronavirus and can provide information about past exposure to Covid-19, and 1 lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) test format that can detect active coronavirus.

source: Positivity rates, as well as positive (PPVs) and negative predictive values (NPVs), were calculated for each week after the first clinical presentation for COVID-19.
target: After the first clinical sign for Covid-19 starts, the positivity rate and other measures to find the percentage of positive and negative (no virus) results in tests that are true positive, called positive predictive value, and true negative results, called negative predictive value, are calculated each week.

source: Furthermore, combinations of tests were assessed within an orthogonal testing approach employing two independent assays and predictive values were calculated.
target: Combinations of tests are measured to better understand how the primary tests are working.

source: Heat maps were constructed to graphically illustrate operational test characteristics.
target: Maps are created to show how the different tests operate.

source: During a follow-up period of more than 9 weeks, chemiluminescence assays and one ELISA IgG test showed stable positivity rates after the third week.
target: During a follow-up period of more than 9 weeks, a type of ECLIA and one ELISA antibody test showed stable positivity rates after the third week.

source: With the exception of ECLIA, the PPVs of the other chemiluminescence assays were ≥95% for COVID-19 only after the second week.
target: Except for ECLIA, the positive predictive value (percentage of positive tests that are true positives) of the other tests similar to ECLIA are ≥95% for Covid-19 only after the second week.

source: ELISA and LFIA had somewhat lower PPVs.
target: ELISA and LFIA have somewhat lower positive predictive values.

source: IgM exhibited insufficient predictive characteristics.
target: The IgM antibody that suggests a recent infection did not have enough information to do predictive calculations.

source: An orthogonal testing approach provided PPVs ≥ 95% for patients with a moderate pretest probability (e.g., symptomatic patients), even for tests with a low single test performance.
target: An orthogonal testing approach is when a person who tests positive are tested again with a second test. Orthogonal testing provided a positive predictive value of  ≥ 95% for patients who probably had the disease (e.g., symptomatic patients).

source: After the second week, NPVs of all but IgM assays were ≥95% for patients with low to moderate pretest probability.
target: After the second week, negative predictive values (percentage of negative test results that are true negatives) of all but the IgM antibody test are ≥95% for patients with a low or moderate chance of likely having the disease before being tested.

source: When interpreting results from SARS-CoV-2 tests, the pretest probability, time of blood draw, and assay characteristics must be carefully considered.
target: When reading results from coronavirus tests, the estimate of the chance of having the disease before confirming with a test, the time that blood is drawn, and test characteristics must be considered.

source: An orthogonal testing approach increases the accuracy of positive, but not negative, predictions.
target: An orthogonal testing approach when a person with a positive results receives a second test increases the accuracy of positive, but not negative, predictions.

source: Background: Reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR) assays are used to test for infection with the SARS-CoV-2 virus.
target: A test called reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR) is used to test for infection for the coronavirus (which causes COVID-19 - a viral, respiratory illness) by looking for traces of genes from the virus.

source: RT-PCR tests are highly specific and the probability of false positives is low, but false negatives are possible depending on swab type and time since symptom onset.
target: The chances of getting an incorrect positive (or virus-detected) result is low when using RT-PCR tests, but false negatives (when a person tests negative - not having the virus - but they are actually positive) are possible depending on swab type and time since symptoms started.

source: Aim: To determine how the probability of obtaining a false-negative test in infected patients is affected by time since symptom onset and swab type.
target: The aim of this study is to determine how the likelihood of getting a false-negative test in patients with coronavirus is impacted by the time since symptoms started and the type of swab, a small piece of soft material sometimes on the end of a small stick.

source: Methods: We used generalised additive mixed models to analyse publicly available data from patients who received multiple RT-PCR tests and were identified as SARS-CoV-2 positive at least once.
target: Publicly available data from patients who received several RT-PCR tests and identified as positive for coronavirus are analyzed.

source: Results: The probability of a positive test decreased with time since symptom onset, with oropharyngeal (OP) samples less likely to yield a positive result than nasopharyngeal (NP) samples.
target: The likelihood of a positive test decreased with the time passed since the start of symptoms. Samples from the middle part of the throat are less likely to give a positive result than swabs from the throat and back of the nose.

source: The probability of incorrectly identifying an uninfected individual due to a false-negative test was considerably reduced if negative tests were repeated 24 hours later.
target: The chance of incorrectly identifying an uninfected person because of a false-negative is reduced if negative tests are repeated 24 hours later.

source: For a small false-positive test probability (<0.5%), the true number of infected individuals was larger than the number of positive tests.
target: For a small chance of a false-positive test (<0.5%), the true number of infected individuals is larger than the number of positive tests.

source: For a higher false-positive test probability, the true number of infected individuals was smaller than the number of positive tests.
target: For a higher chance of a false-positive test, the true number of infected individuals is smaller than the number of positive tests.

source: Conclusion: NP samples are more sensitive than OP samples.
target: In conclusion, the samples from the back of the nose called nasopharyngeal are more sensitive than the samples taken in the middle of the throat.

source: The later an infected individual is tested after symptom onset, the less likely they are to test positive.
target: The later an infected individual is tested after symptom start, the less likely they are to test positive.

source: This has implications for identifying infected patients, contact tracing and discharging convalescing patients who are potentially still infectious.
target: This finding may influence decisions on identifying infected patients, contact tracing, or disease tracking, and discharging or releasing recovering patients who are possibly still infectious and can transmit the virus.

source: Background: Reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR) assays are used to test for infection with the SARS-CoV-2 virus.
target: Certain molecular tools called RT-PCR assays test for infection with the SARS-CoV-2 virus, which can cause a threatening breathing-related illness.

source: RT-PCR tests are highly specific and the probability of false positives is low, but false negatives are possible depending on swab type and time since symptom onset.
target: RT-PCR tests are very specific and detecting a target when there is none (or a false positive) is low. However, not detecting a target when there is one (or a false negative) is possible depending on the swab and time since symptoms.

source: Aim: To determine how the probability of obtaining a false-negative test in infected patients is affected by time since symptom onset and swab type.
target: The authors' aim is to determine the likelihood of a false-negative test in infected patients is affected by time since symptoms and swab type.

source: Results: The probability of a positive test decreased with time since symptom onset, with oropharyngeal (OP) samples less likely to yield a positive result than nasopharyngeal (NP) samples.
target: The chance of detecting the target decreased with time since symptoms. Also, oropharyngeal (OP) or mouth samples are less likely to detect the target than nasopharyngeal (NP) or nasal samples.

source: The probability of incorrectly identifying an uninfected individual due to a false-negative test was considerably reduced if negative tests were repeated 24 hours later.
target: The chance of incorrectly identifying an uninfected individual due to a false-negative test was reduced if tests showing no detection were repeated 24 hours later.

source: For a small false-positive test probability (<0.5%), the true number of infected individuals was larger than the number of positive tests.
target: For a small false-positive test chance (<0.5%), the true number of infected individuals was larger than the number of positive tests or tests detecting the target.

source: For a higher false-positive test probability, the true number of infected individuals was smaller than the number of positive tests.
target: For a higher false-positive test likelihood, the true number of infected individuals was smaller than the number of positive tests.

source: Conclusion: NP samples are more sensitive than OP samples.
target: NP samples are more sensitive than OP samples.

source: The later an infected individual is tested after symptom onset, the less likely they are to test positive.
target: The later an infected individual is tests after symptoms show, the less likely they are to test positive.

source: This has implications for identifying infected patients, contact tracing and discharging convalescing patients who are potentially still infectious.
target: Time since symptoms has implifications for identifying infected patients, tracing spread, and releasing recovering patients who may still be infectious.

source: Background: Coronavirus disease (COVID-19) has affected more than 100 countries worldwide and the discharge criteria of patients with COVID-19 vary across different countries.
target: Covid-19 (a viral, breathing-related disease) has affected more than 100 countries around the world, and the basis used to decide when patients with Covid-19 can be sent home is different across countries.

source: In China, patients with two negative respiratory viral RNA tests taken at least one day apart can be discharged with no further quarantine required.
target: In China, patients with two negative (or undetected) viral tests for Covid-19 taken at least one day apart can be sent home with no further quarantine (isolation) required.

source: Currently, PCR testing of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) in fecal sample is not routinely performed.
target: Currently, testing of for coronavirus using a fecal or poop sample is not usually done.

source: Methods: We present a patient with COVID-19, whose respiratory swabs became negative but fecal sample remained positive for SARS-CoV-2 RNA.
target: This paper describes a patient with Covid-19 whose tests from swabs of the back of the nose and throat were negative but fecal sample were positive for (or had) coronavirus, which causes Covid-19.

source: Results: Stool sample collected on 27th of February was still positive for SARS-CoV-2 RNA, 24 days after the first negative respiratory swab.
target: The stool (feces) sample collected on 27th of February was still positive for genetic material of the coronavirus, 24 days after the first negative swab from the nose and throat.

source: Conclusions: Based on the experience from the 2003 SARS epidemic, we recommend that fecal RNA testing of SARS-CoV-2 should be incorporated into the discharge criteria to minimize the risk of transmission from the gastrointestinal tract.
target: In conclusion, based on the experience from previous epidemics of lung infections, researchers recommend that testing fecal samples for coronavirus should be included into the standard for sending people home to decrease the risk of transmission (or spread) from the digestive system.

source: Background: Coronavirus disease (COVID-19) has affected more than 100 countries worldwide and the discharge criteria of patients with COVID-19 vary across different countries.
target: Coronavirus disease (COVID-19), which is a viral breathing-related disease, has affected over 100 countries worldwide. The hospital release criteria of patients with COVID-19 varies across countries.

source: In China, patients with two negative respiratory viral RNA tests taken at least one day apart can be discharged with no further quarantine required.
target: In China, patients with two tests detecting no virus and taken at least one day apart can be released with no further isolation needed.

source: Currently, PCR testing of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) in fecal sample is not routinely performed.
target: Currently, testing of severe acute respiratory syndrome cornoavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), which causes COVID-19, in poop samples in not regularly performed.

source: Methods: We present a patient with COVID-19, whose respiratory swabs became negative but fecal sample remained positive for SARS-CoV-2 RNA.
target: We show a patient with COVID-19, whose poop, but not nose swabs, shows traces of the virus.

source: Results: Stool sample collected on 27th of February was still positive for SARS-CoV-2 RNA, 24 days after the first negative respiratory swab.
target: Poop sample collected on 27th of February shows signs of the virus, 24 days after the first nose swab that did not detect the virus.

source: Conclusions: Based on the experience from the 2003 SARS epidemic, we recommend that fecal RNA testing of SARS-CoV-2 should be incorporated into the discharge criteria to minimize the risk of transmission from the gastrointestinal tract.
target: Based on the 2003 SARS virus epidemic, we recommend that poop testing for SARS-CoV-2 should be added to the hospital release criteria to minimize risk of spread from the digestive tract.

source: Diagnosis of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-COV-2) cases is based on the count of real-time reverse transcription-plymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) positive people.
target: Diagnosis of cases of coronavirus (a virus causing the lung infection known as Covid-19) is based on the count of positive RT-PCR tests, a type of test that looks for traces of DNA from the virus and signals positive if there is virus present.

source: Viral load by real-time RT-PCR has been suggested as a biomarker of the SARS-CoV-2 infection.
target: The amount of virus in the body, called the viral load, measured by the RT-PCR test is seen as an indication of coronavirus infection.

source: However, the association of viral load and severity of the disease is not yet resolved.
target: However, the association of the amount of virus and the severity of the disease is not yet understood.

source: Nasopharyngeal samples from 458 patients were tested by RT-PCR for SARS-CoV-2 diagnosis.
target: Samples from the back of the nose from 458 patients are tested by RT-PCR for coronavirus.

source: Relative quantitation was made by the comparative threshold cycle (ΔΔCt ) formula between ORF1ab viral and RNase P housekeeping genes.
target: Additional tests are done to measure the viral load.

source: Most prevalent clinical signs were cough (75.8%), myalgia (66.7%), and fever (48.5%).
target: The most common signs are cough, muscle aches and pains, and fever.

source: Hypertension (18.2%), neurological diseases (15.1%), and asthma and hypothyroidism (12.1%) were most frequent comorbidities.
target: High blood pressure, diseases affecting the brain and nerves, asthma, and hypothyroidism (not enough thyroid hormone in the body) were the most frequent conditions of other illnesses.

source: Fever, either as an exclusive symptom or combined with others, was associated with high viral loads ( 2−ΔΔCt range, 35.65-155.16; 4.25-4.89 log10 RNA copies/test]).
target: Fever, either as the only symptom or combined with others, is associated with high viral loads.

source: During the first week after onset of symptoms in mild patients up to 60 years-old was detected the peak of viral load.
target: During the first week after symptoms start in mild patients up to 60 years-old is the peak of viral load.

source: Children under 10 years old have a high viral load (313.84; 2.50) in the first 2 days postinfection with a sharp decline thereafter.
target: Children under 10 years old have a high viral load in the first 2 days post-infection followed by a sharp decline.

source: Cases between 10 and 49 years old mostly showed low and moderate viral load during the first 2 days postinfection (range, 0.03 to 17.24; -1.50 to 1.24).
target: Cases between 10 and 49 years old mostly showed low and moderate viral load during the first 2 days after infection.

source: Patients over 60 years old have high viral load up to the second week after the onset of symptoms (range, 25.32-155.42; 1.40-2.19), indicating the longer presence of the virus in them.
target: Patients over 60 years old have high viral load up to the second week after the start of symptoms, indicating the virus stays present in them longer.

source: These findings suggest the viral load in nasopharyngeal swabs would help to monitor the SARS-CoV-2 infection in mild coronavirus disease 2019 cases.
target: These findings suggest the viral load in swabs from the nose would help to monitor the coronavirus infection in mild coronavirus disease 2019 cases.

target: Detecting cases of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-COV-2), which causes a breathing-related viral disease, is based on the count of people which a certain molecular test (real-time RT-PCR) detects.

target: Nasal samples from 458 patients were tested by RT-PCR for SARS-CoV-2.

target: Most common symptoms were cough (75.8%), muscle pain (66.7%), and fever (48.5%).

target: High blood pressure (18.2%), brain-related diseases (15.1%), and asthma and reduced thyroid function, which affects metabolism, (12.1%) were the most frequent associated conditions.

source: Fever, either as an exclusive symptom or combined with others, was associated with high viral loads ( 2−ΔΔCt range, 35.65-155.16; 4.25-4.89 log10 RNA copies/test]).

source: During the first week after onset of symptoms in mild patients up to 60 years-old was detected the peak of viral load.

source: Children under 10 years old have a high viral load (313.84; 2.50) in the first 2 days postinfection with a sharp decline thereafter.
target: Children under 10 years have a high virus amount in the first 2 days after infection with a sharp decline after.

source: Cases between 10 and 49 years old mostly showed low and moderate viral load during the first 2 days postinfection (range, 0.03 to 17.24; -1.50 to 1.24).

source: Patients over 60 years old have high viral load up to the second week after the onset of symptoms (range, 25.32-155.42; 1.40-2.19), indicating the longer presence of the virus in them.

target: The virus amount in nasal swabs may help monitor the SARS-CoV-2 infection in cases of mild coronavirus disease 2019, a breathing-related viral disease.

source: This study aimed to monitor severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) viral loads and specific serum-antibodies (immunoglobulin [Ig] G and M) among confirmed patients and asymptomatic carriers from returning healthy travelers.
target: This study aims to monitor the amount of coronavirus (which causes COVID-19 - the breathing-related illness) in the blood, also called viral load, and the specific, infection-fighting proteins used by the immune system, called antibodies, among patients with coronavirus and healthy travelers who have the virus but no symptoms.

source: The throat swabs, sputum, and stool samples from 57 hospitalized coronavirus disease (COVID-19) patients and 8 asymptomatic carriers, among 170 returning healthy travelers were tested using reverse-transcription real-time polymerase chain reaction.
target: Samples from 57 hospitalized Covid-19 patients and 8 asymptomatic carriers (people who have the virus but no symptoms) are tested.

source: Sequential results showed higher viral RNA loads in the throat, sputum, and stool samples at 3-12 and 6-21 days after symptom onset among severely ill COVID-19 patients.
target: Among seriously ill Covid-19 patients, there is a higher viral load in the throat, sputum (lung), and stool samples at 3-12 and 6-21 days after symptoms start.

source: Shorter viral habitation time (1-8 days) was observed in the oropharyngeal site and intestinal tract of asymptomatic carriers.
target: Among carriers with no symptoms, the viral load is found to stay for a shorter time period (1-8 days) in the throat and in the digestive system.

source: The IgG and IgM response rates were 19/37 (51.4%) and 23/37 (62.6%) among the 29 confirmed patients and 8 asymptomatic carriers, respectively, within 66 days from symptom or detection onset.
target: Antibodies to fight the viruses responded in 51.4% of confirmed patients and 62.6% among asymptomatic people within 66 days from symptom starting.

source: The median duration between symptom onset and positive IgG and IgM results was 30 (n=23; interquartile range [IQR]=20-66) and 23 (n=19; IQR=12-28) days, respectively.
target: The average time between the start of symptoms and positive (or detected) antibodies is 30 days for the IgG antibody, and 23 days for the IgM antibodies, which often suggest a recent infection.

source: Of 170 returning healthy-travelers to China, 4.7% were asymptomatic carriers (8/170) within 2 weeks, and the IgG and IgM positivity rate was 12.8% (12/94).
target: Of 170 returning healthy-travelers to China, 4.7% were asymptomatic carriers within 2 weeks, and the positive result among them for the IgG and IgM antibodies is 12.8%.

source: IgM/IgG-positivity confirmed 3 suspected SARS-CoV-2 cases, despite negative results for SARS-CoV-2 RNA.
target: The IgM/IgG antibodies confirmed 3 suspected coronavirus cases, even though they tested negative for coronavirus using another test.

source: Compared with other respiratory viral infectious diseases, COVID-19 has fewer asymptomatic carriers, lower antibody response rates, and a longer antibody production duration in recovered patients and the contacted healthy population.
target: When compared to other viruses that impact the lungs, Covid-19 has fewer asymptotic carriers and has a lower antibody response. Covid-19 also has a longer period of time developing antibodies in recovered patients.

source: This is an indication of the complexity of COVID-19 transmission.
target: These findings highlight how complex transmission or spread is in Covid-19.

source: This study aimed to monitor severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) viral loads and specific serum-antibodies (immunoglobulin [Ig] G and M) among confirmed patients and asymptomatic carriers from returning healthy travelers.
target: This study monitors virus amonts and naturally-created blood antibodies, which combat foreign viruses, of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), which causes a breathing-related viral disorder, among infected patients and carries without symptoms from returning healthy travelers.

source: The throat swabs, sputum, and stool samples from 57 hospitalized coronavirus disease (COVID-19) patients and 8 asymptomatic carriers, among 170 returning healthy travelers were tested using reverse-transcription real-time polymerase chain reaction.
target: The throat swabs, mucus, and poop samples from 57 hospitalized patients with coronavirus disease (COVID-19), caused by SARS-CoV-2, and 8 virus carries without symptoms, among 170 returning healthy travelers, were tested with a molecular tool.

target: Results show higher virus amount in throat, mucus, and stool samples at 3-12 and 6-21 days after symptoms first showed among severely ill COVID-19 patients.

source: Shorter viral habitation time (1-8 days) was observed in the oropharyngeal site and intestinal tract of asymptomatic carriers.
target: Shorter virus lifetime (1-8 days) was seen in the throat near the mount and digestive tract of carriers without symptoms.

source: The IgG and IgM response rates were 19/37 (51.4%) and 23/37 (62.6%) among the 29 confirmed patients and 8 asymptomatic carriers, respectively, within 66 days from symptom or detection onset.
target: Specific blood antibody detection rates were 18/37 (51.4%) and 23/37 (62.6%) among the 29 confirmed patients and 8 carriers without symptoms, respectively, within 66 days from start of symptoms or detection.

source: The median duration between symptom onset and positive IgG and IgM results was 30 (n=23; interquartile range [IQR]=20-66) and 23 (n=19; IQR=12-28) days, respectively.
target: Average duration between start of symptoms and antibody results was 30 and 23 days, respectively.

source: Of 170 returning healthy-travelers to China, 4.7% were asymptomatic carriers (8/170) within 2 weeks, and the IgG and IgM positivity rate was 12.8% (12/94).
target: Of the 170 returning healthy-travelers to China, 4.7% were infected showing no symptoms (8/170) within 2 weeks. Antibdoy detection rate was 12.8% (12/94).

source: IgM/IgG-positivity confirmed 3 suspected SARS-CoV-2 cases, despite negative results for SARS-CoV-2 RNA.
target: Antibody detection confirmed 3 suspected SARS-CoV-2 cases, despite other test results not detecting the virus.

source: Compared with other respiratory viral infectious diseases, COVID-19 has fewer asymptomatic carriers, lower antibody response rates, and a longer antibody production duration in recovered patients and the contacted healthy population.
target: Relative to other breathing-related viral infectious diseases, COVID-19 has fewer carriers without symptoms, lower antibody response rates, and a longer antibody production duration in recovered and contacted healthy people.

source: This is an indication of the complexity of COVID-19 transmission.
target: These results show the complexity of COVID-19 spread.

source: The number of serological assays for SARS-CoV-2 has skyrocketed in the past year.
target: The number of blood tests that check for antibodies (infection-fighting proteins) for the coronavirus (which causes COVID-19, the lung infection) has skyrocketed in the past year.

source: Concerns have been raised regarding their performance characteristics, depending on the disease severity and the time of the analysis post-symptom onset (PSO).
target: Concerns have been raised on the performance of the tests, depending on the disease severity and the time of the test after symptoms start.

source: Thus, independent validations using an unbiased sample selection are required for meaningful serology data interpretation.
target: Therefore, confirming the performance of the test using an unbiased sample is required in order to understand and describe the antibody data.

source: We aimed to assess the clinical performance of six commercially available assays, the seroconversion, and the dynamics of the humoral response to SARS-CoV-2 infection.
target: Researchers aim to assess the performance of six available blood tests, the seroconversion (when the body starts producing antibodies), and how the body creates antibodies in response to coronavirus infection.

source: The study included 528 serum samples from 156 patients with follow-up visits up to six months PSO and 161 serum samples from healthy people.
target: The study included 528 blood samples from 156 patients with follow-up visits up to 6 months after the start of symptoms and 161 blood samples from healthy people.

source: The IgG/total antibodies positive percentage increased and remained above 95% after six months when chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA) IgG antiS1/S2 and electro-chemiluminescent assay (ECLIA) total antiNP were used.
target: The total amount of antibodies increased and remained above 95% after six months when using certain tests to identify antibodies.

source: At early time points PSO, chemiluminescent microparticle immunoassay (CMIA) IgM antiS achieved the best sensitivity.
target: At early times after the start of symptoms, a blood test that looks for certain antibodies that suggest recent infection has the best ability to correctly diagnose a positive (or virus-containing) case.

source: IgM and IgG appear simultaneously in most circumstances, and when performed in parallel the sensitivity increases.
target: Two types of antibodies, IgM and IgG, appear at the same time in most circumstances, and when performed together, the ability to correctly diagnose a positive diagnosis increases.

source: The severe and the moderate clinical forms were significantly associated with higher seropositivity percentage and antibody levels.
target: The severe and the moderate clinical forms have major associations with a higher number of positive tests and antibody levels.

source: High specificity was found in all evaluated assays, but the sensitivity was variable depending on the time PSO, severity of disease, detection method and targeted antigen.
target: The ability to correctly have a negative (or no virus) test among negative people is found in all evaluated blood tests, but the ability to correctly diagnose a true positive varies depending on the time after the start of symptoms and the type of test.

source: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a disorder characterized by involuntary movements, typically of the orofacial muscles and also of the extremities and other muscle groups.
target: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is characterized by uncontrolled movements, usually of the face muscles and also of the arms, legs, and other muscles.

source: The condition is associated with exposure to dopamine receptor blocking agents, including antipsychotics.
target: TD is related to antipsychotic use.

source: Because the indications and off-label uses for these agents have expanded over the last 2 decades, a larger number of patients are receiving antipsychotic medications than in the past.
target: Because reasons to prescribe and unapproved uses for antipsychotics have increased, more patients are taking antipsychotic drugs than in the past.

source: While evidence suggests that patients being treated with second-generation antipsychotics have less risk for developing TD than those treated with first-generation antipsychotics, the decreased risk is not as great as was originally expected.
target: Although data suggest that patients taking second-generation (newer) antipsychotics are less likely to develop TD than those taking first-generation (older) antipsychotics, the decreased risk is not as great as originally thought.

source: In addition, patients with chronic psychiatric conditions often require long-term use of antipsychotics, putting them at risk for TD.
target: In addition, patients with long-term mental conditions often need long-term antipsychotics use, putting them at risk for TD.

source: This article addresses the prevalence, risk factors, and prevention of TD; assessment strategies including diagnostic criteria and rating scales; and evidence for TD treatments, including 2 newly approved medications: deutetrabenazine and valbenazine.
target: This article looks at the total number of TD cases at a given time, risk factors, and prevention of TD; ways to measure TD including signs and symptoms and questionnaires; and TD treatments, including 2 newly approved drugs: deutetrabenazine and valbenazine.

source: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a disorder characterized by involuntary movements, typically of the orofacial muscles and also of the extremities and other muscle groups.
target: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a disorder of involuntary movements, typlically of the lower face muscles, limbs, and other muscle groups.

source: The condition is associated with exposure to dopamine receptor blocking agents, including antipsychotics.
target: The condition is linked to exposure with blocking agents, like antipsychotics used to treat mental illnesses, of the chemical messenger dopamine.

source: Because the indications and off-label uses for these agents have expanded over the last 2 decades, a larger number of patients are receiving antipsychotic medications than in the past.
target: Since the allowed and off-label uses for these agents has increased over the last 20 years, more patients are receiving antipsychotics than before.

source: While evidence suggests that patients being treated with second-generation antipsychotics have less risk for developing TD than those treated with first-generation antipsychotics, the decreased risk is not as great as was originally expected.
target: While patients treated with one class of antipsychotics may have less risk for developing TD than those treated with another class, the decreased risk is not as big as expected.

source: In addition, patients with chronic psychiatric conditions often require long-term use of antipsychotics, putting them at risk for TD.
target: Also, patients with long-lasting mental illnesses often need long-term use of antipsychotics, pytting them at risk for TD.

source: This article addresses the prevalence, risk factors, and prevention of TD; assessment strategies including diagnostic criteria and rating scales; and evidence for TD treatments, including 2 newly approved medications: deutetrabenazine and valbenazine.
target: This article describes the widespreadness of TD, its risk factors, its prevention, its identification strategies like medical criteria and ratings, and evidence for its treatments, including deutetrabenazine and valbenzaine used for treating movement disorders.

source: Aim: The aim of this study was to summarize the characteristics, efficacy, and safety of vesicular monoamine transporter-2 (VMAT-2) inhibitors for treating tardive dyskinesia (TD).
target: We aimed to summarize the characteristics, how well it works, and safety of vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT-2) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), for treating tardive dyskinesia (TD) - a movement disorder.

source: Materials and methods: We conducted a literature search in PubMed, Cochrane Database, and ClinicalTrials.gov, screening for systematic reviews, meta-analyses or double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trials (DBRPCTs) reporting efficacy or safety data of VMAT-2 inhibitors (tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine, and valbenazine) in patients with TD.
target: We searched for published scientific studies, including studies that summarized other studies, used statistics to combine results from other studies, or randomly assigned participants to groups receiving TD treatment or sugar pills that looked at how well treatments worked and safety of VMAT-2 inhibitors (tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine, and valbenazine) in patients with TD.

source: A random effects meta-analysis of efficacy and safety data from DBRPCTs was performed.
target: We used statistical methods to determine how well treatments work and safety results across multiple studies.

source: Results: Two acute, 12-week DBRPCTs with deutetrabenazine 12-48 mg/day (n=413) and 4 acute, 4-6-week double-blind trials with valbenazine 12.5-100 mg/day (n=488) were meta-analyzable, without meta-analyzable, high-quality data for tetrabenazine.
target: We used statistical methods to combine results from two 12-week studies with deutetrabenazine 12-48 mg/day (413 patients) and four 4-6 week studies with valbenazine 12.5-100 mg/day (488 patients). No high-quality data were available for tetrabenazine for a similar analysis.

source: Regarding reduction in total Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale (AIMS) scores (primary outcome), both deutetrabenazine (k=2, n=413, standardized mean difference [SMD] =-0.40, 95% confidence interval [CI] =-0.19, -0.62, p<0.001; weighted mean difference (WMD) =-1.44, 95% CI =-0.67, -2.19, p<0.001) and valbenazine (k=4, n=421, SMD =-0.58, 95% CI =-0.26, -0.91, p<0.001; WMD =-2.07, 95% CI =-1.08, -3.05, p<0.001) significantly outperformed placebo.
target: Results favored deutetrabenazine and valbenazine over sugar pills in scores on a rating scale that measures involuntary movements (AIMS).

source: Results were confirmed regarding responder rates (≥50% AIMS total score reduction; deutetrabenazine: risk ratio [RR] =2.13, 95% CI =1.10, 4.12, p=0.024, number-needed-to-treat [NNT] =7, 95% CI =3, 333, p=0.046; valbenazine: RR =3.05, 95% CI =1.81, 5.11, p<0.001, NNT =4, 95% CI =3, 6, p<0.001).
target: The percentage of patients who had 50% reduction in scores on the same rating scale (AIMS) favored deutetrabenazine and valbenazine over sugar pills.

source: Less consistent results emerged from patient-rated global impression-based response (p=0.15) and clinical global impression for deutetrabenazine (p=0.088), and for clinical global impression change for valbenazine (p=0.67).
target: Results were less consistent using another rating scale done by patients and doctors for deutetrabenazine and by doctors for valbenazine.

source: In an open-label extension (OLE) study of deutetrabenazine (≤54 weeks) and a dose-blinded valbenazine study (≤48 weeks), responder rates increased over time.
target: In one study of deutetrabenazine and one study of valbenazine, the percentage of patients who had a 50% reduction in scores on the AIMS rating scale went up over time.

source: With valbenazine, discontinuation effects were studied, showing TD symptom recurrence towards baseline severity levels within 4 weeks after valbenazine withdrawal.
target: Effects of stopping valbenazine were studied, showing TD symptoms returning to levels before stopping valbenazine within 4 weeks of stopping the drug.

source: No increased cumulative or specific adverse (AEs) events versus placebo (acute trials) in extension versus acute trial data were observed.
target: No increased unfavorable and unintended effects were observed in studies where participants continued to take the studied drug compared to sugar pills.

source: Conclusion: The 2 VMAT-2 inhibitors, valbenazine and deutetrabenazine, are effective in treating TD, both acutely and long-term, without concerns about increased risk of depression or suicide in the TD population.
target: We conclude that the 2 VMAT-2 inhibitors, valbenazine and deutetrabenazine, work to treat TD, both in the short- and long-term, without causing increased risk of depression or suicide in people with TD.

source: No head-to-head comparison among VMAT-2 inhibitors and no high-quality, meta-analyzable data are available for tetrabenazine in patients with TD.
target: No studies comparing VMAT-2 inhibitors or high-quality tetrabenazine data that could be compared across studies were available.

source: Aim: The aim of this study was to summarize the characteristics, efficacy, and safety of vesicular monoamine transporter-2 (VMAT-2) inhibitors for treating tardive dyskinesia (TD).
target: This study's aim was to summarize the characteristics, effect, and safety of vesicular monoamine transporter-2 (VMAT-2) inhibitors, drugs that treat movement disorders, for treating tardive dyskinesia (TD), a movement disorder of irregular, jerking movements.

source: Materials and methods: We conducted a literature search in PubMed, Cochrane Database, and ClinicalTrials.gov, screening for systematic reviews, meta-analyses or double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trials (DBRPCTs) reporting efficacy or safety data of VMAT-2 inhibitors (tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine, and valbenazine) in patients with TD.
target: We searched online for studies reporting the effect and safety of VMAT-2 inhibitors like tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine, and valbenazine in patients with TD.

source: Results: Two acute, 12-week DBRPCTs with deutetrabenazine 12-48 mg/day (n=413) and 4 acute, 4-6-week double-blind trials with valbenazine 12.5-100 mg/day (n=488) were meta-analyzable, without meta-analyzable, high-quality data for tetrabenazine.
target: Two short, 12-week studies with deutetrabenazine12-48 mg/day (with 413 patients) and 4 short, 4-6-week studies with valbenazine 12.5-100 mg/day (with 488 patients) were analyzed. There was no high-quality data for tetrabenazine.

source: Regarding reduction in total Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale (AIMS) scores (primary outcome), both deutetrabenazine (k=2, n=413, standardized mean difference [SMD] =-0.40, 95% confidence interval [CI] =-0.19, -0.62, p<0.001; weighted mean difference (WMD) =-1.44, 95% CI =-0.67, -2.19, p<0.001) and valbenazine (k=4, n=421, SMD =-0.58, 95% CI =-0.26, -0.91, p<0.001; WMD =-2.07, 95% CI =-1.08, -3.05, p<0.001) significantly outperformed placebo.
target: Regarding reducing irregular, involuntary movements (the primary symptom), both deutetrabenazine and valbenazine outperformed placebo or a dummy treatment.

source: Results were confirmed regarding responder rates (≥50% AIMS total score reduction; deutetrabenazine: risk ratio [RR] =2.13, 95% CI =1.10, 4.12, p=0.024, number-needed-to-treat [NNT] =7, 95% CI =3, 333, p=0.046; valbenazine: RR =3.05, 95% CI =1.81, 5.11, p<0.001, NNT =4, 95% CI =3, 6, p<0.001).
target: Results were confirmed regarding responder rates, or the proportion of patients with a reduction in symptoms in a define period of time.

source: Less consistent results emerged from patient-rated global impression-based response (p=0.15) and clinical global impression for deutetrabenazine (p=0.088), and for clinical global impression change for valbenazine (p=0.67).
target: Patient-rated global impressions for deutetrabenazine and clinical global impressions for deutetrabenazine and valbenazine were less strong.

source: In an open-label extension (OLE) study of deutetrabenazine (≤54 weeks) and a dose-blinded valbenazine study (≤48 weeks), responder rates increased over time.
target: In a long-term study of deutetrabenazine (≤54 weeks) and a valbenazine study (≤48 weeks), responder rates increased.

source: With valbenazine, discontinuation effects were studied, showing TD symptom recurrence towards baseline severity levels within 4 weeks after valbenazine withdrawal.
target: With valbenazine, drug use stoppage effects were studied, showing TD symptom reappearance of initial severity 4 weeks after valbenazine stoppage.

source: No increased cumulative or specific adverse (AEs) events versus placebo (acute trials) in extension versus acute trial data were observed.
target: No increased or specific, serious or adverse events (AEs) versus dummy treatment (acute trials) in long-term versus short-term trials were seen.

source: Conclusion: The 2 VMAT-2 inhibitors, valbenazine and deutetrabenazine, are effective in treating TD, both acutely and long-term, without concerns about increased risk of depression or suicide in the TD population.
target: The 2 VMAT-2 inhibitors, valbenazine and deutetrabenazine, can treat TD, both short- and long-term, without concerns about increased risk of depression or suicide in the TD population.

source: No head-to-head comparison among VMAT-2 inhibitors and no high-quality, meta-analyzable data are available for tetrabenazine in patients with TD.
target: No direct comparison between the VMAT-2 inhibitors and no high-quality data are available for tetrabenazine in patients with TD.

source: Tardive dyskinesias (TDs) are still common long-term sequelae of antipsychotic treatment.
target: Tardive dyskinesias (TDs) - movement disorders - are still common long-term consequences of antipsychotic drugs.

source: They are generally irreversible and associated with cognitive deficits, a decrease in quality of life and increased mortality.
target: TDs generally cannot be reversed and are linked with intellectual disabilities, decreased quality of life and increased death.

source: Furthermore, they potentially contribute to further stigmatization of the affected patients.
target: Furthermore, TDs possibly cause patients to be viewed more negatively by society.

source: However due to limited treatment options, antipsychotic drugs are still one of the cornerstones in treatment of most severe mental illnesses.
target: Because of a lack of treatment options, antipsychotic drugs are still one of the main treatments for serious mental illness.

source: Therefore, knowledge about risk factors and prevention of TDs is crucial.
target: Therefore, knowing about TD risk factors and prevention is very important.

source: If TDs occur, the immediate optimization of the antipsychotic drug regimen is required.
target: IF TDs happen, the amount of antipsychotic drugs prescribed must be changed quickly to an appropriate amount.

source: Targeted medical treatments such as VMAT - 2 inhibitors can be considered.
target: Specialized drugs such as vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT - 2) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), can be used.

source: The novel VMAT-2 inhibitors are not yet approved in Germany.
target: Germany has not yet approved the new VMAT-2 inhibitors.

source: Other drugs that are currently used to treat TDs include clonazepam and gingko biloba.
target: Clonazepam and gingko biloba, used for seizures and memory problems, respectively, are other drugs that can be used to treat TDs.

source: This review summarizes the current evidence of treatment options of TDs and seeks to formulate clinical recommendations for the prevention and management of TDs.
target: We summarize the current data on TD treatment options and try to come up with recommendations for doctors to prevent and treat TDs.

source: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a common, iatrogenic movement disorder affecting many individuals treated with dopamine-receptor blocking agents (DRBAs).
target: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a common movement disorder in many people taking antipsychotics.

source: Studying treatment of TD can be complex, as the symptoms can be affected by changes in either dosage or type of DRBA, as well as by the variable natural course of the disease.
target: Studying treatment of TD can be difficult, because the symptoms can change based on changes to how much or what kind of antipsychotics are used, and by the range of normal disease progression.

source: Historically many pharmacological therapies have been studied in TD, finding varying degrees of treatment success.
target: Many drugs made to treat TD have been studied with different levels of success.

source: Most recently, the VMAT2 inhibitors valbenazine and deutetrabenazine were rigorously studied in TD in large, phase III clinical trials, and were shown to be beneficial in this population.
target: Most recently, the vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT2) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), valbenazine and deutetrabenazine, were studied in TD in large human trials, and were shown to work in this group.

source: In this article, we will review various treatments of TD, including manipulation of the offending agent, VMAT2 inhibitors, other non-VMAT2-inhibiting medications, and non-pharmacological approaches.
target: In this article, we will look at different treatments of TD, including changing the drug causing TD, VMAT2 inhibitors, other non-VMAT2-inhibiting drugs, and non-drug options.

source: Background: Tardive dyskinesia is a movement disorder characterised by irregular, stereotyped, and choreiform movements associated with the use of antipsychotic medication.
target: Tardive dyskinesia is characterized by possibly irreversible, abnormal, uncontrolled movements related to the use of antipsychotics.

source: We aim to provide recommendations on the treatment of tardive dyskinesia.
target: We try to suggest ways to treat tardive dyskinesia.

source: Results: Preventing tardive dyskinesia is of primary importance, and clinicians should follow best practice for prescribing antipsychotic medication, including limiting the prescription for specific indications, using the minimum effective dose, and minimising the duration of therapy.
target: Preventing tardive dyskinesia is very important, and doctors should prescribe antipsychotics as recommended, including only prescribing them for certain conditions, using the lowest working dose, and limiting how long patients take them.

source: The first-line management of tardive dyskinesia is the withdrawal of antipsychotic medication if clinically feasible.
target: The first recommended treatment of tardive dyskinesia is to have the patient stop taking antipsychotics if possible.

source: Yet, for many patients with serious mental illness, the discontinuation of antipsychotics is not possible due to disease relapse.
target: Stopping antipsychotics is not possible for many patients with serious mental illness due to the disease returning.

source: Switching from a first-generation to a second-generation antipsychotic with a lower D2 affinity, such as clozapine or quetiapine, may be effective in reducing tardive dyskinesia symptoms.
target: Switching from a first-generation (older) to a second-generation (newer) antipsychotic, such as clozapine or quetiapine, may help reduce tardive dyskinesia symptoms.

source: The strongest evidence for a suitable co-intervention to treat tardive dyskinesia comes from tests with the new VMAT inhibitors, deutetrabenazine and valbenazine.
target: Studies show tests with new vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), deutetrabenazine and valbenazine, to be the best additional treatment for tardive dyskinesia.

source: These medications have not been approved for use in Canada.
target: Canada has not approved these drugs for use.

source: Conclusion: Data on tardive dyskinesia treatment are limited, and the best management strategy remains prevention.
target: We conclude that information on tardive dyskinesia treatment is limited, and preventing TD is the best way to manage it.

source: More long-term safety and efficacy data are needed for deutetrabenazine and valbenazine, and their routine availability to patients outside of the USA remains in question.
target: More studies that look at safety and how well deutetrabenazine and valbenazine work are needed, and it is uncertain how reliably available these drugs are to patients outside of the United States.

source: Background: Tardive dyskinesia is a movement disorder characterised by irregular, stereotyped, and choreiform movements associated with the use of antipsychotic medication.
target: Tardive duskinesia is a movement disorder of irregular, repetitive, and jerking movements linked with using antipsychotic medication for treating mental illnesses.

source: We aim to provide recommendations on the treatment of tardive dyskinesia.
target: We aim to provide recommendation on treating tardive dyskinesia.

source: Results: Preventing tardive dyskinesia is of primary importance, and clinicians should follow best practice for prescribing antipsychotic medication, including limiting the prescription for specific indications, using the minimum effective dose, and minimising the duration of therapy.
target: Preventing tardive dyskinesia is very important. Clinicians should follow the best policies for prescribing antipsychotic medication. These include limiting the amount for specific symptoms, using the minimum effective dose, and minimising the duration of treatment.

source: The first-line management of tardive dyskinesia is the withdrawal of antipsychotic medication if clinically feasible.
target: The first-to-try treatment of tardive dyskinesia is removing antipsychotic medication if clinically possible.

source: Yet, for many patients with serious mental illness, the discontinuation of antipsychotics is not possible due to disease relapse.
target: Yet, for many patients with serious mental illness, removing antipsychotics is not possible due to disease reappearance.

source: Switching from a first-generation to a second-generation antipsychotic with a lower D2 affinity, such as clozapine or quetiapine, may be effective in reducing tardive dyskinesia symptoms.
target: Switching from one class to another class of antipsychotics, like clozapine or quetiapine, may help reduce tardive dyskinesia symptoms.

source: The strongest evidence for a suitable co-intervention to treat tardive dyskinesia comes from tests with the new VMAT inhibitors, deutetrabenazine and valbenazine.
target: The strongest evidence for a suitable co-treatment for tardive dyskinesia comes from tests with new VMAT inhibitors, deutetrabenazine and valbenazine, which treat movement disorders.

source: These medications have not been approved for use in Canada.
target: These drugs have not been approved for use in Canada.

source: Conclusion: Data on tardive dyskinesia treatment are limited, and the best management strategy remains prevention.
target: Data on tardive dyskinesia treatment is limited. The best treatment remains prevention.

source: More long-term safety and efficacy data are needed for deutetrabenazine and valbenazine, and their routine availability to patients outside of the USA remains in question.
target: We need more long-term safety and effectiveness data for deutetrabenazine and valbenazine. Their regular availability to patients outside the USA is unknown.

source: Purpose of review: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is caused by exposure to medications with dopamine antagonism, mainly antipsychotics.
target: Taking medications, mainly antipsychotics, that reduce activity of dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward) causes tardive dyskinesia (TD) - a movement disorder.

source: It often distresses individuals, physically and emotionally and affects their quality of life.
target: TD often causes physical and emotional pain or suffering and affects the quality of life of patients.

source: We evaluated peer-reviewed recently published articles with a goal of providing a critically appraised update on the latest advancements in this field.
target: We rated scientific studies to summarize the latest advancements in the area of TD.

source: Recent findings: In 2017, FDA approved VMAT2 inhibitors, deutetrabenazine and valbenazine.
target: We found that FDA approved vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT2) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), deutetrabenazine and valbenazine, in 2017.

source: They have demonstrated efficacy in several class 1 studies.
target: These drugs work in several clinical trials.

source: Also there have been update in the evidence-based guidelines for treatment for tardive dyskinesia.
target: Best practice guidelines for doctors for treating tardive dyskinesia have also been updated.

source: Various medication classes are being used for treatment of TD with VMAT2 inhibitors to be first FDA-approved medications.
target: Different drugs are being used to treat TD with VMAT2 inhibitors to be the first drug approved by the FDA.

source: Their use should be tailored to the individual patient.
target: Drugs used to treat TD should be specific to the individual patient.

source: Long-term studies will further guide us in how to optimize treatment, especially in the real-world setting.
target: Long-term studies will determine how best to treat patients, especially in the real world.

source: As clinicians, we need to take into consideration all aspects of symptomatology, etiology, potential side effects of the medications, to find the best possible "match" for our patients.
target: As doctors, we need to consider all symptoms, causes, and possible drug side effects to find the best possible "match" for our patients.

source: Tardive dyskinesia (TD), a condition of potentially irreversible abnormal involuntary movements that is associated with dopamine receptor blocking agents (DRBAs), produces significant impairment of functioning and quality of life for patients.
target: Tardive dyskinesia (TD), characterized by possibly irreversible, abnormal, uncontrolled movements related to the use of antipsychotics, negatively affects functioning and quality of life for patients.

source: Contrary to expectations, TD has not vanished despite the introduction of SGAs.
target: Although many believed it would be different, TD has not gone away even with the use of second-generation antipsychotics (SGAs), which are less likely to cause movement side effects.

source: Instead, changing prescription practices and increased off-label prescription of DRBAs have placed more patients than ever at risk of this potentially dangerous and disabling condition.
target: Instead, changes in how antipsychotics are prescribed and antipsychotics prescribed for unapproved use have increased the patients at risk of TD.

source: This activity provides an overview of treatment strategies for TD as part of an individualized management plan, including DRBA medication adjustment and antidyskinetic treatment.
target: We describe treatment options for TD as part of an individual-specific plan, including changing antipsychotic dosing and treating dyskinesia.

source: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is an iatrogenic condition that encompasses a wide phenomenological spectrum of movement disorders caused by exposure to dopamine receptor blocking agents (DRBAs).
target: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a condition that includes a wide range of uncontrollable movement disorders caused by taking antipsychotics.

source: TD may cause troublesome or disabling symptoms that impair quality of life.
target: TD may cause physical and emotional pain or suffering that negatively affects quality of life.

source: Due to frequent, often inappropriate, use of DRBAs, TD prevalence rates among patients exposed to DRBAs continue to be high.
target: The total number of TD cases in patients who have taken antipsychotics at a given time continues to be high due to frequent, often unnecessary, antipsychotic use.

source: The judicious use of DRBAs is key to the prevention of TD, reduction of disease burden, and achieving lasting remission.
target: Sensible use of antipsychotics is needed to prevent, reduce disability and death associated with, and end TD for good.

source: Dopamine-depleting vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 inhibitors are considered the treatment of choice of TD.
target: Vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT2) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), are the best treatment for TD.

source: Objective: To examine the efficacy of pharmacologic treatments for tardive dyskinesia (TD).
target: We aim to understand how well different drugs work to treat tardive dyskinesia (TD) - a movement disorder.

source: Data sources: PubMed was searched on December 12, 2017, for randomized, placebo-controlled trials examining the treatment of TD using the search terms (drug-induced dyskinesia OR tardive dyskinesia) AND (psychotic disorders OR schizophrenia).
target: On December 12, 2017, we searched for published scientific studies looking at the treatment of TD versus sugar pills.

source: Study selection: Studies were included if they examined tardive dyskinesia treatment as the primary outcome and were randomized and placebo-controlled trials.
target: We included studies if they looked at treatment of TD as the main result and randomly assigned participants to groups receiving TD treatment or sugar pills.

source: A meta-analysis was conducted utilizing a fixed-effects model.
target: We used statistical methods to combine the results of multiple studies.

source: Results: Vitamin E was associated with significantly greater reduction in TD symptoms compared to placebo (standardized mean difference [SMD] = 0.31 ± 0.08; 95% CI, 0.16 to 0.46; z = 4.1; P < .001).
target: Results favored vitamin E over sugar pills in TD symptom reduction.

source: There was significant evidence of publication bias in vitamin E studies (Egger test: P = .02).
target: The results of the published vitamin E studies were different from results of unpublished studies.

source: Shorter duration of treatment and lower dose of vitamin E were significantly associated with greater measured treatment benefit.
target: Taking vitamin E for a shorter amount of time and at a lower dose was associated with greater TD symptom reduction.

source: Vitamin B₆ was associated with significantly greater reduction in TD symptoms compared to placebo (SMD = 1.41 ± 0.22; 95% CI, 0.98 to 1.85; z = 6.4; P < .001) in 2 trials conducted by the same research group.
target: Results favored vitamin B6 over sugar pills in TD symptom reduction.

source: Vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitors demonstrated significant benefit on tardive dyskinesia symptoms compared to placebo (SMD = 0.63 ± 0.11; 95% CI, 0.41 to 0.85; z = 5.58; P < .005).
target: Results favored vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 (VMAT2) inhibitors, drugs that reduce dopamine (a chemical messenger released when your brain is expecting a reward), over sugar pills in TD symptom reduction.

source: Amantadine was associated with significantly greater score reduction compared to placebo (SMD = 0.46 ± 0.21; 95% CI, 0.05 to 0.87; z = 2.20; P < .05).
target: Results favored amantadine - a dopamine promoter - over sugar pills.

source: Calcium channel blockers were not associated with significantly greater score reduction compared to placebo (SMD = 0.31 ± 0.33; 95% CI, -0.34 to 0.96; z = 0.93; P = .35).
target: Results did not favor calcium channel blockers, which block transport of calcium in the body, over sugar pills.

source: Conclusions: Data from multiple trials suggests that VMAT2 inhibitors, vitamin E, vitamin B₆, and amantadine may be effective for the treatment of TD.
target: We conclude that many studies show that VMAT2 inhibitors, vitamin E, vitamin B₆, and amantadine may work to treat TD.

source: Evidence of publication bias and a significant negative association of dose and duration of treatment with measured efficacy suggest that the benefits of vitamin E in TD may be overstated.
target: Because results of the published vitamin E studies were different from results of unpublished studies and taking vitamin E for a shorter amount of time and at a lower dose was associated with greater TD symptom reduction, the benefits of vitamin E in TD might not be as great as studies suggest.

source: Head-to-head trials are needed to compare the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of pharmacologic agents for TD.
target: Studies comparing TD treatments to each other are needed to compare how well they work and how cost-effective they are.

source: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a condition of potentially irreversible abnormal involuntary movements associated with dopamine receptor blocking agents, such as antipsychotics.
target: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is characterized by possibly irreversible, abnormal, uncontrolled movements related to the use of antipsychotics.

source: While prevention is the best strategy, it is not always possible.
target: While prevention is the best approach, it is not always possible.

source: This report outlines strategies to reduce TD symptoms, including the use of the FDA-approved treatment options (valbenazine and deutetrabenazine).
target: This report lists approaches to reduce TD symptoms, including the use of treatments options approved by the FDA (such as the drugs valbenazine and deutetrabenazine).

source: Objectives: Elevated plasma homocysteine has been implicated as a risk factor for hypertension.
target: Increase plasma, or blood, homocysteine has been identified as a risk factor for hypertension (high blood pressure). Homocysteine is a chemical your body produces to help make proteins.

source: C677T polymorphism in methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase gene (MTHFR) is a major determinant of hyperhomocysteinemia, which results in endothelial dysfunction.
target: An identified genetic variation or gene type within the human population is a major determinant of hyperhomocysteinemia. Hyperhomocysteinemia is a condition with excess homocysteine in the blood. The condition can result in endothelial dysfunction, or a heart disease where the blood vessels narrow instead of opening.

source: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors appear to remedy the endothelial dysfunction and restore endothelium-dependent vasodilatation.
target: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are a type of drug commonly used to treat high blood pressure. ACE Inhibitors appear to fix the endothelial (or blood vessel lining) dysfunction and allow blood vessels to open.

source: The co-existence of genetic polymorphisms in drug metabolizing enzymes, targets, receptors, and transporters may influence the drug efficacy.
target: The presence of the genetic variation in specific bodily locations that metabolize or digest drugs, such as enzymes and receptors, may influence how well a drug works.

source: The purpose of this study was to investigate whether short-term blood pressure control by benazepril, an ACE inhibitor, was modulated by C677T MTHFR gene polymorphism.
target: The goal of this study was to investigate if short-term blood pressure control by an ACE inhibitor (Benazepril) is effected by the identified genetic variation.

source: Methods and results: A total of 444 hypertensive patients, aged 27 to 65 years, without any anti hypertensive therapy within 2 weeks were included.
target: A total of 444 hypertensive patients, aged 27 to 65 years, without any anti hypertensive therapy within 2 weeks were included.

source: All of them were treated orally with benazepril at a single daily fixed dosage of 10 mg for 15 consecutive days.
target: All patients were treated with Benazepril with a single, daily dose of 10 mg for 15 consecutive days.

source: Blood pressures were measured at baseline and on the 16th day of treatment.
target: Blood pressures were measured prior to the study beginning (baseline) and on the 16th day of treatment.

source: Among them, the frequency of MTHFR C677T genotype CC, CT and TT was 24.3%, 51.8%, and 23.9%, respectively.
target: The unique genetic variation has three unique "versions" or genotypes, known as CC, CT, or TT. Among the patients, the frequency of the genetic variation genotype CC, CT, and TT was 24.3%, 51.8%, and 23.9%, respectively.

source: In a recessive model (CC+CT versus TT genotype), both baseline diastolic blood pressure (DBP) and diastolic blood pressure response (DeltaDBP) were significantly higher in patients with the TT genotype than in those with the CT or CC genotype (P value=0.0076 for DBP, and P value=0.0005 for DeltaDBP).
target: In a statistical analysis, several blood pressure measurements were higher in patients with the TT genotype than those with the CT or CC genotype.

source: We further divided all patients into three groups based on the tertiles of the DeltaBP distribution.
target: The authors further divided all patients into three groups based on where their diastolic blood pressure response (DeltaDBP) fell within a population scale. Diastolic blood pressure is the lowest pressure when the heart is relaxed.

source: Compared to subjects in the lowest tertile of DeltaDBP, the adjusted relative odds of having the TT genotype among subjects in the highest tertile was 2.6 (95% CI, 1.4 to 4.9).
target: Patients with the highest DeltaDBP had the highest chance of having the TT genotype.

source: However, baseline systolic blood pressure (SBP) and SBP response did not significantly associate with MTHFR C677T polymorphism.
target: However, baseline systolic blood pressure (when the heart is contracting) was not significantly associated with the unique genetic variation.

source: Conclusions: Our finding suggests that MTHFR C667T polymorphism modulated baseline DBP and DBP responsiveness by short-term treatment of ACE inhibitor in Chinese essential hypertensive patients.
target: This study suggests that the unique genetic variation altered baseline DBP by short-term treatment of ACE inhibitor in hypertensive patients.

source: Objectives: Elevated plasma homocysteine has been implicated as a risk factor for hypertension.
target: Increased blood levels of homocysteine, a specific chemical, may be a risk factor for high blood pressure.

source: C677T polymorphism in methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase gene (MTHFR) is a major determinant of hyperhomocysteinemia, which results in endothelial dysfunction.
target: A specific mutation in DNA (C677T) encoding methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR), a protein that transforms homocysteine, greatly influences hyperhomocysteinemia, or high blood levels of homocysteine. This condition results in damage to cells that line the heart and blood vessels.

source: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors appear to remedy the endothelial dysfunction and restore endothelium-dependent vasodilatation.
target: Blood pressure medication called angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors seem to repair damage to cells lining the heart and blood vessels and restore blood vessel widening.

source: The co-existence of genetic polymorphisms in drug metabolizing enzymes, targets, receptors, and transporters may influence the drug efficacy.
target: The co-existence of varied DNA for drug-digesting enzymes, targets, target sites, and transporters may affect drug effect.

source: The purpose of this study was to investigate whether short-term blood pressure control by benazepril, an ACE inhibitor, was modulated by C677T MTHFR gene polymorphism.
target: This study investigates if short-term blood pressure control by benazepril, an ACE inhibitor, was affected by variation in the DNA sequence for the C667T MTHFR gene.

source: Methods and results: A total of 444 hypertensive patients, aged 27 to 65 years, without any anti hypertensive therapy within 2 weeks were included.
target: 444 patients with high blood pressure, aged 27 to 65 years, without any high blood pressure therapy within 2 weeks were included.

source: All of them were treated orally with benazepril at a single daily fixed dosage of 10 mg for 15 consecutive days.
target: All of them swallowed benazepril at a single daily fixed dose of 10 mg for 15 consecutive days.

source: Blood pressures were measured at baseline and on the 16th day of treatment.
target: Blood pressures were measured at start and on the 16th day of treatment.

source: Among them, the frequency of MTHFR C677T genotype CC, CT and TT was 24.3%, 51.8%, and 23.9%, respectively.
target: Among them, the gene sequence for MTHFR C677T varied.

source: In a recessive model (CC+CT versus TT genotype), both baseline diastolic blood pressure (DBP) and diastolic blood pressure response (DeltaDBP) were significantly higher in patients with the TT genotype than in those with the CT or CC genotype (P value=0.0076 for DBP, and P value=0.0005 for DeltaDBP).
target: Diastolic blood pressure at the start and end were much higher for patients with a certain DNA sequence for the MTHFR C677T gene than patients with two other DNA sequences.

source: We further divided all patients into three groups based on the tertiles of the DeltaBP distribution.
target: We then divided all patients into 3 groups based on the distribution of blood pressure readings at the study's end.

source: Compared to subjects in the lowest tertile of DeltaDBP, the adjusted relative odds of having the TT genotype among subjects in the highest tertile was 2.6 (95% CI, 1.4 to 4.9).
target: Compared to those with the lowest blood pressure measurements, the chances of having a certain DNA sequence for the MTHFR C667T gene among those with the highest blood pressure readings was 2.6 times higher.

source: However, baseline systolic blood pressure (SBP) and SBP response did not significantly associate with MTHFR C677T polymorphism.
target: However, systolic blood pressure at the start and end was not linked to DNA sequences.

source: Conclusions: Our finding suggests that MTHFR C667T polymorphism modulated baseline DBP and DBP responsiveness by short-term treatment of ACE inhibitor in Chinese essential hypertensive patients.
