target: Twenty-two patients with at least two sALP measurements below the desired range were enrolled. Any patients with additional disease or health complications were excluded.

source: Data about clinical features, mineral and bone markers, serum pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP), urine phosphoethanolamine (PEA), lumbar and femur bone densitometry, and column X-ray were collected.
target: Several data points for patient health and biological indicators of disease status were collected.

source: Peripheral blood DNA of each participant was analyzed to detect ALPL gene anomalies.
target: Patient DNA was analyzed to detect ALPL gene anomalies.

source: Results: Pathogenic ALPL variants (pALPL) occurred in 23% and benign variants in 36% of patients (bALPL), while nine patients harbored wild-type alleles (wtALPL).
target: Several types of mutations within the ALPL gene were found amongst the participant population. These variants include pathogenic (harmful) ALPL variants (pALPL), benign (harmless) variants (bALPL), and wild-type (normal) variants (wtALPL). pALPL are variants that increases a person's chance to get sick from a disease. bALPL are mutations that do not impact human health. wtALPL variants are genes that are not mutated or changed in form.

source: Fragility fractures and dental anomalies were more frequent in patients harboring pALPL and bALPL than in wtALPL patients.
target: Bone and tooth damage were more frequent in patients harboring pALPL and bALPL than in wtALPL patients.

source: Of note, wtALPL patients comprised women treated with tamoxifen for hormone-sensitive breast cancer.
target: Of note, wtALPL patients comprised women treated with tamoxifen, a drug for breast cancer.

source: Mineral and bone markers were similar in the three groups.
target: Mineral and bone markers were similar in the three groups.

source: Mean urine PEA levels were significantly higher in patients harboring pALPL than those detected in patients harboring bALPL and wtALPL; by contrast, serum PLP levels were similar in the three groups.
target: Average urine phosphoethanolamine, a compound that can indicate bone disease, was significantly higher in patients with pALPL than the other two variant types. By contrast, serum pyridoxal-5'-phosphate, an indicator of vitamin B6 volume, was similar in the three groups.

source: A 6-points score, considering clinical and biochemical features, was predictive of pALPL detection [P = 0.060, OR 1.92 (95% CI 0.972, 3.794)], and more significantly of pALPL or bALPL [P = 0.025, OR 14.33 (95% CI 1.401, 14.605)].
target: Certain hospital evaluation techniques were able to predict pALPL and bALPL.

source: Conclusion: In osteopenic/osteoporotic patients, single clinical or biochemical factors did not distinguish hypophosphatasemic patients harboring pALPL or bALPL from those harboring wtALPL.
target: In patients with osteoporosis (brittle bones), visible health effects and biological compound indicators do not distinguish hypophosphatasemia (low serum ALP) patients harboring pALPL or bALPL variants from those harboring wtALPL.

source: Occurrence of multiple clinical and biochemical features is predictive of ALPL anomalies, and, therefore, they should be carefully identified.
target: However, visible health effects and biological compound indicators can be predictive of ALPL anomalies. Therefore, they should be carefully identified.

source: Tamoxifen emerged as a hypophosphatasemic drug.
target: Tamoxifen emerged as a drug to treat hypophosphatasemia.

source: Background: Severe hypercalcemia is rare in newborns; even though often asymptomatic, it may have important sequelae.
target: Severe hypercalcemia, or too high calcium blood levels, is rare in newborns. Even though hypercalcemia often shows no symptoms, it may have important effects on overall health.

source: Hypophosphatemia can occur in infants experiencing intrauterine malnutrition, sepsis and early high-energy parenteral nutrition (PN) and can cause severe hypercalcemia through an unknown mechanism.
target: Hypophosphatemia is when the body has low levels of phosphorous. Hypophosphatemia can occur in infants experiencing malnutrition while in the womb, blood infection, and early high-energy parenteral nutrition (PN). PN is when a mother is given nutrients through IV. Hypophosphatemia can cause severe hypercalcemia through an unknown mechanism.

source: Monitoring and supplementation of phosphate (PO4) and calcium (Ca) in the first week of life in preterm infants are still debated.
target: It is debated if phosphate (PO4) and calcium (Ca) should be given in the first week of life in preterm infants.

source: Case presentation: We report on a female baby born at 29 weeks' gestation with intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) experiencing sustained severe hypercalcemia (up to 24 mg/dl corrected Ca) due to hypophosphatemia while on phosphorus-free PN.
target: This study reports on a female baby born at 29 weeks' gestation (time in womb). The infant had intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) and long-term severe hypercalcemia due to hypophosphatemia while on phosphorus-free PN. IUGR is when a baby does not grow to normal weight during pregnancy.

source: Hypercalcemia did not improve after hyperhydration and furosemide but responded to infusion of PO4.
target: Hypercalcemia did not improve after excess water intake but responded to infusion of PO4.

source: Eventually, the infant experienced symptomatic hypocalcaemia (ionized Ca 3.4 mg/dl), likely exacerbated by contemporary infusion of albumin.
target: Eventually, the infant experienced symptoms of hypocalcemia. This was likely worsened by infusion of albumin, a blood protein.

source: Subsequently, a normalization of both parathyroid hormone (PTH) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was observed.
target: Following this, parathyroid hormone (PTH), which controls calcium and phosphorus in the blood, and alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which removes phosphate groups,  returned to normal levels.

source: Conclusions: Although severe hypercalcemia is extremely rare in neonates, clinicians should be aware of the possible occurrence of this life-threatening condition in infants with or at risk to develop hypophosphatemia.
target: Severe hypercalcemia is extremely rare in newborns. However, doctors should be aware of the possible occurrence of this life-threatening condition in infants with or at risk to develop hypophosphatemia.

source: Hypophosphatemic hypercalcemia can only be managed with infusion of PO4, with strict monitoring of Ca and PO4 concentrations.
target: Hypophosphatemic hypercalcemia can only be managed with infusion of PO4, with strict monitoring of Ca and PO4 concentrations.

source: Background: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is an inborn disease caused by pathogenic variants in ALPL.
target: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a disease caused by mutations within the gene ALPL. Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is the heritable, tooth and bone disease. The gene ALPL encodes for alkaline phosphatase (ALP), an enzyme that helps bone strength.

source: Low levels of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) are a biochemical hallmark of the disease.
target: Low levels of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) are a hallmark of the disease.

source: Scarce knowledge about the prevalence of HPP in Scandinavia exists, and the variable clinical presentations make diagnostics challenging.
target: There is little knowledge about the prevalence of HPP in Scandinavia. Due to the variation in HPP clinical presentations, diagnosing the disease is challenging.

source: The aim of this study was to investigate the prevalence of ALPL variants as well as the clinical and biochemical features among adults with endocrinological diagnoses and persistent hypophosphatasaemia.
target: The aim of this study was to investigate the prevalence of ALPL variants (gene types). The study also aimed to identify HPP biological identifiers among adults. This study was specifically interested in adults with HPP along with endocrine (hormonal) disorders and persistent hypophosphatasemia (low body phosphorus levels).

source: Methods: A biochemical database containing ALP measurements of 26,121 individuals was reviewed to identify adults above 18 years of age with persistently low levels of ALP beneath range (≤ 35 ± 2.7 U/L).
target: A database containing ALP measurements of 26,121 individuals was reviewed to identify adults above 18 years of age with persistently low levels of ALP.

source: ALPL genetic testing, biochemical evaluations and assessment of clinical features by a systematic questionnaire among included patients, were performed.
target: ALPL genetic testing, evaluation of body compound levels, and assessment of health by a systematic questionnaire among included patients, were performed.

source: Results: Among 24 participants, thirteen subjects (54.2%) revealed a disease-causing variant in ALPL and reported mild clinical features of HPP, of which musculoskeletal pain was the most frequently reported (n = 9).
target: Among 24 participants, thirteen subjects revealed a disease-causing variant in ALPL and reported mild health effects of HPP. Of these mild health effects, musculoskeletal pain was the most frequently reported.

source: The variant c. 571G > A; p.(Glu191 Lys) was identified in six subjects, and an unreported missense variant (c.1019A > C; p.(His340Pro)) as well as a deletion of exon 2 were detected by genetic screening.
target: The same genetic variant was identified in six subjects. An unreported missense (gene-altered) variant and a genetic deletion were detected by genetic screening.

source: Biochemical analyses showed no significant differences in ALP (p = 0.059), the bone specific alkaline phosphatase (BALP) (p = 0.056) and pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) (p = 0.085) between patients with an ALPL variant and negative genetic screening.
target: Analysis of compound levels within the body showed no significant differences between patients with an ALPL variant and negative (no-detection) genetic screening.

source: Patients with a variant in ALPL had significantly higher PLP levels than healthy controls (p = 0.002).
target: Patients with a variant in ALPL had significantly higher PLP levels than control patients.

source: We observed normal ALP activity in some patients classified as mild HPP, and slightly increased levels of PLP in two subjects with normal genetic screening and four healthy controls.
target: Normal ALP activity was observed in some patients classified as mild HPP. A slightly increased level of PLP was found in two subjects with normal genetic screening and four healthy controls.

source: Among 51 patients with persistent hypophosphatasaemia, fifteen subjects (29.4%) received antiresorptive treatment.
target: Among 51 patients with persistent hypophosphatasemia, 15 received treatment to increase bone strength.

source: Two patients with unrecognized HPP were treated with bisphosphonates and did not show complications due to the treatment.
target: Two patients with undiagnosed HPP were treated with bisphosphonates, drugs that slow bone loss. They did not show complications due to the treatment.

source: Conclusions: Pathogenic variants in ALPL are common among patients with endocrinological diagnoses and low ALP.
target: Genetic variations in ALPL are common among patients with endocrine disorders and low ALP.

source: Regarding diagnostics, genetic testing is necessary to identify mild HPP due to fluctuating biochemical findings.
target: Genetic testing is necessary to identify mild HPP due to fluctuating body compound level findings.

source: Antiresorptive treatment is a frequent reason for hypophosphatasaemia and effects of these agents in adults with a variant in ALPL and osteoporosis remain unclear and require further studies.
target: Treatment to increase bone strength is a frequent reason for hypophosphatasemia. Effects of these agents in adults with a variant in ALPL and osteoporosis (brittle bones) remain unclear and require further studies.

source: Background: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is an inborn disease caused by pathogenic variants in ALPL.
target: Low levels of blood phosphate, a high-energy molecule, or hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a from-birth disease caused by disease-causing variations in ALPL (the gene for alkaline phosphatase or a protein that influences phosphate levels).

source: Low levels of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) are a biochemical hallmark of the disease.
target: Low levels of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) are a marker of HPP.

source: Scarce knowledge about the prevalence of HPP in Scandinavia exists, and the variable clinical presentations make diagnostics challenging.
target: Little is known about the amount of HPP in Scandinavia. The diverse symptoms make identification challenging.

source: The aim of this study was to investigate the prevalence of ALPL variants as well as the clinical and biochemical features among adults with endocrinological diagnoses and persistent hypophosphatasaemia.
target: The study aimed to investigate the amount of ALPL variants and physical and biochemical markers among adults with hormonal diagnoses and persistant low ALP levels.

source: Methods: A biochemical database containing ALP measurements of 26,121 individuals was reviewed to identify adults above 18 years of age with persistently low levels of ALP beneath range (≤ 35 ± 2.7 U/L).
target: We reviewed a database of ALP measurements from 26,121 individuals to identify adults over 18 years with persistently low levels of ALP.

source: ALPL genetic testing, biochemical evaluations and assessment of clinical features by a systematic questionnaire among included patients, were performed.
target: ALPL gene testing, biological tests, and assessment of symptoms by a questionnaire among included patients were performed.

source: Results: Among 24 participants, thirteen subjects (54.2%) revealed a disease-causing variant in ALPL and reported mild clinical features of HPP, of which musculoskeletal pain was the most frequently reported (n = 9).
target: Among 24 patients, thirteen (54.2%) had a disease-causing variant in ALPL and mild symptoms of HPP, of which musculoskeletal pain was the most frequent.

source: The variant c. 571G > A; p.(Glu191 Lys) was identified in six subjects, and an unreported missense variant (c.1019A > C; p.(His340Pro)) as well as a deletion of exon 2 were detected by genetic screening.
target: A specific gene mutation that altered a protein segment was found in six subjects. A similar gene mutation and deletion of a gene segment was detected by genetic tests.

source: Biochemical analyses showed no significant differences in ALP (p = 0.059), the bone specific alkaline phosphatase (BALP) (p = 0.056) and pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) (p = 0.085) between patients with an ALPL variant and negative genetic screening.
target: Tests showed no differences in ALP, bone-specific ALP, and pyridoxal phosphate (PLP) or active vitamin B6 between patients with an ALPL mutation and no genetic diseases.

source: Patients with a variant in ALPL had significantly higher PLP levels than healthy controls (p = 0.002).
target: Patients with an ALPL mutation had much higher PLP levels than healthy patients.

source: We observed normal ALP activity in some patients classified as mild HPP, and slightly increased levels of PLP in two subjects with normal genetic screening and four healthy controls.
target: We saw normal ALP activity in some patients with mild HPP. We also saw slightly increased PLP in two subjects with no gene diseases and four healthy patients.

source: Among 51 patients with persistent hypophosphatasaemia, fifteen subjects (29.4%) received antiresorptive treatment.
target: Among 51 with persistent low ALP levels, fifteen (29.4%) had anti-bone-breakdown treatment.

source: Two patients with unrecognized HPP were treated with bisphosphonates and did not show complications due to the treatment.
target: Two patients with unrecognized HPP were treated with anti-bone-breakdown drugs and did not show side effects from treatment.

source: Conclusions: Pathogenic variants in ALPL are common among patients with endocrinological diagnoses and low ALP.
target: Disease-causing mutations in ALPL are common in patients with hormonal diagnoses and low ALP.

source: Regarding diagnostics, genetic testing is necessary to identify mild HPP due to fluctuating biochemical findings.
target: Regarding detection, gene testing is needed to identify mild HPP due to diverse biochemical findings.

source: Antiresorptive treatment is a frequent reason for hypophosphatasaemia and effects of these agents in adults with a variant in ALPL and osteoporosis remain unclear and require further studies.
target: Anti-bone-breakdown treatment is frequent reason for low ALP levels and treatment effects in adults with an ALPL mutation and bone breakdown remain unclear and need further studies.

source: Summary: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a rare and under-recognised genetic defect in bone mineralisation.
target: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a rare and under-recognized genetic defect in bone mineralization. Bone mineralization is when bone become calcified.

source: Patients presenting with fragility fractures may be mistakenly diagnosed as having osteoporosis and prescribed antiresorptive therapy, a treatment which may increase fracture risk.
target: Patients presenting with fragility fractures may be mistakenly diagnosed as having osteoporosis (brittle bones). These patients may be prescribed antiresorptive therapy, or drugs that block the breakdown of bone. These drugs may increase fracture risk.

source: Adult-onset HPPhypophosphatasia was identified in a 40-year-old woman who presented with bilateral atypical femoral fractures after 4 years of denosumab therapy.
target: Adult-onset HPP was identified in a 40-year-old woman. She had with femur fractures after 4 years of using denosumba, a drug to treat osteoporosis.

source: A low serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and increased serum vitamin B6 level signalled the diagnosis, which was later confirmed by identification of two recessive mutations of the ALPL gene.
target: A low serum (blood) alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and increased serum vitamin B6 level signaled the diagnosis. Her diagnosis of HPP was later confirmed by identification of two recessive gene mutations of the ALPL gene. ALP is an enzyme that helps bone strength. ALPL is the gene that encodes ALP.

source: The patient was treated with teriparatide given the unavailability of ALP enzyme-replacement therapy (asfotase alfa).
target: The patient was treated with teriparatide (an osteoporosis drug) due to the unavailability of ALP enzyme-replacement therapy.

source: Fracture healing occurred, but impaired mobility persisted.
target: The fractures healed. However, her mobility was impaired.

source: HPP predisposes to atypical femoral fracture (AFF) during antiresorptive therapy; hence, bisphosphonates and denosumab are contraindicated in this condition.
target: HPP makes someone more likely to experience femur fractures during antiresorptive therapy. Because of this, bisphosphonates (drugs that limit bone density) and denosumab should not be given to people with this condition.

source: Screening patients with fracture or 'osteoporosis' to identify a low ALP level is recommended.
target: Healthcare workings should screen patients with fracture or 'osteoporosis' to identify a low ALP level.

source: Learning points: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is a rare and under-recognised cause of bone fragility produced by impaired matrix mineralisation that can be misdiagnosed as a fragility fracture due to age-related bone loss.
target: HPP is a rare and under-recognized cause of bone fragility. HPP is caused by impaired mineralization that can be misdiagnosed as a fragility fracture due to age-related bone loss.

source: Antiresorptive therapy is contraindicated in HPP.
target: Antiresorptive therapy is not recommended in HPP treatment.

source: Low serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) provides a clue to the diagnosis.
target: Low serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) can aid in diagnosis.

source: Elevated serum vitamin B6 (an ALP substrate) is indicative of HPP, while identification of a mutation in the ALPL gene is confirmatory.
target: Elevated serum vitamin B6 (a chemical that works with ALP) is suggestive of HPP. Identifying mutations within the ALPL gene can confirm HPP status.

source: Enzyme therapy with recombinant ALP (asfotase alfa) is currently prohibitively costly.
target: Therapy with a chemical ALP reacts with, known as asfotase alfa, is currently unmanageably expensive.

source: Treatment with anabolic bone agents such as teriparatide has been reported, but whether normally mineralized bone is formed requires further study.
target: Treatment with anabolic (repairing) bone agents has been reported. However, if this treatment helps normally mineralized bone form needs further study.

source: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is the heritable dento-osseous disease caused by loss-of-function mutation(s) of the gene ALPL that encodes the tissue-nonspecific isoenzyme of alkaline phosphatase (TNSALP).
target: Hypophosphatasia (HPP) is the heritable, tooth and bone disease. HPP is caused by mutations of the gene ALPL. ALPL encodes the tissue-nonspecific isoenzyme of alkaline phosphatase (TNSALP). TNSALP is an enzyme that assists in breaking down phosphate groups. Phosphate groups are important in activating proteins.

source: TNSALP is a cell-surface homodimeric phosphomonoester phosphohydrolase expressed in healthy people especially in the skeleton, liver, kidneys, and developing teeth.
target: TNSALP is expressed in healthy people, especially in the skeleton, liver, kidneys, and developing teeth.

source: In HPP, diminished TNSALP activity leads to extracellular accumulation of its natural substrates including inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), an inhibitor of mineralization, and pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP), the principal circulating form of vitamin B6 (B6).
target: In HPP, decreased TNSALP activity leads to extracellular accumulation of its natural substrates, including pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP). PLP is the principal circulating form of vitamin B6 (B6).

source: Autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive inheritance involving >450 usually missense defects scattered throughout ALPL largely explains the remarkably broad-ranging severity of this inborn-error-of-metabolism.
target: Different mutations of ALPL largely explains the remarkably broad-ranging severity of this genetic inborn-error-of-metabolism.

source: In 1985 when we identified elevated plasma PLP as a biochemical hallmark of HPP, all 14 investigated affected children and adults had markedly increased PLP levels.
target: In 1985, elevated (blood) plasma PLP was identified as hallmark of HPP. In the study, all 14 investigated affected children and adults had increased PLP levels.

source: However, pyridoxal (PL), the dephosphorylated form of PLP that enters cells to cofactor many enzymatic reactions, was not low but often inexplicably elevated.
target: However, pyridoxal (PL), an alternate form of PLP that enters cells to cofactor (help) many enzymatic reactions, was often unexplainably elevated.

source: Levels of pyridoxic acid (PA), the B6 degradation product quantified to assess B6 sufficiency, were unremarkable.
target: Levels of pyridoxic acid (PA), a compound used to determine B6 levels in the body, were unremarkable.

source: Canonical signs or symptoms of B6 deficiency or toxicity were absent.
target: Signs or symptoms of B6 deficiency or toxicity were absent.

source: B6-dependent seizures in infants with life-threatening HPP were later explained by their profound deficiency of TNSALP activity blocking PLP dephosphorylation to PL and diminishing gamma-aminobutyric acid synthesis in the brain.
target: B6-dependent seizures in infants with life-threatening HPP were later explained by their low TNSALP activity. Lack of TNSALP activity was blocking PLP dephosphorylation, removal of phosphate groups, to PL. This diminished formation of gamma-aminobutyric acid, a neurotransmitter, in the brain.

source: Now, there is speculation that altered B6 metabolism causes further clinical complications in HPP.
target: It is theorized that abnormal B6 metabolism causes further health complications in HPP.

source: Herein, we assessed the plasma PL and PA levels accompanying previously reported elevated plasma PLP concentrations in 150 children and adolescents with HPP.
target: This study assessed (measured) the blood PL and PA levels in patients with previously reported elevated plasma PLP concentrations. The evaluated patient pool consisted of 150 children and adolescents with HPP.

source: Their mean (SD) plasma PL level was nearly double the mean for our healthy pediatric controls: 66.7 (59.0) nM versus 37.1 (22.2) nM (P < 0.0001), respectively.
target: Their average plasma PL level was nearly double the average of healthy pediatric controls.

source: Their PA levels were broader than our pediatric control range, but their mean value was normal; 40.2 (25.1) nM versus 39.3 (9.9) nM (P = 0.7793), respectively.
target: Their PA levels were broader than our pediatric (child) control range with a normal average value.

source: In contrast, adults with HPP often had plasma PL and PA levels suggestive of dietary B6 insufficiency.
target: In contrast, adults with HPP often had plasma PL and PA levels that suggest a dietary B6 insufficiency.

source: We discuss why the B6 levels of our pediatric patients with HPP would not cause B6 toxicity or deficiency, whereas in affected adults dietary B6 insufficiency can develop.
target: This paper discusses why B6 levels of children with HPP would not cause B6 toxicity or deficiency, whereas in affected adults dietary B6 insufficiency can develop.

source: Background: Tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (TNSALP) encoded by the ALPL gene is of particular importance for bone mineralization.
target: Background: Tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (TNSALP) is an enzyme that assists in breaking down phosphate groups. Phosphate groups are important in activating proteins. TNSALP is encoded by a gene, known as ALPL, that is of particular importance for bone mineralization or when minerals deposit onto bone.

source: Mutation in the ALPL gene can lead to persistent low ALP activity resulting in the rare disease Hypophosphatasia (HPP) that is characterized by disturbed bone and dental mineralization.
target: Mutation, or a structural change, in the ALPL gene can cause persistent low alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity. ALP is an enzyme that helps bone strength. Low ALP activity can result in the rare disease Hypophosphatasia (HPP). HPP is characterized by disturbed bone and dental or tooth mineralization.

source: While severe forms are extremely rare with an estimated prevalence of 1/100.000, recent studies suggest that moderate form caused by heterozygous mutations are much more frequent with an estimated prevalence of 1/508.
target: Severe forms of HPP are extremely rare. However, there is an estimated prevalence of 1/100,000 people. Recent studies suggest that moderate HPP is more common with an estimated prevalence of 1/508 people.

source: The purpose of this study was to estimate the prevalence of low AP levels in the population based on laboratory measurements.
target: The aim of this study was to estimate the prevalence of low ALP levels in the population based on laboratory measurements.

source: Methods: In this study, the prevalence of low AP activity and elevated pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP) levels was analyzed in 6.918.126 measurements from 2011 to 2016 at a single laboratory in northern Germany.
target: The prevalence of low AP activity and elevated pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP) levels was analyzed in 6,918,126 measurements from 2011 to 2016. PLP is a co-enzyme or helper in several body processes.

source: Only laboratory values of subjects older than 18 years of age were included.
target: Only laboratory values of subjects older than 18 years of age were included.

source: Only the first measurement was included, all repeated values were excluded.
target: Only the first measurement was included. All repeated values were excluded.

source: Results: In total, 8.46% of the measurements of a total of 6.918.126 values showed a value < 30 U/L. 0.59% of the subjects with an ALP activity below 30 U/L had an additional PLP measurement.
target: In total, 8.46% of the measurements showed a value < 30 U/L. 0.59% of the subjects with an ALP activity below 30 U/L had an additional PLP measurement.

source: Here, 6.09% showed elevated pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP) levels.
target: Of the participants, 6.09% showed elevated pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP) levels.

source: This suggest that 0.52% (1:194) of subjects show laboratory signs of HPP.
target: This suggest that 0.52% of subjects show signs of HPP.

source: Conclusion: These data support the genetic estimation that the prevalence of moderate forms of HPP may be significantly higher than expected.
target: These data support the estimation that the prevalence of moderate forms of HPP is higher than expected.

source: Based on these data, we recommend automatically measurement of PLP in the case of low ALP activity and a notification to the ordering physician that HPP should be included in the differential diagnosis and further exploration is recommended.
target: The authors recommend automatic measurement of PLP in cases of low ALP activity. Physicians should be notified that HPP should be included in the diagnosis and further exploration is recommended.

source: Objectives: To characterize the relationship of anifrolumab pharmacokinetics with efficacy and safety in patients with moderate to severe SLE despite standard therapy, using pooled data from 2 phase 3 trials.
target: The objective of this study is to describe the relationship of how the drug called anifrolumab works with the effectiveness and safety in patients who have moderate to severe lupus (when your immune system attacks healthy cells) despite receiving standard therapy. The study uses data gathered from clinical study trials.

source: Methods: TULIP-1 and TULIP-2 were randomized, placebo-controlled, 52-week trials of intravenous anifrolumab (every 4 weeks for 48 weeks).
target: TULIP-1 and TULIP-2 are 52-week clinical trials of anifrolumab given through a vein every 4 weeks for 48 weeks.

source: For the exposure-response analysis, BILAG-based Composite Lupus Assessment (BICLA) or SLE Responder Index (SRI[4]) response rates at week 52 in each quartile/tertile of average anifrolumab serum concentration (Cave) were compared for anifrolumab and placebo in all-comers, patients who completed treatment, and interferon gene signature (IFNGS)-high patients who completed treatment, using average marginal effect logistic regression.
target: Anifrolumab and placebo are compared using different assessment tools and analyses.

source: Relationships between exposure and key safety events were assessed graphically.
target: Relationships between exposure to the medication and key safety events are assessed graphically.

source: Results: Of patients in TULIP-1/TULIP-2 who received anifrolumab (150 mg [n = 91], 300 mg [n = 356]) or placebo (n = 366), 574 completed treatment, of whom 470 were IFNGS high.
target: Of patients in TULIP-1/TULIP-2 who received anifrolumab (a total of 447 patients receiving a dose amount of 150 or 300mg) or sham treatment/placebo (366 patients), 574 patients completed treatment.

source: In the exposure-efficacy analyses, BICLA and SRI(4) treatment differences favoring anifrolumab 300 mg vs placebo were observed across Cave subgroups and all analysis populations.
target: In the exposure-effectiveness analyses, treatment differences favoring anifrolumab (at 300) vs placebo are observed across subgroups of the study participants and all analysis populations.

source: Logistic regression identified Cave as a significant covariate for predicted BICLA response, as higher anifrolumab Cave predicted greater efficacy.
target: The concentration of the drug is found to be a significant factor for having a response, as higher anifrolumab in blood predicted greater effectiveness.

source: There was no evidence of exposure-driven incidence of key safety events through week 52 in patients receiving anifrolumab 150 or 300 mg.
target: There is no evidence of key safety events from taking the drug through week 52 in patients receiving anifrolumab 150 or 300 mg.

source: Conclusion: While higher Cave predicted greater efficacy, consistent positive benefit favouring anifrolumab 300 mg vs placebo was observed in BICLA and SRI(4) responses across Cave subgroups in the TULIP trials.
target: In conclusion, while higher concentration of the drug in blood predicted greater effectiveness, ongoing positive benefits of anifrolumab 300 mg vs placebo is observed across subgroups in the TULIP trials.

source: There was no evidence of exposure-driven safety events.
target: There is no evidence of safety events from exposure to the drug.

source: Objectives: To characterize the relationship of anifrolumab pharmacokinetics with efficacy and safety in patients with moderate to severe SLE despite standard therapy, using pooled data from 2 phase 3 trials.
target: Using data from clinical trials, the study aimed to describe how anifrolumab, an immunosuppressive drug, operates effectively and safely in patients with moderate to severe full-body or systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an inflammatory disease in which immune cells mistakely attack joints, skin, brain cells and more, under standard treatment.

source: Methods: TULIP-1 and TULIP-2 were randomized, placebo-controlled, 52-week trials of intravenous anifrolumab (every 4 weeks for 48 weeks).
target: We analyzed two randomized, 52-week trials, called TULIP-1 and TULIP-2, which involved injected anifrolumab treatment (every 4 weeks for 48 weeks).

source: For the exposure-response analysis, BILAG-based Composite Lupus Assessment (BICLA) or SLE Responder Index (SRI[4]) response rates at week 52 in each quartile/tertile of average anifrolumab serum concentration (Cave) were compared for anifrolumab and placebo in all-comers, patients who completed treatment, and interferon gene signature (IFNGS)-high patients who completed treatment, using average marginal effect logistic regression.
target: To track patient health, we used specific assessments along with measurements of average anifrolumab blood concetration (Cave) to compare anifrolumab-treated patients, sham-treatment patients, patients who completed treatment, and patients with increased immune-specific genetic markers.

source: Relationships between exposure and key safety events were assessed graphically.
target: Trends between health and key safety events were assessed graphically.

source: Results: Of patients in TULIP-1/TULIP-2 who received anifrolumab (150 mg [n = 91], 300 mg [n = 356]) or placebo (n = 366), 574 completed treatment, of whom 470 were IFNGS high.
target: Of patients in TULIP-1/TULIP-2 who got anifrolumab (91 received 150 mg, 356 received 300 mg) or sham treatment (366 patients), 574 completed treatment, of whom 470 had high levels of immune-specific molecules.

source: In the exposure-efficacy analyses, BICLA and SRI(4) treatment differences favoring anifrolumab 300 mg vs placebo were observed across Cave subgroups and all analysis populations.
target: Patient health favored anifrolumab 300 mg vs dummy treatment across all analuzed populations.

source: Logistic regression identified Cave as a significant covariate for predicted BICLA response, as higher anifrolumab Cave predicted greater efficacy.
target: Higher anifrolumab levels were linked to greater treatment success.

source: There was no evidence of exposure-driven incidence of key safety events through week 52 in patients receiving anifrolumab 150 or 300 mg.
target: There was no evidence of treatment-related safety events through week 52 in patients receiving anifrolumab 150 or 300 mg.

source: Conclusion: While higher Cave predicted greater efficacy, consistent positive benefit favouring anifrolumab 300 mg vs placebo was observed in BICLA and SRI(4) responses across Cave subgroups in the TULIP trials.
target: Whil higher anifrolumab levels predicted greater success, consistent positive benefit favouring anifrolumab 300 mg vs dummy treatment was seen across assessments in subgroups of the TULIP trials.

source: There was no evidence of exposure-driven safety events.
target: There was no evidence of treatment-related safety events.

source: Lupus nephritis (LN) is the most frequent severe organ manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
target: Lupus nephritis is the most common form systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus - when your immune system attacks healthy cells) that severely impacts organs.

source: About 30% of patients are refractory to treatment.
target: About 30% of patients do not respond to standard treatment.

source: The authors report a case of treatment of LN with interleukin-17-targeted therapy, demonstrating its possible benefit, after reports of T helper 17 cell involvement in SLE pathogenesis.
target: This report summarizes a case of treating lupus nephritis by targeting a type of protein called interleukin-17, demonstrating its possible benefit.

source: We present the case of a childbearing age woman with SLE, who developed refractory LN despite all the indicated therapeutic options.
target: The case is of a childbearing age woman with lupus who developed lupus nephritis and did not respond to available treatment.

source: During follow up, infection with human papillomavirus was detected, a possible trigger, and the following management was based on this discovery.
target: During follow up visits with the patient, infection with a common sexually transmitted disease called human papillomavirus (HPV) is detected. A possible trigger and the following care and treatment is based on this discovery.

source: We currently know that cytokines play a major role in tissue damage and interleukin-17 (IL-17) seems to be a fundamental key in SLE and LN, having shown its expression in renal glomeruli and urinary sediment.
target: Interleukin-17 (IL-17 - a chemical messenger) seems to be a basic key in lupus and lupus nephritis.

source: Thus, it was decided to start treatment with an anti-IL-17A antibody, secukinumab.
target: Therefore, it is decided to start treatment with an anti-IL-17A antibody drug called secukinumab.

source: After starting secukinumab, clinical and biological features improved and complete renal response was achieved.
target: After starting secukinumab, clinical and biological features improved, and complete kidney response was achieved.

source: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a common autoimmune connective tissue disease with unclear etiology and pathogenesis.
target: Systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus) is a common autoimmune disease where the body's immune system mistakenly attacks healthy tissues. It is unclear how the disease develops.

source: Mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) and MSC derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) play important roles in regulating innate and adaptive immunity, which are involved in many physiological and pathological processes and contribute to the immune homeostasis in SLE.
target: Mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) are cells that are present in adult bone marrow and the umbilical cord, and extracellular vesicles released from MSC cells are particles that carry molecules, proteins and other substances between cells.  These play important roles in regulating natural immunity and immunity acquired after exposure to a virus or to bacteria, which are involved in many disease development processes.

source: The effects of MSCs and EVs on SLE have been drawing more and more attention during the past few years.
target: The effects of MSC cells and extracellular vesicles on lupus have been drawing more and more attention during the past few years.

source: This article reviews the immunomodulatory effects and underlying mechanisms of MSC/MSC-EVs in SLE, which provides novel insight into understanding SLE pathogenesis and guiding the biological therapy.
target: This article reviews the changes in the body's immune system and underlying process of MSC and extracellular vesicles in lupus, which provides new insight into understanding the disease development of lupus and guiding therapy.

source: Objective: To determine the relative effectiveness and safety of belimumab and anifrolumab in patients with active systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
target: The objective of this study is to determine the effectiveness and safety of belimumab and anifrolumab, medications that lower the strength of the body's immune system, in patients with active systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus - when your immune system attacks healthy cells).

source: Materials and methods: A Bayesian network meta-analysis was performed from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) on the effectiveness and safety of belimumab 10 mg, anifrolumab 300 mg, and a placebo in patients with active SLE.
target: An analysis using data from different clinical studies is performed to understand the effectiveness and safety of belimumab, anifrolumab, and a placebo (sham treatment) in patients with active SLE.

source: Results: Six RCTs (2,757 patients) were included in this study.
target: Researchers found 6 relevant clinical studies that together have a total of 2,757 patients.

source: The response rate based on the SLE 4 Responder Index (SRI4) at 52 weeks was substantially higher in the belimumab 10-mg and anifrolumab 300-mg groups than that in the placebo group (OR 2.90, % credible intervals (CrIs) 2.37 - 3.56; OR 1.60, % CrI 1.22 - 2.08).
target: Using an assessment tool to measure how disease reacts to medication and other therapy, the response rate at 52 weeks is much higher in the belimumab and anifrolumab groups than that in the placebo group.

source: The SRI4 response rate for belimumab 10 mg was significantly higher than that for anifrolumab 300 mg (OR 1.82, 95% CrI 1.30 - 2.53).
target: The response rate for belimumab is much higher than the response for anifrolumab.

source: Ranking probability based on surface under the cumulative ranking curve (SUCRA) indicated that belimumab 10 mg was most likely the best treatment for SRI4 response (SUCRA = 0.999), followed by anifrolumab 300 mg (SUCRA = 0.500) and placebo (SUCRA = 0.001).
target: Using data, a ranking system suggested that belimumab is most likely the best treatment for a response rate, followed by anifrolumab, and placebo.

source: The number of serious adverse events (SAEs) did not differ significantly among the 4 treatment groups.
target: The number of serious negative events are not significantly different among the 4 treatment groups.

source: Conclusion: Based on the SRI4 response rate, belimumab 10 mg had the best response efficacy, followed by anifrolumab 10 mg.
target: In conclusion, based on the assessment (measurement) of response rate, belimumab has the best response effectiveness, followed by anifrolumab.

source: However, there was no difference in the number of SAEs among the treatment options.
target: However, there is no difference in the number of serious negative events among the treatment options.

source: Objective: To determine the relative effectiveness and safety of belimumab and anifrolumab in patients with active systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
target: The study aimed to determine the relative success and safety of belimumab and anifrolumab, common immunosuppressive drugs, for patients with full-body or systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an inflammatory disease in which immune cells mistakely attack joints, skin, brain cells and more.

source: Materials and methods: A Bayesian network meta-analysis was performed from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) on the effectiveness and safety of belimumab 10 mg, anifrolumab 300 mg, and a placebo in patients with active SLE.
target: An analysis was performed on randomized controlled trials (RCTs) on the success and safety of belimumab 10 mg, anifrolumab 300 mg, and a dummy treatment in patients with active SLE.

source: Results: Six RCTs (2,757 patients) were included in this study.
target: Six studies (2,757 patients) were analyzed.

source: The response rate based on the SLE 4 Responder Index (SRI4) at 52 weeks was substantially higher in the belimumab 10-mg and anifrolumab 300-mg groups than that in the placebo group (OR 2.90, % credible intervals (CrIs) 2.37 - 3.56; OR 1.60, % CrI 1.22 - 2.08).
target: Patient health at 52 weeks was much higher in the belimumab 10-mg and anifrolumab 300-mg groups than the dummy treatment group.

source: The SRI4 response rate for belimumab 10 mg was significantly higher than that for anifrolumab 300 mg (OR 1.82, 95% CrI 1.30 - 2.53).
target: Patient health for the belimumab 10 mg group was higher than that for the anifrolumab 300 mg group.

source: Ranking probability based on surface under the cumulative ranking curve (SUCRA) indicated that belimumab 10 mg was most likely the best treatment for SRI4 response (SUCRA = 0.999), followed by anifrolumab 300 mg (SUCRA = 0.500) and placebo (SUCRA = 0.001).
target: Based on the analysis, belimumab 10 mg was most likely the best treatment, followed by anifrolumab 300 mg, and the sham treatment.

source: The number of serious adverse events (SAEs) did not differ significantly among the 4 treatment groups.
target: The number of harmful side effects did not differ among the 4 treatment groups.

source: Conclusion: Based on the SRI4 response rate, belimumab 10 mg had the best response efficacy, followed by anifrolumab 10 mg.
target: Based on patient health measures, belimumab 10 mg had the best success, followed by anifrolumab 10 mg.

source: However, there was no difference in the number of SAEs among the treatment options.
target: However, there was no difference in the number of harmful side effects among treatment options.

source: Objective: Randomised trials of type I anti-CD20 antibodies rituximab and ocrelizumab failed to show benefit in proliferative lupus nephritis (LN).
target: Clinical trials studying the infection-fighting antibodies called rituximab and ocrelizumab failed to show benefit in severe cases of lupus nephritis, a kidney disease caused by lupus (when your immune system attacks healthy cells).

source: We compared obinutuzumab, a humanised type II anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that induces potent B-cell depletion, with placebo for the treatment of LN in combination with standard therapies.
target: Obinutuzumab is an antibody that reduces B-cells, a type of protein that produces antibodies but also associated with diseases such as lupus. Researchers compared obinutuzumab with a placebo (inactive substance that looks like a medicine) for the treatment of lupus nephritis in combination with standard therapies.

source: Methods: Patients with LN receiving mycophenolate and corticosteroids were randomised to obinutuzumab 1000 mg or placebo on day 1 and weeks 2, 24 and 26, and followed through week 104.
target: Patients with lupus nephritis receiving standard treatments such as steroids are randomly put in a group to either receive obinutuzumab or a placebo. The drug or placebo are given on day 1 and weeks 2, 24 and 26, and followed through week 104.

source: The primary endpoint was complete renal response (CRR) at week 52.
target: The  main result is complete kidney response at week 52.

source: Exploratory analyses through week 104 were conducted.
target: Additional analyses through week 104 are also done.

source: The prespecified alpha level was 0.2. Results: A total of 125 patients were randomised and received blinded infusions.
target: A total of 125 patients are randomly placed into either the obinutuzumab or the placebo group and did not know which group they are in.

source: Achievement of CRR was greater with obinutuzumab at week 52 (primary endpoint, 22 (35%) vs 14 (23%) with placebo; percentage difference, 12% (95% CI -3.4% to 28%), p=0.115) and at week 104 (26 (41%) vs 14 (23%); percentage difference, 19% (95% CI 2.7% to 35%), p=0.026).
target: Achievement of complete kidney response is greater with obinutuzumab at week 52  and at week 104.

source: Improvements in other renal response measures, serologies, estimated glomerular filtration rate and proteinuria were greater with obinutuzumab.
target: Improvements in other kidney and blood tests are greater with obinutuzumab.

source: Obinutuzumab was not associated with increases in serious adverse events, serious infections or deaths.
target: Obinutuzumab is not associated with increases in serious negative events, serious infections or deaths.

source: Non-serious infusion-related reactions occurred more frequently with obinutuzumab.
target: Reactions related to the infusion (injection) occurred more frequently with obinutuzumab but are not serious reactions.

source: Conclusions: Improved renal responses through week 104 were observed in patients with LN who received obinutuzumab plus standard therapies compared with standard therapies alone.
target: In conclusion, improved kidney responses through week 104 are observed in patients with lupus nephritis who received obinutuzumab plus standard treatment compared with standard treatments alone.

source: Obinutuzumab was well tolerated and no new safety signals were identified.
target: Patients were able to tolerate side effects of obinutuzumab, and no new safety issues were identified.

source: Introduction: Low-dose interleukin-2 (IL-2) selectively restores disturbances of regulatory T cells (Treg) and conventional T cells, resulting in the induction of remission in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus.
target: Interleukin-2 (IL-2) is a type of protein that promotes and develops immune cells at the start of an immune response and keeps them alive. A low-dose of interleukin-2 can restore changes of immune cells called T cells and Treg cells, resulting in the start of remission (recovery) in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus - when your immune system attacks healthy cells).

source: However, to date no research has been carried out on the efficacy of low-dose IL-2 in the treatment of refractory lupus nephritis (LN).
target: However, to date, no research has been carried out on the effectiveness of low-dose IL-2 in the treatment of lupus nephritis, a type of kidney disease caused by lupus.

source: The aim of the study reported here was to investigate the renal response to low-dose IL-2 in patients with refractory LN.
target: The aim of this study is to investigate the kidney response to low-dose IL-2 in patients with lupus nephritis that is not responding to standard treatment.

source: Methods: The study population comprised ten patients with refractory LN who failed to achieve complete response or who had relapsed while being treated with at least two conventional immunosuppressive agents.
target: The study includes 10 patients with lupus nephritis who failed to achieve a  complete response to standard treatment or who improved and then worsened while being treated with at least two standard treatments.

source: One treatment cycle consisted of IL-2 at a dose of 1 million IU administered subcutaneously every other day for 2 weeks followed by a 2-week break.
target: One treatment cycle consists of IL-2 given by an injection every other day for 2 weeks followed by a 2-week break.

source: All patients received three cycles of IL-2 and were then followed up for another 12 weeks without any increase in the dose of previous immunosuppressive agents and steroids.
target: All patients receive three cycles of IL-2 and are then followed up for another 12 weeks without any increase in the dose of the previous treatments or steroids.

source: Results: Of the ten patients enrolled in the study, seven (70%) achieved ≥ 50% improvement in proteinuria at 12 weeks after initiating treatment with IL-2.
target: Of the 10 patients enrolled in the study, 7 (70%) achieved 50% or more improvement in proteinuria (elevated levels of protein in the urine) at 12 weeks after starting IL-2 treatment.

source: Median proteinuria was significantly reduced by 50.3% at week 12, from 1.83 (interquartile range [IQR] 1.23-3.21) g/24 h at baseline to 0.91 (IQR 0.52-1.60) g/24 h at 12 weeks (P = 0.005).
target: Average proteinuria is significantly reduced by 50.3% at week 12.

source: This was accompanied by a 71% reduction in urine erythrocytes, from 64/µl (IQR 24-102/µl) at baseline to 18/µl (IQR 2-20/µl) at 12 weeks (P = 0.018).
target: There is also a 71% decrease in red blood cells in urine at 12 weeks.

source: Anti-ds DNA was decreased from 27.9 (IQR 7.6-40.28) IU/ml at baseline to 14.1 (IQR 7.3-20.12) IU/ml (P = 0.021) at week 12, while complements C3 and C4 were slightly increased (P = 0.445, P = 0.241, respectively).
target: An antibody (infection-fighting molecule) associated with lupus called anti-ds DNA decreased from the start of the study to week 12, while protein complements C3 and C4 used to monitor the immune systems are slightly increased.

source: A significant expansion of Treg cells, from 9.3% at baseline to 16.6% at 12 weeks, was also found (P < 0.05).
target: A significant expansion of the immune Treg cells, from 9.3% at the start of the study to 16.6% at 12 weeks, is also found.

source: No serious adverse events occurred during the treatment period.
target: No serious negative events occurred while patients receive the treatment.

source: Conclusions: Low-dose IL-2 therapy may have a promising role in the treatment of refractory LN as an alternative and safe therapeutic approach.
target: In conclusion, low-dose IL-2 therapy may have a promising role in the treatment of refractory lupus nephritis as an alternative and safe treatment approach.

source: It may be used as multi-target combination therapy in clinical practice.
target: Low-dose IL-2 may be used in combination with other medicines in clinical practice.

source: Significance: Metformin has been proposed as a treatment for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
target: Metformin is a medication that has been proposed as a treatment for systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus - when your immune system attacks healthy cells).

source: The primary target of metformin, the electron transport chain complex I in the mitochondria, is associated with redox homeostasis in immune cells, which plays a critical role in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diseases.
target: Metformin targets certain cells in the immune system that play a critical role in development of autoimmune diseases, which are diseases where the immune system mistakenly attacks healthy cells and tissues.

source: This review addresses the evidence and knowledge gaps on whether a beneficial effect of metformin in lupus may be due to a restoration of a balanced redox state.
target: This review addresses the evidence and knowledge gaps on whether a beneficial effect of metformin in lupus may be due to a restoration of redox homeostasis, a balanced level of reactive oxygen molecules that signal to other cells.

source: Recent Advances: Clinical trials in SLE patients with mild to moderate disease activity and preclinical studies in mice have provided encouraging results for metformin.
target: Clinical trials in lupus patients with mild to moderate disease activity and studies in mice have provided encouraging results for metformin.

source: The mechanism by which this therapeutic effect was achieved is largely unknown.
target: The mechanism that creates this therapeutic effect is largely unknown.

source: Metformin regulates redox homeostasis in a context-specific manner.
target: Metformin regulates redox homeostasis in specific situations.

source: Multiple cell types contribute to SLE, with evidence of increased mitochondrial oxidative stress in T cells and neutrophils.
target: Multiple cell types contribute to lupus.

source: Critical issues: The major knowledge gaps are whether the efficacy of metformin is linked to a restored redox homeostasis in the immune system, and if it does, in which cell types it occurs?
target: The major knowledge gaps are whether the effectiveness of metformin is linked to a restored redox homeostasis in the immune system, and if it is linked, in which type of cells does it occur.

source: We also need to know which patients may have a better response to metformin, and whether it corresponds to a specific mechanism?
target: Researchers also need to know which patients may have a better response to metformin, and whether it relates to a specific mechanism or process in the body.

source: Finally, the identification of biomarkers to predict treatment outcomes would be of great value.
target: Finally, the identification of specific biological factors to predict treatment outcomes would be of great value.

source: Significance: Metformin has been proposed as a treatment for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
target: Metformin, an anti-diabetic medication, may treat full-body or systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an inflammatory disease in which immune cells mistakely attack joints, skin, brain cells and more.

source: The primary target of metformin, the electron transport chain complex I in the mitochondria, is associated with redox homeostasis in immune cells, which plays a critical role in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diseases.
target: The primary target of metformin, a specific energy-generator in the cell, is linked with electrical balance in immune cells, which influence the creation of diseases in which immune cells mistakely attack healthy cells.

source: This review addresses the evidence and knowledge gaps on whether a beneficial effect of metformin in lupus may be due to a restoration of a balanced redox state.
target: This article reviews the evidence and knowledge gaps on whether metformin's beneficial effects in lupus may be due to a restored, balanced electrical state.

source: Recent Advances: Clinical trials in SLE patients with mild to moderate disease activity and preclinical studies in mice have provided encouraging results for metformin.
target: Clinical trials in SLE patients with mild to moderate disease severity and mice studies show encouraging results for metformin.

source: The mechanism by which this therapeutic effect was achieved is largely unknown.
target: How this beneficial effect is achieved is largely unknown.

source: Metformin regulates redox homeostasis in a context-specific manner.
target: Metformin monitors electrical balance depending on the situation.

source: Multiple cell types contribute to SLE, with evidence of increased mitochondrial oxidative stress in T cells and neutrophils.
target: Multiple cell types contribute to SLE, with evidence of increased cellular stress in certain immune cells called T cells and neutrophils.

source: Critical issues: The major knowledge gaps are whether the efficacy of metformin is linked to a restored redox homeostasis in the immune system, and if it does, in which cell types it occurs?
target: The major knowledge gaps are whether the success of metformin is linked to a restored electrical balance in the immune system. If it does, in which cell types it occurs?

source: We also need to know which patients may have a better response to metformin, and whether it corresponds to a specific mechanism?
target: We also need to know which patients may have a better response to metformin, and if it corresponds to a specfic biological process?

source: Finally, the identification of biomarkers to predict treatment outcomes would be of great value.
target: Finally, identifying markers to predict treatment outcomes would be very useful.

source: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic autoimmune disease.
target: Systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus) is a type of chronic autoimmune disease where the body's immune system mistakenly attacks healthy cells and tissues.

source: Although previous studies have demonstrated that SLE is related to the imbalance of cells in the immune system, including B cells, T cells, and dendritic cells, etc., the mechanisms underlying SLE pathogenesis remain unclear.
target: The mechanisms and processes of how lupus develops remain unclear.

source: Therefore, effective and low side-effect therapies for SLE are lacking.
target: Therefore, effective therapies with low side effects for lupus are lacking.

source: Recently, mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapy for autoimmune diseases, particularly SLE, has gained increasing attention.
target: Recently, therapy using mesenchymal stem cells (MSC), cells that are present in adult bone marrow and umbilical cords, for diseases including lupus has gained increasing attention.

source: This therapy can improve the signs and symptoms of refractory SLE by promoting the proliferation of Th2 and Treg cells and inhibiting the activity of Th1, Th17, and B cells, etc.
target: This therapy can improve the signs and symptoms of lupus by promoting the development of Th2 and Treg cells that are involved in activating the immune system and slowing the activity of other cells that are overactive in cases of lupus (Th1, Th17, and B cells, etc.).

source: However, MSC therapy is also reported ineffective in some patients with SLE, which may be related to MSC- or patient-derived factors.
target: However, MSC therapy is also reported ineffective in some patients with lupus, which may be related to the stem cells or patient's factors.

source: Therefore, the therapeutic effects of MSCs should be further confirmed.
target: Therefore, the treatment effects of MSCs should be further confirmed.

source: This review summarizes the status of MSC therapy in refractory SLE treatment and potential reasons for the ineffectiveness of MSC therapy from three perspectives.
target: This review summarizes the status of MSC therapy in lupus treatment and potential reasons for the ineffectiveness of MSC therapy from three perspectives.

source: We propose various MSC modification methods that may be beneficial in enhancing the immunosuppression of MSCs in SLE.
target: Researchers suggest various changes in MSC methods that may be beneficial in slowing the immune effect of MSCs in lupus.

source: However, their safety and protective effects in patients with SLE still need to be confirmed by further experimental and clinical evidence.
target: However, their safety and protective effects in patients with lupus still need to be confirmed by more evidence.

source: As treatment options in advanced systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE) are limited, there is an urgent need for new and effective therapeutic alternatives for selected cases with severe disease.
target: Because treatment options in advanced systematic lupus erythematosus (lupus - when your immune system attacks healthy cells) are limited, there is an urgent need for new and effective treatment alternatives for selected cases with severe disease.

source: Bortezomib (BTZ) is a specific, reversible, inhibitor of the 20S subunit of the proteasome.
target: Bortezomib (BTZ) is a specific, reversible drug that stops the function of the protein complex called proteasome that breaks down unneeded or damaged proteins.

source: Herein, we report clinical experience regarding efficacy and safety from all patients receiving BTZ as therapy for SLE in Sweden during the years 2014-2020.
target: This study reports the effectiveness and safety of all patients receiving BTZ for lupus in Sweden during 2014-2020.

source: 8 females and 4 males were included with a mean disease duration at BTZ initiation of 8.8 years (range 0.7-20 years).
target: Eight females and 4 males are included with an average disease duration of 8.8 years when BZT is started.

source: Renal involvement was the main target for BTZ.
target: Kidney involvement was the main target for BTZ.

source: Reduction of global disease activity was recorded by decreasing SLEDAI-2K scores over time and remained significantly reduced at the 6-month (p=0.007) and the 12-month (p=0.008) follow-up visits.
target: Reduction of global disease activity is recorded by decreasing scores over time of the Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Disease Activity Index (SLEDAI-2K) that tracks disease activity and remained significantly reduced at the 6-month and the 12-month follow-up visits.

source: From BTZ initiation, complement protein 3 (C3) levels increased significantly after the 2nd treatment cycle (p=0.05), the 6-month (p=0.03) and the 12-month (p=0.04) follow-up visits.
target: From BTZ initiation, levels of a protein that supports the immune system called complement protein 3 (C3) increased significantly after the 2nd treatment cycle, the 6-month, and the 12-month follow-up visits.

source: The urine albumin/creatinine ratio declined over time and reached significance at the 6-month (p=0.008) and the 12-month follow-up visits (p=0.004).
target: The urine test showing albumin protein in urine declined over time and reduced significantly at the 6-month and the 12-month follow-up visits.

source: Seroconversion of anti-dsDNA (27%), anti-C1q (50%) and anti-Sm (67%) was observed.
target: The presence of certain antibodies (infecting-fighting molecules) related to lupus is observed.

source: 6 of 12 patients experienced at least one side-effect during follow-up, whereof the most common adverse events were infections.
target: Six out of 12 patients experienced at least one side-effect during follow-up, the most common adverse events are infections.

source: Safety parameters (C-reactive protein, blood cell counts) mainly remained stable over time.
target: Tests to measure safety, including blood cell counts, mainly remained stable over time.

source: To conclude, we report favorable therapeutic effects of BTZ used in combination with corticosteroids in a majority of patients with severe SLE manifestations irresponsive to conventional immunosuppressive agents.
target: In conclusion, researchers found beneficial effects of BTZ when used in combination with corticosteroids (an anti-inflammatory drug also known as steroids) in most patients with severe lupus who do not respond to the standard treatments.

source: Reduction of proteinuria was observed over time as well as seroconversion of some autoantibody specificities.
target: Reduction of proteinuria (elevated protein in urine) was observed over time as well as changes to specific antibodies.

source: In most patients, tolerance was acceptable but mild adverse events was not uncommon.
target: In most patients, the side effects were tolerable but mild adverse events were not uncommon.

source: Special attention should be paid to infections and hypogammaglobinemia.
target: Special attention should be paid to infections and low levels of antibodies.

source: Therapeutic success in treating patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is limited by the multivariate disease etiology, multi-organ presentation, systemic involvement, and complex immunopathogenesis.
target: Successfully treating patients with systematic lupus erythematosus (lupus - when your immune system attacks healthy cells) is limited by the different causes, its presence in multiple organs and systems, and a complex immune response.

source: Agents targeting B-cell differentiation and survival are not efficacious for all patients, indicating a need to target other inflammatory mediators.
target: Treatments that target certain inflammatory (infection-fighting) processes in the body may be needed for some patients.

source: One such target is the type I interferon pathway.
target: One such target is the type I interferon pathway, a specific immune system pathway.

source: Type I interferons upregulate interferon gene signatures and mediate critical antiviral responses.
target: Type I interferons increase the production of interferon genes (genes that allow communication between cells to activate the immune system) and brings about critical immune responses to fight viruses.

source: Dysregulated type I interferon signaling is detectable in many patients with SLE and other autoimmune diseases, and the extent of this dysregulation is associated with disease severity, making type I interferons therapeutically tangible targets.
target: Problems with the signaling in type I interferons are found in patients with lupus, and the extent of the problem is connected to how serious the disease is, making type I interferons possible targets for treatment.

source: The recent approval of the type I interferon-blocking antibody, anifrolumab, by the US Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of patients with SLE demonstrates the value of targeting this pathway.
target: The recent approval of the type I interferon-blocking antibody medicine, anifrolumab, by the US Food and Drug Administration for treating patients with lupus shows the value of targeting this pathway.

source: Nevertheless, the interferon pathway has pleiotropic biology, with multiple cellular targets and signaling components that are incompletely understood.
target: Nevertheless, the interferon pathway has multiple cells and signaling parts that are not completely understood.

source: Deconvoluting the complexity of the type I interferon pathway and its intersection with lupus disease pathology will be valuable for further development of targeted SLE therapeutics.
target: Understanding the type I interferon pathway and how it relates to lupus will be valuable for future development of lupus treatments.

source: This review summarizes the immune mediators of the interferon pathway, its association with disease pathogenesis, and therapeutic modalities targeting the dysregulated interferon pathway.
target: This review summarizes the immune process of the interferon pathway, its association with disease development, and therapeutic methods that target problems interferon pathway.

source: Therapeutic success in treating patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is limited by the multivariate disease etiology, multi-organ presentation, systemic involvement, and complex immunopathogenesis.
target: Treatment success for patients with full-body or systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an inflammatory disease in which immune cells mistakely attack joints, skin, brain cells and more, is limited by muti-factor disease causes, multi-organ damage, full-body effect, and complex causes for immune cell dysfunction.

source: Agents targeting B-cell differentiation and survival are not efficacious for all patients, indicating a need to target other inflammatory mediators.
target: Treatment targeting creation and survival of antibody-creating immune cells are not effective for all patients, indicating a need to target other inflammatory groups.

source: One such target is the type I interferon pathway.
target: One target group is the type I interferon pathway, a cellular signaling pathway that affects the entire immune system.

source: Type I interferons upregulate interferon gene signatures and mediate critical antiviral responses.
target: Type I interferons increase the amount of certain signaling proteins and influence important antiviral responses.

source: Dysregulated type I interferon signaling is detectable in many patients with SLE and other autoimmune diseases, and the extent of this dysregulation is associated with disease severity, making type I interferons therapeutically tangible targets.
target: Impaired type I interferon signaling is detectable in many with SLE and similar diseases. The extent of this impairment is linked with disease severity, making type I interferons a possible treatment target.

source: The recent approval of the type I interferon-blocking antibody, anifrolumab, by the US Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of patients with SLE demonstrates the value of targeting this pathway.
target: The recent approval of the type I interferon blocking molcule or antibody, anifrolumab, by a government agency for treating SLE demonstrates the value of targeting this pathway.

source: Nevertheless, the interferon pathway has pleiotropic biology, with multiple cellular targets and signaling components that are incompletely understood.
target: Still, the interferon pathway affects multiple targets and signaling pathways that are incompletely understood.

source: Deconvoluting the complexity of the type I interferon pathway and its intersection with lupus disease pathology will be valuable for further development of targeted SLE therapeutics.
target: Understanding the complex type I interferon pathway and its influence on lupus disease will be valuable for further development of targeted SLE treatments.

source: This review summarizes the immune mediators of the interferon pathway, its association with disease pathogenesis, and therapeutic modalities targeting the dysregulated interferon pathway.
target: This review summarizes the immune middlemen of the interferon pathway, its link with disease creation, and treatments targeting the pathway when it is impaired.

source: Edema is an accumulation of fluid in the interstitial space that occurs as the capillary filtration exceeds the limits of lymphatic drainage, producing noticeable clinical signs and symptoms.
target: Edema is a build up of fluid in the body's tissues and does not drain properly, producing noticeable clinical signs and symptoms.

source: The rapid development of generalized pitting edema associated with systemic disease requires timely diagnosis and management.
target: The rapid development of pitting edema, which is when pressing into the swollen area leaves a pit or indentation, associated with diseases that affects other parts of the body requires timely diagnosis and management.

source: The chronic accumulation of edema in one or both lower extremities often indicates venous insufficiency, especially in the presence of dependent edema and hemosiderin deposition.
target: The ongoing build up of edema in one or both lower legs often suggests veins in the lower limbs are not allowing enough blood to flow to the heart, especially in the presence of gravity-related swelling and build up of iron deposits under the skin that has a shade of brown pigment.

source: Skin care is crucial in preventing skin breakdown and venous ulcers.
target: Skin care is crucial in preventing skin breakdown and venous ulcers, open sores on the skin from abnormal vein function.

source: Eczematous (stasis) dermatitis can be managed with emollients and topical steroid creams.
target: Inflamed skin in the lower legs called eczematous (stasis) dermatitis is a kind of eczema (skin swelling) caused by poor circulation and can be helped with moisturizers and topical steroid creams.

source: Patients who have had deep venous thrombosis should wear compression stockings to prevent postthrombotic syndrome.
target: Patients who have had a condition where a blood clot forms in the vein called deep venous thrombosis should wear compression stockings.

source: If clinical suspicion for deep venous thrombosis remains high after negative results are noted on duplex ultrasonography, further investigation may include magnetic resonance venography to rule out pelvic or thigh proximal venous thrombosis or compression.
target: If a doctor believes a diagnosis for deep venous thrombosis is possible even after negative (no detection) results are noted on tests, further investigation may include imaging scans to rule out pelvic or thigh proximal venous thrombosis or compression.

source: Obstructive sleep apnea may cause bilateral leg edema even in the absence of pulmonary hypertension.
target: Occasional airflow blockage during sleep called obstructive sleep apnea may cause edema in both legs even in the absence of high blood pressure.

source: Brawny, nonpitting skin with edema characterizes lymphedema, which can present in one or both lower extremities.
target: Discolored, non-pitting (non-indentable) skin with edema is often a sign of lymphedema, fluid build-up in soft tissues when the lymph system is damaged or blocked, which can present in one or both lower limbs.

source: Possible secondary causes of lymphedema include tumor, trauma, previous pelvic surgery, inguinal lymphadenectomy, and previous radiation therapy.
target: Possible other causes of lymphedema include tumor, trauma, previous pelvic surgery, inguinal lymphadenectomy (lymph node dissection), and previous radiation therapy.

source: Use of pneumatic compression devices or compression stockings may be helpful in these cases.
target: Use of compression devices that are inflatable sleeves worn on the legs or compression stockings may be helpful in these cases.

source: Edema is an accumulation of fluid in the interstitial space that occurs as the capillary filtration exceeds the limits of lymphatic drainage, producing noticeable clinical signs and symptoms.
target: Edema is a buildup of fluid in the body that occurs as the filtration rate exceeds the drainage rate, producing noticeable symptoms.

source: The rapid development of generalized pitting edema associated with systemic disease requires timely diagnosis and management.
target: The rapid development of swelling linked with full-body disease needs timely identification and treatment.

source: The chronic accumulation of edema in one or both lower extremities often indicates venous insufficiency, especially in the presence of dependent edema and hemosiderin deposition.
target: The long-lasting buildup of fluid in one or both legs often indicates blood vessel impairment, especially in the presence of swelling in the limbs and iron buildup.

source: Skin care is crucial in preventing skin breakdown and venous ulcers.
target: Skin care is needed in preventing skin breakdown and vein-related open sores.

source: Eczematous (stasis) dermatitis can be managed with emollients and topical steroid creams.
target: Skin inflammation from swelling in the legs can be managed with specific moisturizers and steroid creams.

source: Patients who have had deep venous thrombosis should wear compression stockings to prevent postthrombotic syndrome.
target: Patients who have had deep venous thrombosis (deep vein blood clotting) should wear compression socks to prevent its symptoms like pain and swelling.

source: If clinical suspicion for deep venous thrombosis remains high after negative results are noted on duplex ultrasonography, further investigation may include magnetic resonance venography to rule out pelvic or thigh proximal venous thrombosis or compression.
target: If clinicians remain suspicious of deep venous thrombosis even when lab tests do not detect it, further lab tests may be performed to rule out other forms of blood clotting and compression.

source: Obstructive sleep apnea may cause bilateral leg edema even in the absence of pulmonary hypertension.
target: Obstructuve sleep apnea (disorder in which breathing is consistently interrupted during sleep by a blockage) may cause swelling in both legs even without lung-related high blood pressure.

source: Brawny, nonpitting skin with edema characterizes lymphedema, which can present in one or both lower extremities.
target: Sturdy skin with swelling characterizes lymphedema (fluid buildup when the body's drainage system is blocked), which can present in one or both legs.
