target: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is a rare disorder caused by changes in the gene called GLI3.

source: Individuals with PHS present with a variably penetrant variety of urogenital system malformations, including renal aplasia or hypoplasia, hydroureter, hydronephrosis or a common urogenital sinus.
target: Patients with PHS have a variety of problems with reproduction and urinary organs, including poor development of the kidneys, backup of urine in the duct between the kidney and the bladder, swelling of the kidneys, or a shared opening for the birth canal  and the urinary tract.

source: The embryologic mechanisms controlled by GLI3R that result in these pathologic phenotypes are undefined.
target: It is not known how the specific problems arise

source: We demonstrate that germline expression of GLI3R causes renal hypoplasia, associated with decreased nephron number, and hydroureter and hydronephrosis, caused by blind-ending ureters.
target: We show that variations in the GLI3 gene cause poor development of the kidneys, and a closed end in the duct that runs between the kidney and the bladder, which causes swelling of the kidneys and the duct.

source: Mice with obligate GLI3R expression also displayed duplication of the ureters that was caused by aberrant common nephric duct patterning and ureteric stalk outgrowth.
target: Mice with the same change in the GLI3 gene also had two ducts that connected a kidney to the bladder

source: These developmental abnormalities are associated with suppressed Hedgehog signaling activity in the cloaca and adjacent vesicular mesenchyme.
target: The abnormal development is caused by the lack of information the embryonic cells (cells that forms when eggs are fertilized)  need for proper development.

source: Using mutant mice with Gli3R and Gli3 null alleles, we demonstrate that urogenital system patterning and development is controlled by the levels of GLI3R and not by an absence of full-length GLI3.
target: Using mutant mice, we show that the development of the reproduction and urinary organs  is controlled by the variants in the GLI3 gene.

source: We conclude that the urogenital system phenotypes observed in PHS are caused by GLI3R-dependent perturbations in nephric duct patterning, renal branching morphogenesis and nephrogenic progenitor self-renewal.
target: Different problems in PHS are caused by the different mistakes in the development of the urinary organs due to the information provided by the variants of the GLI3 gene.

source: Pallister-Hall syndrome was initially recognized under fairly unique circumstances involving exhumation of the very first case.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome was first identified in unearthed human remains.

source: The first two cases had dramatic and unusual features including a hypothalamic hamartoblastoma, imperforate anus, an unusual type of polydactyly with the extra digit being central, hypopituitarism with secondary hypoadrenalism, and lethality after birth (probably due to hypoadrenalism).
target: The first two bodies had unusual features including a brain mass, missing anus, a sixth finger in the center, and death after birth.

source: As the full spectrum and variability of anomalies was recognized, it became clear that it was not such a rare disorder.
target: As more cases were found, it became clear that PHS is not such a rare disorder.

source: Shortly after familial cases were recognized, the responsible gene was identified at GLI3.
target: Shortly after, it was recognized that the disease runs in the families due to changes in the gene called GLI3.

source: However, since other different conditions also involved GLI3, elaborating the domains of the gene and the types of mutations needed to be defined in order to have a clear correlation of the genotype-phenotype relations.
target: Other problems can be caused by changes in the same gene GLI3. Different changes in the different parts of the gene cause different problems.

source: Pallister Hall syndrome is autosomal dominant disorder usually diagnosed in infants and children.
target: Pallister Hall syndrome is passed down by one of the parents. It is usually diagnosed in infants and children.

source: Current diagnostic criteria include presence of hypothalamic hamartoma, post axial polydactyly and positive family history, but the disease has variable manifestations.
target: It is diagnosed if it runs in the family, and the children have hypothalamic hamartoma (a non-cancer growth of tissues in the brain), and extra fingers or toes, but these are not the only signs of the disease.

source: Herein we report Pallister Hall syndrome diagnosed in a family where both patients were adults.
target: We diagnosed Pallister Hall syndrome in a family where both patients were adults.

source: A 59 year old man developed seizures 4 years prior to our evaluation of him, at which time imaging showed a hypothalamic hamartoma.
target: A 59 year old man developed seizures 4 years before we examined him. An imaging examination showed a hypothalamic hamartoma.

source: The seizures were controlled medically.
target: The seizures were under control.

source: He did well until he had visual changes after a traumatic head injury.
target: He did well until he had changes in eyesight after a head injury.

source: Repeat MRI showed slight expansion of the mass with formal visual field testing demonstrating bitemporal hemianopsia.
target: MRI showed the mass has expanded slightly and the patient's vision was missing in the outer half of both the right and left eye.

source: There was no evidence of pituitary dysfunction except for large urine volume.
target: There were no signs of problems with the hormones made in the pituitary gland (which maintain body functions), except for large amount of urine.

source: He underwent surgery to debulk the hamartoma and the visual field defects improved.
target: The patient had surgery to reduce the hamartoma and his vision improved.

source: There was no hypopituitarism post-operatively, and the polydyspia resolved.
target: After the surgery, the pituitary gland functioned normally, and the patient no longer was excessively thirsty.

source: His 29 year old daughter also had seizures and hypothalamic hamartoma.
target: His 29 year old daughter also had seizures and hypothalamic hamartoma.

source: Both patients had had polydactyly with prior surgical correction in childhood.
target: Both patients had surgery to remove the extra fingers in childhood.

source: The daughter underwent genetic testing, which revealed a previously undescribed heterozygous single base pair deletion in exon 13 of the GLI3 gene causing a frameshift mutation.
target: The daughter had genetic testing that showed she had a variant that caused the disease.

source: Further investigation into family history revealed multiple members in previous generations with polydactyly and/or seizures.
target: Many family members in previous generations had extra fingers and seizures.

source: Pallister-Hall syndrome is caused by an inherited autosomal dominant or de novo mutation in GLI3 gene.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome is caused by either parent passing down the disease or a new harmful change in the genes.

source: This rare syndrome has not had prevalence defined, however.
target: It is not known how many people have this rare disease.

source: Generally, diagnoses are made in the pediatric population.
target: Generally, the disease is recognized in children.

source: Our report adds to the few cases detected in adulthood.
target: We described some of the few cases detected in adults.

source: Polydactyly is a relatively common abnormality in infants.
target: Extra fingers or toes are relatively common in newborns.

source: However, it can be a marker of a wide variety of neurological and systemic abnormality.
target: It can be a sign of different problems with the nervous and other systems.

source: Hence, it is important for pediatrician and physician to have insight into the various association of this apparently innocuous anomaly.
target: Doctors need to know about the conditions that cause extra fingers and toes.

source: In this write-up, we report an extremely rare syndrome associated with polydactyly that is Pallister-Hall syndrome.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome is a rare condition associated with having extra fingers and toes.

source: A 10-month-old male child born by lower segment cesarean section presented with global delay associated with microcephaly, frontal bossing, hypertelorism, flat nose, short philtrum, incomplete cleft in the upper lip and hard palate, polydactyly, and syndactyly.
target: A 10-month-old male child had a small head, unusually prominent forehead, increased distance between the eyes, flat nose, a shorter than normal distance between the upper lip and the nose, incomplete cleft (slit) in the upper lip and the roof of the mouth, extra fingers, and fused fingers.

source: The child presented with repeated vomiting and crying episodes.
target: The child vomited and cried repeatedly.

source: The patient was investigated which revealed a hypothalamic hamartomas.
target: An examination showed the child had hypothalamic hamartomas, a non-cancer growth of tissue in the brain.

source: Pallister-Hall syndrome is a very rare autosomal dominant genetic disorder due to mutation in GLI3 gene in the short arm of chromosome 7 with variable penetrance and expressivity.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome is a rare disorder passed down by one of the parents. It ranges from mild to severe.

source: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is an extremely rare syndrome of unknown prevalence with autosomal dominant inheritance due to GLI3 gene mutations classically characterized by the presence of a hypothalamic hamartoma and polydactyly.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is a rare disease. It is not known how many people have the disease. It is caused by harmful changes in the genes that are passed down by either parent. The disease is characterized by the presence of a hypothalamic hamartoma (a non-cancer growth in the brain) and extra fingers or toes.

source: Additional diagnostic criteria include bifid epiglottis, imperforate anus, small nails, hypopituitarism, growth hormone deficiency, and genital hypoplasia.
target: Other signs include a split in the flap that protects the windpipe, missing or blocked opening to the anus, small nails, reduced function of the pituitary gland, which controls body functions and growth through chemical substances called hormones, dwarfism, and poor development of the reproductive organs.

source: It is typically diagnosed in infancy and early childhood, presenting with seizures and/or precocious puberty due to the hypothalamic hamartoma, and with limb anomalies due to central polydactyly.
target: It is usually recognized in infancy and early childhood by the presence of seizures, signs of the child's body changing to that of an adult too soon, and extra fingers or toes.

source: Our patient had presented with polysyndactyly at birth.
target: Our patient had extra and fused fingers at birth.

source: However, as this is not uncommon in infants and is usually as part of the sporadic, isolated form of polydactyly, no further work up was done.
target: Because extra fingers are common and may have different causes, no tests were done.

source: He then presented at age 16 years with a headache and subjective visual changes, with brain imaging revealing a hypothalamic hamartoma.
target: At the age of 16 he started having headaches and changes in vision. Brain imaging showed a hypothalamic hamartoma, a non-cancer growth in the brain tissues.

source: He did not have a history of seizures or central precocious puberty.
target: He did not have seizures or premature puberty.

source: Genotyping revealed a pathogenic variant affecting the GLI3 gene.
target: Genetic testing showed harmful variants.

source: We encourage all clinicians to consider PHS or an associated syndrome with a clinical finding of polydactyly.
target: Clinicians should think about PHS when they see extra fingers and toes.

source: Further, as the natural history continues to reveal itself, this patient's presentation provides important new data to the broad phenotypic spectrum of PHS.
target: The disease signs in this patient add new knowledge about PHS.

source: Introduction: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is a rare autosomal dominant syndrome characterized by polydactyly, bifid or shortened epiglottis, visceral anomalies, hypothalamic hamartoma often combined with hypopituitarism.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is a rare disease passed down from one of the parents. Its signs are six fingers and toes, shortened or split flap that protects the windpipe, problems with the organs in the chest and belly, non-cancer mass in the brain that often causes problems with pituitary gland, which regulates body functions and growth.

source: PHS is characterized by significant variability in the expression of clinical symptoms.
target: Signs of PHS in different patients are very different.

source: The clinical course ranges from mild with a good prognosis to severe and which can lead to death during the neonatal period.
target: PHS ranges from mild with good outcomes to severe that can lead to death of the newborns.

source: Case report: Two-years-old girl with facial dysmorphia, skeletal malformations of hand and feet and growth hormone deficiency.
target: We describe a two-year-old girl with deformities of the face, hand and feet and dwarfism.

source: PHS was diagnosed on the basis of the presented symptoms and genetic tests.
target: PHS was diagnosed on the basis of the presented symptoms and genetic tests.

source: Summary: Skeletal malformations, such as polydactyly or oligodactyly, are a markers which can be associated with endocrinological disorders.
target: Abnormalities, such as the presence of extra or fewer than five fingers or toes, are associated with disorders of the endocrine system, which includes the glands that regulate body functions through chemicals called hormones.

source: Quick and correct diagnosis would help in planning treatment during childhood and giving family counseling, including prenatal advice regarding the next pregnancy of the child's mother.
target: Quick and correct diagnosis would help in planning treatment for the child and advise the family regarding the next pregnancy of the child's mother.

source: Traumatic injury to the peripheral nerves often results in persistent discomfort.
target: After peripheral nerves (the parts of the nervous system outside the brain and spinal cord) are injured, people often feel discomfort for a long time.

source: Substance P has been implicated as a mediator of pain, and depletion of this neurotransmitter has been shown to reduce pain.
target: A chemical called Substance P is involved in the process of feeling pain. Lowering the amount of this chemical involved in the transmission of nerve signals lessens pain.

source: Subjects suffering from traumatic dysesthesia of the trigeminal nerve were treated with capsaicin, a substance P depleter with significant long-term effects.
target: Capsaicin was a good long-term treatment for people who experienced an unpleasant abnormal sensation after injury to the trigeminal nerve. Trigeminal nerve is a large, three-part nerve in the head that provides sensation.  Capsaicin is a chemical that lowers the amount of Substance P  in the body.

source: This form of therapy may be used individually or in combination with other pharmacologic interventions in the treatment of traumatic trigeminal dysesthesia.
target: Drugs that lower the amount of Substance P  in the body may be used alone or together with other medications to treat traumatic trigeminal dysesthesia (an unpleasant abnormal sensation after injury to the trigeminal nerve.)

source: Objective: To report a novel wireless neuromodulation system for treatment of refractory craniofacial pain.
target: This study presents a new wireless nerve stimulation system for treatment of uncontrollable pain of the head and face.

source: Background: Previous studies utilizing peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) of the occipital and trigeminal nerves reported positive outcomes for alleviating neuropathic pain localized to the craniofacial and occipital areas.
target: Previous studies reported that stimulation of the nerves called trigeminal (meaning "threefold", for the three branches of the nerve)  and occipital (relating to the back of the head) reduces pain in the head and face.

source: However several technological limitations and cosmetic concerns inhibited a more widespread acceptance and use of neuromodulation.
target: Due to the shortcomings of the devices and concerns about the appearance, the nerve stimulation systems are not used widely.

source: Also, a relatively high incidence of adverse events like electrode erosions, dislocation, wire fracture and/or infection at the surgical site mandates a change in our approach to neuromodulation technology and implant techniques in the craniofacial region.
target: The relatively common complications are worn and broken wires, devices moving from the original placement, and infection around the device. To avoid these complications, we need to change the nerve stimulation systems and the way they are implanted (placed) in the head and face.

source: Methods: We report a novel approach for the management of craniofacial pain with a wirelessly powered, minimally invasive PNS system.
target: We describe a new peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) system for treatment of pain in the head and neck. The system is wireless and is placed using small cuts and few stitches.

source: The system is percutaneously implanted and placed subcutaneously adjacent to affected facial nerves via visual guidance by the clinician.
target: The doctors place the system under the skin, next to the damaged facial nerves

source: In this feasibility study, pilot evidence was gathered in a cohort of ten subjects suffering from a combination of chronic headaches, facial pain for at least 15 days per month and for at least 4h/day.
target: This study included ten patients who experienced chronic headaches and facial pain for four or more hours a day and at least 15 days a month.

source: Results: At four weeks post-implant follow up, all patients reported sustained pain relief of the primary pain area.
target: Four weeks after the systems were implanted, all patients felt continuing ain relief.

source: Electrode location and total number of electrodes used per subject varied across the cohort.
target: The patients had different numbers of wires and implant locations.

source: The average pain reduction using the visual analog scale was ≥82%.
target: On average, patients felt five times less pain after treatment.

source: The procedure had no adverse events or side effects.
target: There were no complications

source: Conclusion: Percutaneous placement of a wireless neurostimulation device directly adjacent to affected craniofacial nerve(s) is a minimally invasive and reversible method of pain control in patients with craniofacial pain refractory to conventional medical managements.
target: Placing a wireless nerve stimulation device under the skin next to the damaged nerves in the head and neck reduces pain that is hard to control otherwise. This treatment is gentle and can be reversed.

source: Trigeminal neuralgia is caused by compression of trigeminal nerve root and it leads to demyelination gradually.
target: Trigeminal nerve pain (neuralgia) is caused by pressure on the trigeminal (meaning "threefold", for the three branches of the nerve)  nerve. The pressure leads to gradual loss of the protecting layer that covers the nerve.

source: It was almost idiopathic and occurred unexpected.
target: It arises spontaneously and unexpected.

source: The upper cervical spinal cord contains the spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus.
target: The spinal cord is the bundle of nerve tissues that connects the brain with the body. In the upper neck region, it contains parts of the trigeminal nerve.

source: Fibers with cell bodies in the trigeminal ganglion enter in the upper pons and descend caudally to C2 level.
target: The trigeminal nerve cells and fibers enter the brainstem, the bottom part of the brain connected to the spinal cord, and go down to the second vertebrae at the top of the cervical spine.

source: We experienced a rare patient with facial pain, which was paroxysmal attack with severe pain after a clear event, cervical spinal injury (C2).
target: A rare patient had severe repeated bouts of facial pain after an injury to the second vertebrae at the top of the neck region.

source: So, this case reminds us of a possible cause of trigeminal neuralgia after a trauma of the head and neck.
target: This patient reminds us that trigeminal neuralgia may be caused by injuries to the head and neck.

source: Orofacial pain syndromes encompass several clinically defined and classified entities.
target: Orofacial pain syndromes are diseases known to cause pain in the face and mouth.

source: The focus here is on the role of clinical neurophysiologic and psychophysical tests in the diagnosis, differential diagnosis, and pathophysiological mechanisms of definite trigeminal neuropathic pain and other chronic orofacial pain conditions (excluding headache and temporomandibular disorders).
target: We discuss clinical examinations of the neural system that help identifying trigeminal nerve pain and other chronic facial pain conditions, but not headaches and problems with TMJ.

source: The International Classification of Headache Disorders 2018 classifies these facial pain disorders under the heading Painful cranial neuropathies and other facial pains.
target: The International Classification of Headache Disorders 2018 classifies these facial pain disorders under the heading Painful cranial neuropathies and other facial pains.

source: In addition to unambiguous painful posttraumatic or postherpetic trigeminal neuropathies, burning mouth syndrome, persistent idiopathic facial and dental pain, and trigeminal neuralgia have also been identified with neurophysiologic and quantitative sensory testing to involve the nervous system.
target: Clinical examinations of the nervous system help identifying painful conditions of the trigeminal nerve, which carries sensation from the face to the brain. These conditions may start after an injury (posttraumatic trigeminal neuropathy) or herpes infection (postherpetic trigeminal neuropathy). Other related conditions are burning mouth syndrome (a burning, scalding, or tingling feeling in the mouth) and persistent idiopathic facial and dental pain, which is described as continuing pain in the face and teeth of unknown cause.

source: Despite normal clinical examination, these all include clusters of patients with evidence for either peripheral or central nervous system pathology compatible with the subclinical end of a continuum of trigeminal neuropathic pain conditions.
target: Even patients who appear normal on clinical examination have damage to their nervous system that can cause trigeminal nerve pain.

source: Useful tests in the diagnostic process include electroneuromyography with specific needle, neurography techniques for the inferior alveolar and infraorbital nerves, brain stem reflex recordings (blink reflex with stimulation of the supraorbital, infraorbital, mental, and lingual nerves; jaw jerk; masseter silent period), evoked potential recordings, and quantitative sensory testing.
target: Diagnostic tests include recording electrical signals in the muscles (electroneuromyography), MRI of the nerves around the eyes, blink reflexes and jaw jerk when different nerves are stimulated, recording electrical signals after the nerves are stimulated, and tests for pain sensation.

source: Habituation of the blink reflex and evoked potential responses to repeated stimuli evaluate top-down inhibition, and navigated transcranial magnetic stimulation allows the mapping of reorganization within the motor cortex in chronic neuropathic pain.
target: Responsiveness of the blink reflex and records of the electrical signals after repeated nerve stimulation show if the response is suppressed. Medical imaging called navigated transcranial magnetic stimulation shows the areas of the brain that change due to chronic nerve pain.

source: With systematic use of neurophysiologic and quantitative sensory testing, many of the current ambiguities in the diagnosis, classification, and understanding of chronic orofacial syndromes can be clarified for clinical practice and future research.
target: Consistent use of the above tests will help understanding the chronic facial pain conditions.

source: Introduction: Trigeminal neuralgia is an exemplary neuropathic pain condition characterized by paroxysmal electric-shock-like pain.
target: Neuralgia is pain in the nerves that feels as bursts of electric-shock-like pain.

source: However, up to 50% of patients also experiences concomitant continuous pain.
target: Up tp half of the patients also experience continuous pain.

source: In this neuroimaging study, we aimed to identify the specific anatomical features of trigeminal nerve root in patients with concomitant continuous pain.
target: We study the specific anatomical features of trigeminal nerve root (a part of the nerve that helps the face recognize pain, touch, heat and cold sensations, as well as chewing. We studied patients with continuous pain.

source: Methods: We enrolled 73 patients with a definitive diagnosis of classical and idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia and 40 healthy participants.
target: We studied 73 patients with trigeminal neuralgia (nerve pain) and 40 healthy participants.

source: The diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia was independently confirmed by two clinicians.
target: The diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia was independently confirmed by two clinicians.

source: Patients were grouped as patients with purely paroxysmal pain (45 patients) and patients also with concomitant continuous pain (28 patients).
target: 45 patients had only Burts of pain and 28 patients had continuous pain along with the bursts.

source: All participants underwent a structured clinical examination and a 3T MRI with sequences dedicated to the anatomical study of the trigeminal nerve root, including volumetric study.
target: All patients had clinical examination and MRI.

source: Images analysis was independently performed by two investigators, blinded to any clinical data.
target: Two specialists studied the images without losing at the clinical data.

source: Results: In most patients with concomitant continuous pain, this type of pain, described as burning, throbbing or aching, manifested at the disease onset.
target: In most patients with continuous pain it started as burning, throbbing or aching sensation.

source: Demographic and clinical variables did not differ between the two groups of patients; the frequency of neurovascular compression and nerve dislocation were similar.
target: The patients in the two groups and the frequency and location of the nerve damage signs in these patients were similar

source: Conversely, trigeminal nerve root atrophy was more severe in patients with concomitant continuous pain than in those with purely paroxysmal pain (p = 0.006).
target: On the contrary, patients with both the bursts and continuous pain had more atrophy ((decrease in size) of the trigeminal nerve than the patients with bursts only.

source: Conclusions: Our clinical and neuroimaging study found that in patients with trigeminal neuralgia, concomitant continuous pain was associated with trigeminal nerve root atrophy, therefore suggesting that this type of pain is likely related to axonal loss and abnormal activity in denervated trigeminal second-order neurons.
target: Clinical examination of patients with trigeminal nerve pain and their medical images show that patients who experienced continuous pain along with pain bursts lost some nerve tissues. This type of pain is related to wrong signaling in the nerve that lost tissues.

source: Introduction: The complex nature of facial pain conditions creates a diagnostic challenge which may necessitate specialist referral.
target: Facial pain may be hard to recognize and a specialist might need to examine the patient.

source: Aim: To identify the case mix presenting to a specialist tertiary care facial pain clinic.
target: The study's aim was to identify the mix of patient cases at a specialist facial pain clinic.

source: Methods: A retrospective review of 112 patient records was undertaken.
target: We reviewed the records of 112 patients who visited the specialists at the facial pain clinic.

source: Trends in provisional diagnoses from referrers and the correlation to diagnoses made following specialist consultation were reviewed.
target: We compared the reasons for specialist consultation  given by the primary doctors to the diagnoses made by the specialists.

source: Results: The most common provisional diagnoses recorded in referral letters were painful temporomandibular disorders, trigeminal neuralgia and persistent idiopathic facial pain (PIFP).
target: Most often the primary doctors thought the patients' pain was caused by problems with the  temporomandibular joints that connect the lower jaw to the skull; pain in the trigeminal nerve that sends signals about sensations in the face to the brain; and other facial pain.

source: Over a quarter of referrals did not include a provisional diagnosis.
target: Over a quarter of patients referred to the cliniic had no diagnosis.

source: Following assessment, only one case was not given a definitive diagnosis and no patients were diagnosed with PIFP.
target: The specialist could not diagnose only one patient and have not diagnosed any patients with unspecified facial pain.

source: A causative factor was identified in all the initially queried PIFP cases, and painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain was found in multiple patients.
target: The specialists found causes of all previously unspecified facial pain. Many patients had trigeminal nerve pain following an injury (Painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain ).

source: Conclusions: Painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain should be considered if pain onset coincides with dental treatment or other traumatic events.
target: Painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain should be considered if pain started after dental treatment or injury.

source: PIFP is a rare facial pain diagnosis and may be over-diagnosed by dental and medical practitioners.
target: Unspecified facial pain is rare and may be overused by dentists and physicians.

source: It is important to systematically exclude other causes before reaching this diagnosis.
target: All other causes should be excluded before diagnosis of the unspecified facial pain.

source: This will facilitate effective treatment, manage patient expectations and potentially reduce unnecessary referrals.
target: This will improve treatment and patients' expectations. It may reduce unnecessary referrals.

source: Background: Neuropathic facial pain occurs due to pathologic dysfunctions of a nerve responsible for mediating sensory fibers to the head.
target: Neural facial pain is caused by the nerves that transmit sensation to the head.

source: Surgical interventions, in cases of failed medical therapy, include microvascular decompression, radiofrequency (RF) ablation, percutaneous balloon decompression, and stereotactic radiosurgery.
target: If medicines do not help, surgery may be used. Surgeries relieve compressed nerves (microvascular decompression); or shrink tissues using radio waves (radiofrequency (RF) ablation) or local radiation (stereotactic radiosurgery). Other surgeries prevent the nerve from transmitting signals (percutaneous balloon decompression.)

source: In this review, we focused on RF ablation as a treatment for chronic facial pain.
target: We discuss using radio waves (RF ablation) to treat chronic facial pain.

source: Objectives: The objective of this review was to summarize available evidence behind RF ablation for facial pain, including pain outcome measures, secondary outcomes, and complications.
target: We summarize what is known about RF ablation for facial pain, including treatment results and complications.

source: Results: We reviewed 44 studies and assessed their short- and long-term pain relief measurements, as well as secondary outcomes including patient satisfaction, quality of life improvements, decrease in oral medication use, and recurrence rates.
target: We summarize the pain relief results, patient satisfaction, quality of life, decrease in the drug use, and how often the pain returns.

source: Maximal pain relief was achieved in treatment groups using combined continuous radiofrequency (CRF) and pulsed radiofrequency (PRF) therapies, followed by CRF therapy alone and finally PRF therapy alone.
target: Combining two different types of radio wave treatments (continuous radiofrequency (CRF) and pulsed radiofrequency (PRF))reduced pain the best.  CRF treatment was second best, followed by PRF.

source: All treatment regimens improved secondary outcomes.
target: All treatments improved patient satisfaction and quality of life, and decreased drug use and return of the pain.

source: Common complications of treatment included facial numbness, masseter weakness, cheek hematomas, diminished corneal reflex, and dry eyes.
target: Common complications of the treatment included facial numbness,  weakness of facial muscles, bruised cheeks, failure to blink when the eye is touched, and dry eyes.

source: Conclusions: This systematic review found evidence that RF ablation is efficient in treating patients with facial pain, as well as in improving quality of life and reducing oral medication use.
target: We conclude that radio wave treatment (RF ablation) is efficient in treating patients with facial pain. It also improves quality of life and reduces drug use.

source: Maximal pain control is achieved using combined CRF and PRF therapy.
target: Maximal pain control is achieved using combined CRF and PRF therapy.

source: Complications are uncommon and include facial numbness, masseter weakness, cheek hematomas, diminished corneal reflex, and dry eyes.
target: Complications of the treatment were not frequent and included facial numbness,  weakness of facial muscles, bruised cheeks, failure to blink when the eye is touched, and dry eyes.

source: Introduction: Facial pain (FP) is a type of neuropathic pain which recognizes both central and peripheral causes.
target: Facial pain is nerve pain caused by nerves in the spinal cord and outside the spinal cord and brain.

source: It can be difficult to treat because it can often become resistant to pharmacological treatments.
target: It can be difficult to treat because pain relieve drugs may stop working.

source: Motor Cortex Stimulation (MCS) has been used in selected cases, but the correct indications of MCS in FP have not been fully established.
target: Motor Cortex Stimulation (MCS), surgical stimulation of the nerves, is sometimes used, but it is not known yet when to use it for facial pain.

source: Here we systematically reviewed the literature regarding MCS in FP analysing the results of this technique and studying the possible role of different factors in the prognosis of these patients.
target: We review what is known about treating patients with facial pain using MCS and the outcomes of the treatment.

source: Results: 111 articles were reviewed, and 12 studies were included in the present analysis for a total of 108 patients.
target: We found information about 108 patients.

source: Overall, at latest follow-up (FU), 70.83% of patients responded to MCS.
target: MCS helped almost three quarters of the patients.

source: The preoperative VAS significantly decreased at the latest FU (8.83 ± 1.17 and 4.31 ± 2.05, respectively; p < 0.0001).
target: The patients felt their pain was reduced almost to half of the pain they felt before surgery.

source: Younger age (p = 0.0478) and a peripheral FP syndrome (p = 0.0006) positively affected the definitive implantation rate on univariate analysis.
target: The treatment was more effective in younger patients and those with pain on the side of the face.

source: Younger age emerged as a factor strongly associated to a higher probability to go to a definitive MCS implant on multivariate analysis (p = 0.0415).
target: Younger patients were more likely to get MCS implanted devices

source: Conclusion: Our results evidenced the effectiveness of MCS in treating FP.
target: MCS is an effective treatment for facial pain.

source: Moreover, the younger age emerged as a positive prognostic factor for definitive implantation.
target: Younger patients were more likely to get permanent implanted devices.

source: Post-traumatic trigeminal neuralgia (PTTN), also known as anesthesia dolorosa, is at times a debilitating affliction, but remains a condition with minimal research and without definitive treatment, specifically in the periorbital and malar regions.
target: Post-traumatic trigeminal neuralgia (PTTN), also known as anesthesia dolorosa, refers to the pain in the trigeminal nerve, a three-part nerve in the head that provides sensation. PTTN is a crippling pain that has no established treatments, specifically for the pain around the eyes and back teeth.

source: Below we present a case of PTTN in a patient with historic facial trauma who has successfully achieved resolution of pain.
target: We describe how trigeminal nerve pain was treated in a patient who had it due to an old injury to his face.

source: We describe diagnostic and therapeutic anesthesia blocks and ablative procedures targeting the zygomaticofacial and zygomaticotemporal nerves.
target: We describe surgery and treatment that blocked sensation in the facial nerves around the temples and cheek bones.

source: We promote awareness for the procedures and the potential large impact on the oral and maxillofacial surgery community when treating those suffering from facial pain.
target: This treatment could be used by dental surgeons who treat patients with facial pain.

source: Finally, we present an algorithm that can aid surgeons in diagnosing and treating patients with PTTN.
target: We also describe a decision support tool for recognizing and treating PTTN.

source: The current understanding of ON is that it causes neuropathic pain in the distribution of the greater occipital nerve, the lesser occipital nerve, the third occipital nerve or a combination of the 3.
target: Occipital Neuralgia is the pain in the occipital nerves, the nerves that run from the top of the spinal cord up in the back of the head.

source: It is currently a subset of headaches although there is some debate if ON should be its own condition.
target: It is considered a type of headaches, but some doctors suggest pain in the nerves in the back of the head is a different problem.

source: Occipital neuralgia causes chronic, sharp, stabbing pain in the upper neck, back of the head, and behind the ears that can radiate to the front of the head.
target: Occipital neuralgia is a continuing, sharp, stabbing pain in the upper neck, back of the head, and behind the ears. It can also extend to the front of the head.

source: Diagnosis is typically clinical and patients present with intermittent, painful episodes associated with the occipital region and the nerves described above.
target: The doctors decide the patients have the disease if the pain in the back of the patients' heads comes and goes.

source: Most cases are unilateral pain, however bilateral pain can be present and the pain can radiate to the frontal region and face.
target: Most patients have pain on one side of their head, but the pain could also be on both sides and extend to the front of the head and face.

source: Physical examination is the first step in management of this disease and patients may demonstrate tenderness over the greater occipital and lesser occipital nerves.
target: When the doctors examine the patients, the patients' skin over the nerves in the back of the head  may be tender.

source: Anesthetics like 1% to 2% lidocaine or 0.25% to 0.5% bupivacaine can be used to block these nerves and anti-inflammatory drugs like corticosteroids can be used in combination to prevent compressive symptoms.
target: Drugs like lidocaine can block the feeling of pain in these nerves. Drugs that reduce inflammation, such as steroids may be added to keep the signs from happening.

source: Other treatments like botulinum toxin and radiofrequency ablation have shown promise and require more research.
target: Other treatments like botulinum toxin that blocks the nerve signals and radiofrequency ablation that uses radio waves to shrink tissues may be promising but need to be studied more.

source: Surgical decompression through resection of the obliquus capitis inferior is the definitive treatment however there are significant risks associated with this procedure.
target: Surgeons may permanently relieve the pressure on the nerve by removing a small muscle between the neck vertebra at the base of the head, but this treatment is risky.

source: The timing of the development of specific adaptive immunity after natural SARS-CoV-2 infection, and its relevance in clinical outcome, has not been characterized in depth.
target: The timing of the development of immunity after natural SARS-CoV-2 infection has not been studied in depth.

source: Description of the long-term maintenance of both cellular and humoral responses elicited by real-world anti-SARS-CoV-2 vaccination is still scarce.
target: Data about long-term immunity following anti-SARS-CoV-2 vaccination is still scarce.

source: Here we aimed to understand the development of optimal protective responses after SARS-CoV-2 infection and vaccination.
target: We studied the the development of the strongest protective immune responses after SARS-CoV-2 infection and vaccination.

source: We performed an early, longitudinal study of S1-, M- and N-specific IFN-Î³ and IL-2 T cell immunity and anti-S total and neutralizing antibodies in 88 mild, moderate or severe acute COVID-19 patients.
target: We performed a long-term study of the immune responses and antibody blood levels in patients with mild, moderate or severe acute COVID-19 infection.

source: Moreover, SARS-CoV-2-specific adaptive immunity was also analysed in 234 COVID-19 recovered subjects, 28 uninfected BNT162b2-vaccinees and 30 uninfected healthy controls.
target: Immune response was also studied in 234 COVID-19 recovered subjects, 28 uninfected BNT162b2 (Pfizer)-vaccinees and 30 uninfected healthy unvaccinated people.

source: Upon natural infection, cellular and humoral responses were early and coordinated in mild patients, while weak and inconsistent in severe patients.
target: During natural infection, immune responses were early and coordinated in patients with mild COVID-19, while the immune responses were weak and inconsistent in patients with severe illness.

source: The S1-specific cellular response measured at hospital arrival was an independent predictive factor against severity.
target: Immunity in patients was measured at hospital arrival. Patients with a strong cellular immune response against the virus spike protein had a less severe illness.

source: In COVID-19 recovered patients, four to seven months post-infection, cellular immunity was maintained but antibodies and neutralization capacity declined.
target: In COVID-19 recovered patients, four to seven months post-infection, cellular immunity was maintained but antibody blood levels and the capacity of the antibodies to block the virus declined.

source: Finally, a robust Th1-driven immune response was developed in uninfected BNT162b2-vaccinees.
target: In uninfected BNT162b2-vaccinees, a strong cellular immune response developed.

source: Three months post-vaccination, the cellular response was comparable, while the humoral response was consistently stronger, to that measured in COVID-19 recovered patients.
target: Three months post-vaccination, the cellular immune response was comparable to that measured in patients who recovered from COVID-19. In vaccinated people, the antibody response was consistently stronger to that measured in COVID-19 recovered patients.

source: Thus, measurement of both humoral and cellular responses provides information on prognosis and protection from infection, which may add value for individual and public health recommendations.
target: Our study of the cellular immune response and the antibody response provides information about protection from infection and the likelihood of developing severe infection. This information could be used to influence public health recommendations.

source: Introduction: Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) vaccines are nothing short of a miracle story halting the pandemic across the globe.
target: Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) vaccines are nothing short of a miracle story halting the pandemic across the globe.

source: Nearly half of the global population has received at least one dose.
target: Nearly half of the global population has received at least one dose.

source: Nevertheless, antibody levels in vaccinated people have shown waning, and breakthrough infections have occurred.
target: Nevertheless, antibody blood levels in vaccinated people drop over time, and breakthrough infections have occurred.

source: Our study aims to measure antibody kinetics following AZD1222 (ChAdOx1) vaccination six months after the second dose and the factors affecting the kinetics.
target: We studied the trends in antibody blood levels six months after the second dose of the AZD1222 (ChAdOx1) AstraZeneca vaccine.

source: Materials and methods: We conducted a prospective longitudinal study monitoring for six months after the second of two AZD1222 (ChAdOx1) vaccine doses in healthcare professionals and healthcare facility employees at Veer Surendra Sai Institute of Medical Sciences and Research (included doctors, nurses, paramedical staff, security and sanitary workers, and students).
target: We conducted a 6 month study after the second of two AZD1222 (ChAdOx1) vaccine doses in healthcare professionals and healthcare facility employees at Veer Surendra Sai Institute of Medical Sciences and Research. The study population included doctors, nurses, paramedical staff, security and sanitary workers, and students.

source: Two 0.5-mL doses of the vaccine were administered intramuscularly, containing 5 x 1010 viral particles 28 to 30 days between doses.
target: Two doses of the vaccine were injected into the upper arm, with 28 to 30 days between doses.

source: We collected blood samples one month after the first dose (Round 1), one month after the second dose (Round 2), and six months after the second dose (Round 3).
target: We collected blood samples one month after the first dose (Round 1), one month after the second dose (Round 2), and six months after the second dose (Round 3).

source: We tested for immunoglobulin G (IgG) levels against the receptor-binding domain of the spike protein of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) by chemiluminescence microparticle immunoassay.
target: We measured blood levels of antibodies against the spike protein of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2).

source: We conducted a linear mixed model analysis to study the antibody kinetics and influencing factors.
target: We used statistical methods to measure the change in antibody blood levels over time.

source: Results: Our study included 122 participants (mean age, 41.5 years; 66 men, 56 women).
target: Our study included 122 participants (mean age, 41.5 years; 66 men, 56 women).

source: The geometric mean IgG titers were 138.01 binding antibody units (BAU)/mL in Round 1, 176.48 BAU/mL in Round 2, and 112.95 BAU/mL in Round 3.
target: Antibody blood levels were 138.01 in Round 1, increased to 1,176.48 in Round 2, and dropped to 112.95 in Round 3.

source: Seven participants showed seroreversion, and 11 had breakthrough infections.
target: Seven participants showed a complete loss of measurable blood antibodies, and 11 had breakthrough infections.

source: Eighty-six participants showed a substantial decline in antibody titer from Rounds 2 to 3.
target: Eighty-six participants showed a substantial decline in antibody levels from Rounds 2 to 3.

source: Persons aged 45 or older had higher mean titer than people aged younger than 45 years.
target: Persons aged 45 or older had higher antibody levels than people aged younger than 45 years.

source: Overweight and obese (BMI â‰¥ 25 kg/m2) had a higher mean titer than average or underweight persons.
target: Overweight and obese people (body mass index larger than 25) had higher antibody levels than average or underweight persons.

source: The only significant predictor of IgG titers at six months was SARS-CoV-2 infection on mixed model analysis.
target: The only people who maintained high levels of antibodies at 6 months were in people who had been infected with SARS-CoV2.

source: Conclusion: We found a substantial decline in antibody levels leading to seven cases of seroreversion in healthcare professionals who received the ChAdOx1 vaccine.
target: We found a substantial decline in antibody levels leading to seven cases of loss of antibodies in healthcare professionals who received the ChAdOx1 vaccine.

source: History of prior COVID-19 was the only significant factor in antibody levels at six months.
target: A history of prior COVID-19 infection was the only significant reason for high antibody levels at six months.

source: Seroreversion and breakthrough infection warrant further research into the optimal timing and potential benefits of booster doses of the AZD1222 (ChAdOx1) COVID-19 vaccine.
target: More research needs to be conducted on the optimal timing and potential benefits of booster doses of the AZD1222 (ChAdOx1) COVID-19 vaccine to avoid breakthrough infections or loss of immunity.

source: Estimates of COVID-19 mRNA vaccine effectiveness (VE) have declined in recent months because of waning vaccine induced immunity over time, possible increased immune evasion by SARS-CoV-2 variants, or a combination of these and other factors.
target: It is estimated that the effectiveness of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines has declined in recent months. There are several possible reasons for this. Vaccine-induced immunity decreases over time. New strains of the SARS-CoV-2 virus can become resistant to the vaccine, a process called immune evasion. A combination of these two phenomena or other factors could also cause decreased vaccine effectiveness.

source: CDC recommends that all persons aged â‰¥12 years receive a third dose (booster) of an mRNA vaccine â‰¥5 months after receipt of the second mRNA vaccine dose and that immunocompromised individuals receive a third primary dose.
target: The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommends that all people 12 years and older receive a third booster shot of an mRNA vaccine 5 months or later after receiving the second primary shot. Patients with a weakened immune system should receive a third primary shot.

source: A third dose of BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) COVID-19 vaccine increases neutralizing antibody levels, and three recent studies from Israel have shown improved effectiveness of a third dose in preventing COVID-19 associated with infections with the SARS-CoV-2 B.1.617.2 (Delta) variant.
target: A third dose of the Pfizer vaccine (BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine) increases the blood level of antibodies that neutralize the virus and prevent infection. Three recent studies from Israel have shown that a third booster dose helps prevent COVID-19 caused by the Delta variant (SARS-CoV-2 B.1.617.2).

source: Yet, data are limited on the real-world effectiveness of third doses of COVID-19 mRNA vaccine in the United States, especially since the SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.529 (Omicron) variant became predominant in mid-December 2021.
target: However, in the United States there is little data to prove the effectiveness of third booster shots to prevent COVID-19, especially since the Omicron variant (SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.529) became the most common strain in mid-December 2021.

source: The VISION Network examined VE by analyzing 222,772 encounters from 383 emergency departments (EDs) and urgent care (UC) clinics and 87,904 hospitalizations from 259 hospitals among adults aged â‰¥18 years across 10 states from August 26, 2021 to January 5, 2022.
target: From August 26, 2021 to January 5, 2022, the VISION Network examined vaccine effectiveness among adults 18 and older across 10 states in the US by studying over 222,000 patients in 383 emergency departments and urgent care clinics, and over 87,000 hospitalized inpatients from 259 hospitals.

source: Analyses were stratified by the period before and after the Omicron variant became the predominant strain (>50% of sequenced viruses) at each study site.
target: The analysis was split apart at each study site by looking at the periods before and after the Omicron strain became the most common strain.

source: During the period of Delta predominance across study sites in the United States (August-mid-December 2021), VE against laboratory-confirmed COVID-19-associated ED and UC encounters was 86% 14-179 days after dose 2, 76% â‰¥180 days after dose 2, and 94% â‰¥14 days after dose 3.
target: During the time when the Delta strain was most common in the US (August to mid-December 2021), in emergency departments and urgent care clinics vaccine effectiveness in preventing infection was 86% effective 14-179 days after dose 2, dropped to 76% more than 180 days after dose 2, but increased up to 94% 14 days or more after dose 3.

source: Estimates of VE for the same intervals after vaccination during Omicron variant predominance were 52%, 38%, and 82%, respectively.
target: When the Omicron strain was most common, vaccine effectiveness for the same time intervals was only 52%, 38%, and 82%, respectively.

source: During the period of Delta variant predominance, VE against laboratory-confirmed COVID-19-associated hospitalizations was 90% 14-179 days after dose 2, 81% â‰¥180 days after dose 2, and 94% â‰¥14 days after dose 3.
target: In hospitalized patients, during the Delta strain period vaccine effectiveness was 90% 14-179 days after dose 2, 81% 180 days or longer after dose 2, and 94% 14 days or more after dose 3.

source: During Omicron variant predominance, VE estimates for the same intervals after vaccination were 81%, 57%, and 90%, respectively.
target: During the Omicron period, estimates for the same time intervals after vaccination were 81%, 57%, and 90%, respectively.

source: The highest estimates of VE against COVID-19-associated ED and UC encounters or hospitalizations during both Delta- and Omicron-predominant periods were among adults who received a third dose of mRNA vaccine.
target: The highest estimates of vaccine effectiveness in both patient populations during both the Delta and Omicron periods were in adults who had received a third dose of mRNA vaccine.

source: All unvaccinated persons should get vaccinated as soon as possible.
target: Based on this data, we recommed that all unvaccinated persons should get vaccinated as soon as possible.

source: All adults who have received mRNA vaccines during their primary COVID-19 vaccination series should receive a third dose when eligible, and eligible persons should stay up to date with COVID-19 vaccinations.
target: All adults who have received their first two doses of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines should receive a third dose as soon as they are eligible, and eligible persons should stay up to date with COVID-19 vaccinations and boosters.

source: The BNT162b2 vaccine is highly effective against COVID-19 infection and was delivered with a 3-week time interval in registration studies 1.
target: The BNT162b2 vaccine (Pfizer) is highly effective against COVID-19 infection. In registration studies, the two doses were delivered 3 weeks apart..

source: However, many countries extended this interval to accelerate population coverage with a single vaccine.
target: However, many countries extended the time between the first and second doses to maximize the number of people vaccinated with one dose.

source: It is not known how immune responses are influenced by delaying the second dose.
target: It is not known how antibody responses or cellular immune responses are influenced by delaying the second dose.

source: We provide the assessment of immune responses in the first 14 weeks after standard or extended-interval BNT162b2 vaccination and show that delaying the second dose strongly boosts the peak antibody response by 3.5-fold in older people.
target: We studied the immune response in the first 14 weeks after the standard 3-week interval or the extended interval BNT162b2 vaccination. We showed that delaying the second dose strongly boosts the peak antibody response by 3.5-fold in older people.

source: This enhanced antibody response may offer a longer period of clinical protection and delay the need for booster vaccination.
target: This enhanced antibody response may offer a longer period of protection against infection and delay the need for booster vaccination.

source: In contrast, peak cellular-specific responses were the strongest in those vaccinated on a standard 3-week vaccine interval.
target: In contrast, peak cellular-specific responses were the strongest in those vaccinated on a standard 3-week vaccine interval.

source: As such, the timing of the second dose has a marked influence on the kinetics and magnitude of the adaptive immune response after mRNA vaccination in older people.
target: The timing of the second dose has a strong influence on the antibody response after BNT162b2 vaccination in older people.

source: Objectives: To determine the status of immune responses after primary and booster immunization for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) variants and evaluate the differences in disease-resistance based upon titers of neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) against the variants.
target: We studied the immune response after primary and booster immunization for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) variants. We evaluated the differences in disease resistance based upon blood levels of neutralizing antibodies (antibodies that inactivate the virus) against the variants.

source: Methods: Participants aged 18 - 59 y received two doses of inactivated COVID-19 vaccine, 14 days apart, and a booster dose after 12 m.
target: Participants aged 18 - 59 years old received two doses of inactivated COVID-19 vaccine, 14 days apart, and a booster dose after 12 months.

source: Blood samples were collected before vaccination (baseline), 1 and 6 m after primary immunization, and at multiple instances within 21 d of booster dose.
target: Blood samples were collected before vaccination, 1 and 6 months after primary immunization, and several times within 21 days of booster dose.

source: NAbs against the spike protein of Wuhan-Hu-1 and three variants were measured using pseudovirus neutralization assays.
target: Neutralizing antibodies against the spike protein of the Wuhan-Hu-1 variant and three other variants were measured.

source: Results: Out of 400 enrolled participants, 387 completed visits scheduled within 6 m of the second dose, and 346 participated received the booster dose in the follow-up research.
target: Out of 400 enrolled participants, 387 completed visits scheduled within 6 months of the second dose, and 346 participants received the booster dose in the follow-up research.

source: After 1 m of primary immunization, geometric mean titers (GMTs) of NAbs peaked for Wuhan-Hu-1, while GMTs of other variants were < 30.
target: After 1 month of primary immunization, blood levels of neutralizing antibodies peaked for Wuhan-Hu-1, while blood levels against other variants were lower.

source: After 6 m of primary immunization, GMTs of NAbs against all strains were < 30.
target: After 6 months of primary immunization, blood levels against all strains were reduced.

source: After 3 d of booster immunization, GMTs were unaltered, seroconversion rates reached approximately 50% after 7 d, and GMTs of NAbs against all strains peaked at 14 d.
target: After 3 days of booster immunization, blood levels were unchanged. However, blood levels increased approximately 50% after 7 days, and peaked at 14 days for all strains.

source: Conclusion: Two-dose of inactivated COVID-19 vaccine induced the formation of NAbs and memory-associated immune responses, and high titers of NAbs against the variants obtained after booster immunization may further improve the effectiveness of the vaccine.
target: Two doses of inactivated COVID-19 vaccine produced neutralizing antibodies and immunity, and high levels of neutralizing antibodies against the variants after booster immunization could further improve the effectiveness of the vaccine.

source: Background: Vaccine effectiveness against COVID-19 beyond 6 months remains incompletely understood.
target: The effectiveness of a vaccine against COVID-19 6 months after vaccination is not fully understood.

source: We aimed to investigate the effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccination against the risk of infection, hospitalisation, and death during the first 9 months after vaccination for the total population of Sweden.
target: We studied the effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccination against the risk of infection, hospitalisation, and death during the first 9 months after vaccination for the total population of Sweden.

source: Methods: This retrospective, total population cohort study was done using data from Swedish nationwide registers.
target: We took our data from Swedish nationwide registers.

source: The cohort comprised all individuals vaccinated with two doses of ChAdOx1 nCoV-19, mRNA-1273, or BNT162b2, and matched unvaccinated individuals, with data on vaccinations and infections updated until Oct 4, 2021.
target: The data were from all individuals vaccinated with two doses of ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 (AstraZeneca), mRNA-1273 (Moderna), or BNT162b2 (Pfizer) vaccines, and matched unvaccinated individuals, with data on vaccinations and infections updated until Oct 4, 2021.

source: Two outcomes were evaluated.
target: Two outcomes were evaluated.

source: The first was SARS-CoV-2 infection of any severity from Jan 12 to Oct 4, 2021.
target: The first was SARS-CoV-2 infection of any severity from Jan 12 to Oct 4, 2021.

source: The second was severe COVID-19, defined as hospitalisation for COVID-19 or all-cause 30-day mortality after confirmed infection, from March 15 to Sept 28, 2021.
target: The second was severe COVID-19, defined as hospitalisation for COVID-19 or death from any cause 30 days after confirmed infection, from March 15 to Sept 28, 2021.

source: Findings: Between Dec 28, 2020, and Oct 4, 2021, 842 974 individuals were fully vaccinated (two doses), and were matched (1:1) to an equal number of unvaccinated individuals (total study cohort n=1 685 948).
target: Between Dec 28, 2020, and Oct 4, 2021, 842 974 individuals were fully vaccinated (two doses). We compared these individuals to an equal number of unvaccinated individuals. The total number of people studied was 1,685,948.

source: For the outcome SARS-CoV-2 infection of any severity, the vaccine effectiveness of BNT162b2 waned progressively over time, from 92% (95% CI 92 to 93; p<0Â·001) at 15-30 days, to 47% (39 to 55; p<0Â·001) at 121-180 days, and to 23% (-2 to 41; p=0Â·07) from day 211 onwards.
target: In patients who had SARS-CoV-2 infection of any severity, the vaccine effectiveness of BNT162b2 dropped progressively over time, from 92% at 15-30 days after vaccination, to 47% at 121-180 days, and to 23% from day 211 onwards.

source: Waning was slightly slower for mRNA-1273, with a vaccine effectiveness of 96% (94 to 97; p<0Â·001) at 15-30 days and 59% (18 to 79; p=0Â·012) from day 181 onwards.
target: Reduced vaccine effectiveness was slightly slower for the mRNA-1273 vaccine, with a vaccine effectiveness of 96% at 15-30 days and 59% from day 181 onwards.

source: Waning was also slightly slower for heterologous ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 plus an mRNA vaccine, for which vaccine effectiveness was 89% (79 to 94; p<0Â·001) at 15-30 days and 66% (41 to 80; p<0Â·001) from day 121 onwards.
target: Reduction of vaccine effectiveness was also slightly slower for the combination of the ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 vaccine plus an mRNA vaccine, for which vaccine effectiveness was 89% at 15-30 days and 66% from day 121 onwards.

source: By contrast, vaccine effectiveness for homologous ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 vaccine was 68% (52 to 79; p<0Â·001) at 15-30 days, with no detectable effectiveness from day 121 onwards (-19% [-98 to 28]; p=0Â·49).
target: By contrast, vaccine effectiveness for the ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 vaccine only (not combined with another vaccine) was 68% at 15-30 days, with no detectable effectiveness from day 121 onwards.

source: For the outcome of severe COVID-19, vaccine effectiveness waned from 89% (82 to 93; p<0Â·001) at 15-30 days to 64% (44 to 77; p<0Â·001) from day 121 onwards.
target: In patients who had severe COVID-19, vaccine effectiveness dropped from 89% at 15-30 days to 64% from day 121 onwards.

source: Overall, there was some evidence for lower vaccine effectiveness in men than in women and in older individuals than in younger individuals.
target: Overall, there was some evidence for lower vaccine effectiveness in men than in women and in older individuals than in younger individuals.

source: Interpretation: We found progressively waning vaccine effectiveness against SARS-CoV-2 infection of any severity across all subgroups, but the rate of waning differed according to vaccine type.
target: We found progressively reduced vaccine effectiveness against SARS-CoV-2 infection of any severity across all subgroups of patients, but the rate of reduction differed according to vaccine type.

source: With respect to severe COVID-19, vaccine effectiveness seemed to be better maintained, although some waning became evident after 4 months.
target: In patients who had severe COVID-19, vaccine effectiveness seemed to be better maintained, although some reduction became evident after 4 months.

source: The results strengthen the evidence-based rationale for administration of a third vaccine dose as a booster.
target: Our study provides evidence that a third vaccine dose as a booster will improve immunity against COVID-19.

source: Recognizing that anti-SARS-CoV-2 antibody levels wane over time following the 2-dose SARS-CoV-2 mRNA series, the FDA approved a booster dose for people greater than 12 years old.
target: After vaccination with the 2-dose COVID-19 mRNA vaccine, blood levels of antibodies against the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2 drop over time. To increase blood levels of antibodies again, the FDA approved a booster dose for people greater than 12 years old.

source: Limited data exist on whether a booster dose of the mRNA vaccine results in greater antibody protection than the primary series.
target: However, limited data exist on whether a booster dose of the mRNA vaccine results in greater antibody protection than the primary series.

source: We examined total and neutralizing antibodies to the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2, and neutralizing antibodies against Washington-1 (WA-1) and variants of concern (VOC) including Beta, Delta and Omicron in a longitudinal cohort.
target: We measured the blood levels of all types of antibodies against the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2, and a specific type of antibodies - called neutralizing antibodies - against Washington-1 and other variants of concern including Beta, Delta and Omicron. We followed the trends in antibody levels at several points in time after vaccination.

source: Healthcare workers (HWs) were included in the analysis if serum was collected 1) within 14-44 days post-dose2 of an mRNA SARS-CoV-2 vaccine (Timepoint 1, TP1), or 2) at least 8 months post-dose2 (Timepoint 2, TP2), or 3) within 14-44 days following mRNA booster (Timepoint 3, TP3).
target: Healthcare workers were included in the analysis if serum was collected within 14-44 days post-dose 2 of an mRNA SARS-CoV-2 vaccine, or at least 8 months post-dose 2, or within 14-44 days following an mRNA booster shot.

source: HWs with prior covid-positive PCR were excluded.
target: Healthcare workers who had previously tested positive to COVID-19 were excluded from this study.

source: We found that there is little to no neutralizing capability following a 2-dose mRNA vaccine series against the omicron variant, and neutralizing capacity to any variant strain tested has been lost by 8-months post two-dose vaccination series.
target: We found that there are few to no neutralizing antibodies produced against the omicron variant following a 2-dose mRNA vaccine series. By 8-months post two-dose vaccination series, no neutralizing antibodies remain in blood circulation.

source: However, the mRNA booster series eliminates the immune escape observed by the omicron variant with the two-dose series.
target: However, the mRNA booster reactivates immunity to the omicron variant after the two-dose vaccination.

source: Neutralizing titers were significantly higher for all variants post-boost compared to the titers post two-dose series.
target: Neutralizing antibody blood levels were significantly higher for all variants post-booster compared to the levels after the first two shots.

source: The longitudinal nature of our cohort facilitated the analysis of paired samples pre and post boost, showing a greater than 15-fold increase in neutralization against omicron post-boost in these paired samples.
target: The long-term nature of our study helped us to analyze the trends of antibody blood levels over time. We saw a 15-fold increase in neutralizing antibodies against the omicron variant following the booster shot.

source: An mRNA booster dose provides greater quantity and quality of antibodies compared to a two-dose regimen and is critical to provide any protection against the omicron variant.
target: An mRNA booster dose provides greater quantity and quality of antibodies compared to a two-dose vaccine and is critical to provide any protection against the omicron variant.

source: Background: Immunization against SARS-CoV-2, the causative agent of coronavirus disease-19 (COVID-19) occurs via natural infection or vaccination.
target: Immunity to SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes coronavirus disease-19 (COVID-19) occurs from natural infection or vaccination.

source: However, it is currently unknown how long infection- or vaccination-induced immunological memory will last.
target: However, it is currently unknown how long infection- or vaccination-induced immunity will last.

source: Methods: We performed a longitudinal evaluation of immunological memory to SARS-CoV-2 up to one year post infection and following mRNA vaccination in naÃ¯ve and COVID-19 recovered individuals.
target: We performed a long-term study of immunity to SARS-CoV-2 up to one year post infection and following mRNA vaccination in unexposed people and in people who have recovered from COVID-19 infection.

source: Results: We found that memory cells are still detectable 8 months after vaccination, while antibody levels decline significantly especially in naÃ¯ve subjects.
target: We found that memory cells (immune cells that "remember" having encountered an infection before) are still detectable 8 months after vaccination, while viral antibody blood levels decline significantly, especially in previously unexposed people.

source: We also found that a booster injection is efficacious in reactivating immunological memory to spike protein in naÃ¯ve subjects, while it results ineffective in previously SARS-CoV-2 infected individuals.
target: We also found that a booster shot is effective in reactivating immunity to the virus spike protein in previously unexposed people, while it is ineffective in people who were previously infected with SARS-CoV-2.

source: Finally, we observed a similar kinetics of decay of humoral and cellular immunity to SARS-CoV-2 up to one year following natural infection in a cohort of unvaccinated individuals.
target: Finally, we found a similar reduction of immunity to SARS-CoV-2 up to one year following natural infection in a group of unvaccinated individuals.

source: Conclusion: Short-term persistence of humoral immunity, together with the reduced neutralization capacity versus the currently prevailing SARS-CoV-2 variants, may account for reinfections and breakthrough infections.
target: We conclude that short-term immunity, together with the reduced ability of the immune system to block the newer strains of SARS-CoV-2, might account for breakthrough infections in vaccinated people and reinfections in people who were previously infected.

source: A booster dose restores optimal anti-spike immunity in naÃ¯ve subjects, while the need for vaccinated COVID-19 recovered subjects has yet to be defined.
target: A booster dose restores the strongest immunity against the viral spike protein in unexposed people, but it is not clear if people who have recovered from COVID-19 need to have a booster.

source: There is considerable interest in the waning of effectiveness of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) vaccines and vaccine effectiveness (VE) of booster doses.
target: We are interested in studying the waning effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines and the effectiveness of booster doses.

source: Using linked national Brazilian databases, we undertook a test-negative design study involving almost 14 million people (~16 million tests) to estimate VE of Corona Vac over time and VE of BNT162b2 booster vaccination against RT-PCR-confirmed severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection and severe COVID-19 outcomes (hospitalization or death).
target: Using national Brazilian databases, we studied the trends of negative test results of almost 14 million people (representing about 16 million tests) to estimate the reduced effectiveness of CoronaVac COVID-19 vaccines over time. We also studied the effects of the Pfizer BNT162b2 booster dose on the rates of confirmed COVID-19 infection and severe outcomes (hospitalization or death).

source: Compared with unvaccinated individuals, CoronaVac VE at 14-30 d after the second dose was 55.0% (95% confidence interval (CI): 54.3-55.7) against confirmed infection and 82.1% (95% CI: 81.4-82.8) against severe outcomes.
target: Compared with unvaccinated individuals, the effectiveness of the CoronaVac vaccine at 14-30 days after the second dose was 55% against confirmed infection and 82.1% against severe outcomes.

source: VE decreased to 34.7% (95% CI: 33.1-36.2) against infection and 72.5% (95% CI: 70.9-74.0) against severe outcomes over 180 d after the second dose.
target: Vaccine effectiveness decreased to 34.7% against infection and 72.5% against severe outcomes over 180 days after the second dose.

source: A BNT162b2 booster, 6 months after the second dose of CoronaVac, improved VE against infection to 92.7% (95% CI: 91.0-94.0) and VE against severe outcomes to 97.3% (95% CI: 96.1-98.1) 14-30 d after the booster.
target: A Pfizer BNT162b2 booster shot given 6 months after the second dose of CoronaVac, improved vaccine effectiveness by 92.7% against infection and 97.3% against severe outcomes 14-30 days after the booster.

source: Compared with younger age groups, individuals 80 years of age or older had lower protection after the second dose but similar protection after the booster.
target: Compared with younger age groups, individuals 80 years of age or older had lower protection after the second dose but similar protection after the booster.

source: Our findings support a BNT162b2 booster vaccine dose after two doses of CoronaVac, particularly for the elderly.
target: Based on these results, we recommend a BNT162b2 booster vaccine dose after two doses of CoronaVac, particularly for the elderly.

source: Objectives: To estimate the effectiveness of mRNA vaccines against SARS-CoV-2 infection and severe covid-19 at different time after vaccination.
target: We studied the effectiveness of mRNA vaccines against SARS-CoV-2 infection and severe COVID-19 at different times after vaccination.

source: Design: Retrospective cohort study.
target: This study was performed in Italy from December 27, 2020 to November 7, 2021.

source: Participants: 33 250 344 people aged â‰¥16 years who received a first dose of BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) or mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine and did not have a previous diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection.
target: The participants included people aged 16 years and older who received a first dose of BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) or mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine and did not have a previous diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection.

source: Main outcome measures: SARS-CoV-2 infection and severe covid-19 (admission to hospital or death).
target: We tracked the number of people with SARS-CoV-2 infection and severe COVID-19 (admission to hospital or death).

source: Data were divided by weekly time intervals after vaccination.
target: The data was calculated for each week following vaccination.

source: Incidence rate ratios at different time intervals were estimated by multilevel negative binomial models with robust variance estimator.
target: The incidence rate of infection at different time intervals was estimated using statistical models.

source: Sex, age group, brand of vaccine, priority risk category, and regional weekly incidence in the general population were included as covariates.
target: We recorded the sex, age group, brand of vaccine, and priority risk category of patients, and recorded the regional weekly incidence in the general population.

source: Geographic region was included as a random effect.
target: We tracked the number of cases according to geographic region.

source: Adjusted vaccine effectiveness was calculated as (1-IRR)Ã—100, where IRR=incidence rate ratio, with the time interval 0-14 days after the first dose of vaccine as the reference.
target: Using infection rates at 0-14 days after the first dose of vaccine as our starting point, we followed the trend of vaccine effectiveness

source: Results: During the epidemic phase when the delta variant was the predominant strain of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, vaccine effectiveness against SARS-CoV-2 infection significantly decreased (P<0.001) from 82% (95% confidence interval 80% to 84%) at 3-4 weeks after the second dose of vaccine to 33% (27% to 39%) at 27-30 weeks after the second dose.
target: During the epidemic phase when the delta variant was the most common strain of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, vaccine effectiveness against SARS-CoV-2 infection significantly decreased from 82% at 3-4 weeks after the second dose of vaccine to 33% at 27-30 weeks after the second dose.

source: In the same time intervals, vaccine effectiveness against severe covid-19 also decreased (P<0.001), although to a lesser extent, from 96% (95% to 97%) to 80% (76% to 83%).
target: In the same time range, vaccine effectiveness against severe COVID-19 also decreased, although to a lesser extent, from an average of 96% to an average of 80%.
