source: Increased leukocyte-erythrocyte-endothelial interactions, due to upregulated expression of adhesion molecules and activated endothelium, are thought to play a primary role in initiation and progression of SCD vaso-occlusive crisis and end-organ damage.
target: Increased white blood cell-red blood cell-boundary cell interactions, due to increases expression of sticking molecules and activated boundary cells, are thought to play a main role in starting and developing of SCD vaso-occlusive events and organ damage.

source: Several new pathophysiology-based therapeutic options for SCD are being developed, chiefly targeting the inflammatory pathways.
target: Several new disease effect-based treatments for SCD are being developed, chiefly targeting the inflammatory pathways.

source: Omega-3 fatty acids are polyunsaturated fatty acids that are known to have effects on diverse physiological processes.
target: Omega-3 fatty acids are polyunsaturated fatty acids that are known to have effects on many biological processes.

source: Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) are the principal biologically active omega-3 fatty acids.
target: Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) are the main biologically active omega-3 fatty acids.

source: The therapeutic effects of DHA and EPA on chronic inflammatory disorders and cardiovascular diseases are well recognized.
target: The helpful effects of DHA and EPA on chronic inflammatory disorders and heart-related or cardiovascular diseases are well recognized.

source: The therapeutic effects of omega-3 fatty acids are attributed to their anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic eicosanoids, and the novel class of EPA and DHA derived lipid mediators: resolvins, protectins and maresins.
target: The helpful effects of omega-3 fatty acids are linked to their anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic (blood clotting) eicosanoids (signaling molecules), and the new class of EPA and DHA derived fat level influencers: resolvins, protectins and maresins (other fatty-acid-related molecules).

source: Blood cell membranes of patients with SCD have abnormal fatty acids composition characterized by high ratio of pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid (AA) to anti-inflammatory DHA and EPA (high omega-6/omega-3 ratio).
target: Blood cell membranes or boundaries of patients with SCD have abnormal fatty acids composition characterized by high ratio of pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid (AA) to anti-inflammatory DHA and EPA (high omega-6/omega-3 ratio).

source: In addition, experimental and clinical studies provide evidence that treatment with DHA does confer improvement in rheological properties of sickle RBC, inflammation and hemolysis.
target: Also, experimental and clinical studies provide evidence that treatment with DHA does lead to improvement in blood cell deforming properties of sickle RBC, inflammation and hemolysis.

source: The clinical studies have shown improvements in VOC rate, markers of inflammation, adhesion, and hemolysis.
target: The clinical studies have shown improvements in vaso-occlusion crisis (VOC) rate, markers of inflammation, sticking molecules, and hemolysis.

source: In toto, the results of studies on the therapeutic effects of omega-3 fatty acids in SCD provide good body of evidence that omega-3 fatty acids could be a safe and effective treatment for SCD.
target: In total, the results of studies on the helpful effects of omega-3 fatty acids in SCD provide a good body of evidence that omega-3 fatty acids could be a safe and effective treatment for SCD.

source: Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) are involved both in immune system regulation and inflammation.
target: Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) are involved both in immune system monitoring or regulation and infection-fighting processes such as inflammation.

source: In particular, within the PUFAs category, omega-3 (ω-3) may reduce inflammation, whereas omega-6 (ω-6) PUFAs are generally considered to have a proinflammatory effect.
target: In particular, within the PUFAs category, omega-3 (ω-3) may reduce inflammation, whereas omega-6 (ω-6) PUFAs are generally considered to have an inflammatory effect.

source: Recent evidence highlights an imbalance in the ω-3:ω-6 ratio with an increased intake of ω-6, as a consequence of the shift towards a westernized diet.
target: Recent evidence shows an imbalance in the ω-3:ω-6 ratio with an increased intake of ω-6, as a result of the shift towards a westernized diet.

source: In critical age groups such as infants, toddlers and young children, as well as pregnant and lactating women or fish allergic patients, ω-3 intake may be inadequate.
target: In important age groups such as infants, toddlers and young children, as well as pregnant and milk-giving women or fish allergic patients, ω-3 intake may be improper.

source: This review aims to discuss the potential beneficial effects of PUFAs on pediatric food allergy prevention and treatment, both at prenatal and postnatal ages.
target: This article aims to discuss the potential beneficial effects of PUFAs on child-related food allergy prevention and treatment, both at pre-birth and post-birth ages.

source: Data from preclinical studies with PUFAs supplementation show encouraging effects in suppressing allergic response.
target: Data from preclinical studies with PUFAs supplementation show encouraging effects in reducing allergic response.

source: Clinical studies results are still conflicting about the best timing and dosages of supplementation and which individuals are most likely to benefit; therefore, it is still not possible to draw firm conclusions.
target: Medical study results are still conflicting about the best timing and amounts of supplementation and which individuals are most likely to benefit. Therefore, it is still not possible to draw firm conclusions.

source: With regard to food-allergic children, it is still debated whether PUFAs could slow disease progression or not, since consistent data are lacking.
target: Regarding food-allergic children, it is still argued whether PUFAs could slow disease worsening or not, since consistent data are lacking.

source: In conclusion, more data on the effects of ω-3 PUFAs supplementation alone or in combination with other nutrients are warranted, both in the general and food allergic population.
target: In conclusion, more data on the effects of ω-3 PUFAs supplementation alone or in combination with other nutrients are needed, both in the general and food allergic population.

source: Elevated inflammatory cytokines and chronic pain are associated with shorter leukocyte telomere length (LTL), a measure of cellular aging.
target: Increased infection-fighting (inflammatory) molecules and lasting (chronic) pain are associated with shorter white blood cell gene end or telomere length (LTL), a measure of cellular aging.

source: Micronutrients, such as 25-hydroxyvitamin D (vitamin D) and omega 3, have anti-inflammatory properties.
target: Micronutrients, such as 25-hydroxyvitamin D (vitamin D) and omega 3, have anti-inflammatory properties.

source: Little is known regarding the relationships between vitamin D, omega 6:3 ratio, LTL, inflammation, and chronic pain.
target: Little is known about the relationships between vitamin D, omega 6:3 ratio, LTL, inflammation, and chronic pain.

source: We investigate associations between vitamin D, omega 6:3 ratio, LTL, and C-reactive protein (CRP) in people living with/without chronic pain overall and stratified by chronic pain status.
target: We investigate links between vitamin D, omega 6:3 ratio, LTL, and C-reactive protein (CRP, a marker of imflammation) in people living with/without chronic pain overall and grouped by chronic pain status.

source: A cross-sectional analysis of 402 individuals (63% women, 79.5% with chronic pain) was completed.
target: An analysis of 402 individuals (63% women, 79.5% with chronic pain) was done.

source: Demographic and health information was collected.
target: Basic and health information was collected.

source: Chronic pain was assessed as pain experienced for at least three months.
target: Chronic pain was measured as pain experienced for at least three months.

source: LTL was measured in genomic DNA isolated from blood leukocytes, and micronutrients and CRP were measured in serum samples.
target: LTL was measured in gene DNA isolated from blood white blood cells, and micronutrients and CRP were measured in blood samples.

source: Data were analyzed with general linear regression.
target: Data were analyzed with general mathematical analysis.

source: Although an association between the continuous micronutrients and LTL was not observed, a positive association between omega 6:3 ratio and CRP was detected.
target: Although a link between the continuous micronutrients and LTL was not found, a positive link between omega 6:3 ratio and CRP was detected.

source: In individuals with chronic pain, based on clinical categories, significant associations between vitamin D, omega 6:3 ratio, and CRP were observed.
target: In individuals with chronic pain, based on medical categories, significant links between vitamin D, omega 6:3 ratio, and CRP were observed.

source: Findings highlight the complex relationships between anti-inflammatory micronutrients, inflammation, cellular aging, and chronic pain.
target: Findings show the complex relationships between anti-inflammatory micronutrients, inflammation, cellular aging, and chronic pain.

source: Nephrotic syndrome (NS) is one of the most common glomerular diseases that affect children.
target: Nephrotic Syndrome (NS) is one of the most common kidney diseases in children.

source: Renal histology reveals the presence of minimal change nephrotic syndrome (MCNS) in more than 80% of these patients.
target: Most of the time, looking at kidney tissue under a microscope shows that there is Minimal Change Nephrotic Syndrome (MCNS).

source: Most patients with MCNS have favorable outcomes without complications.
target: Most people with MCNS can be cured without other issues.

source: However, a few of these children have lesions of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, suffer from severe and prolonged proteinuria, and are at high risk for complications.
target: However, some children with NS have a different form called focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Children with this form have excess protein in their urine for long periods of time and are at high risk for additional problems.

source: Complications of NS are divided into two categories: disease-associated and drug-related complications.
target: Additional problems caused by NS come in two categories: problems caused by the disease, and problems caused by drugs used to treat the disease.

source: Disease-associated complications include infections (e.g., peritonitis, sepsis, cellulitis, and chicken pox), thromboembolism (e.g., venous thromboembolism and pulmonary embolism), hypovolemic crisis (e.g., abdominal pain, tachycardia, and hypotension), cardiovascular problems (e.g., hyperlipidemia), acute renal failure, anemia, and others (e.g., hypothyroidism, hypocalcemia, bone disease, and intussusception).
target: Problems caused by the disease include infections, blood clots, shock, heart disease, kidney failure, anemia, and others.

source: The main pathomechanism of disease-associated complications originates from the large loss of plasma proteins in the urine of nephrotic children.
target: The main way NS causes problems is from the large loss of proteins from the blood through urine.

source: The majority of children with MCNS who respond to treatment with corticosteroids or cytotoxic agents have smaller and milder complications than those with steroid-resistant NS.
target: Most children with MCNS that is treatable with drugs have fewer additional problems that children with NS that drugs cannot help.

source: Corticosteroids, alkylating agents, cyclosporin A, and mycophenolate mofetil have often been used to treat NS, and these drugs have treatment-related complications.
target: Several drugs have been used to treat NS. These drugs can cause additional problems when used to treat NS.

source: Early detection and appropriate treatment of these complications will improve outcomes for patients with NS.
target: Finding additional problems early will improve results for patients with NS.

source: Background and objectives: There are very little data available regarding nephrotic syndrome (NS) in elderly (aged ≥65 years) Japanese.
target: There’s not a lot of information about Nephrotic Syndrome (NS) in older Japanese people.

source: The aim of this study was to examine the causes and outcomes of NS in elderly patients who underwent renal biopsies between 2007 and 2010.
target: The goal of this study was to look at the causes and outcomes of NS in older patients that had kidney biopsies (tissue samples) between 2007 and 2010.

source: Design, setting, participants, and measurements: From July 2007 to June 2010, all of the elderly (aged ≥65 years) Japanese primary NS patients who underwent native renal biopsies and were registered in the Japan renal biopsy registry (J-RBR; 438 patients including 226 males and 212 females) were identified.
target: From a registry in Japan, we found all Japanese NS patients 65 or older who had kidney biopsies (tissue samples) from July 2007 to June 2010. This included 438 patients, 226 men and 212 women.

source: From this cohort, 61 patients [28 males and 33 females including 29, 19, 6, 4, and 3 patients with membranous nephropathy (MN), minimal change nephrotic syndrome (MCNS), focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN), and other conditions, respectively] were registered from the representative multi-centers over all districts of Japan, and analyzed retrospectively.
target: Out of these patients, we looked closer at 61 that had certain types of NS.

source: The treatment outcome was assessed using proteinuria-based criteria; i.e., complete remission (CR) was defined as urinary protein level of <0.3 g/day or g/g Cr, and incomplete remission type I (ICR-I) was defined as urinary protein level of <1.0-0.3 g/day or g/g Cr, and renal dysfunction was defined as a serum creatinine (Cr) level of 1.5 times the baseline level.
target: To know how well treatments worked, we looked at the levels of protein in the patients’ urine.

source: Results: In this elderly primary NS cohort, MN was the most common histological type of NS (54.8 %), followed by MCNS (19.4 %), FSGS (17.4 %), and MPGN (8.4 %).
target: In this group of older people with NS, more than half (58%) had a type called Membranous Nephropathy (MN). Another 19% had Minimal Change Nephrotic Syndrome (MCNS), 17% had Focal Segmental Glomerosclerosis (FSGS), and 8% had membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN).

source: Of the patients with MN, MCNS, or FSGS, immunosuppressive therapy involving oral prednisolone was performed in 25 MN patients (86.2 %), 18 MCNS patients (94.7 %), and all 6 FSGS patients (100 %).
target: A drug called prednisolone, which suppresses the immune system, was given to 86% of the patients with MN, 94% of the patients with MCNS, and all 6 patients with FSGS.

source: CR was achieved in all 19 (100 %) MCNS patients.
target: The disease was completely cured in all 19 MCNS patients.

source: In addition, CR and ICR-I were achieved in 16 (55.2 %) and 18 (62.1 %) MN patients and 4 (66.7 %) and 5 (83.3 %) FSGS patients, respectively.
target: Fifty-five percent of MN patients were completely cured and 62% were at least partially cured. Sixty-six percent of FSGS patients were completely cured and 83% were partially cured.

source: There were significant differences in the median time to CR among the MCNS, FSGS, and MN patients (median: 26 vs. 271 vs. 461 days, respectively, p < 0.001), and between the elderly (65-74 years, n = 7) and very elderly (aged ≥75 years, n = 12) MCNS patients (7 vs. 22 days, p = 0.037).
target: There were meaningful differences in how long it took for different types of NS to be cured.  The differences depended both on the type of NS (MCNS, FSGS, or MN) and whether MCNS patients were 75 or older.

source: Relapse occurred in two (6.9 %) of the MN and nine (47.4 %) of the MCNS patients.
target: The disease came back in 7% of patients with MN and 47% of patients with MCNS.

source: Renal dysfunction was observed in five (7.2 %) of the MN patients.
target: Seven percent of patients with MN had problems with kidney function.

source: Serious complications developed in eight (14.8 %) patients, i.e., two (3.7 %) patients died, four (7.4 %, including three MCNS patients) were hospitalized due to infectious disease, and two (3.7 %) developed malignancies.
target: Eight patients had serious problems. For example, two of them died, four were hospitalized for infections, and two developed malignant tumors.

source: The initiation of diabetic therapy was necessary in 14 of the 61 patients (23.0 %) with much higher initial steroid dosage.
target: Treatment for diabetes was needed for 14 of the 61 patients with higher initial steroid dosage.

source: Conclusion: Renal biopsy is a valuable diagnostic tool for elderly Japanese NS patients.
target: Conclusions we can make include the fact that kidney biopsies are valuable for diagnosing NS in older Japanese patients.

source: In this study, most of elderly primary NS patients respond to immunosuppressive therapy with favorable clinical outcomes.
target: Also, in this study, most older NS patients responded well to drugs that suppress the immune system.

source: On the other hand, infectious disease is a harmful complication among elderly NS patients, especially those with MCNS.
target: On the other hand, infections are a harmful related problem among older people with NS, especially if they have MCNS.

source: In future, modified clinical guidelines for elderly NS patients should be developed.
target: In the future, doctors should update guidelines for older NS patients.

source: Background: Few studies have examined the treatment and outcome of adult-onset minimal change nephrotic syndrome (MCNS).
target: Minimal Change Nephrotic Syndrome (MCNS) is a kidney disease that can lead to a group of symptoms called Nephrotic Syndrome (NS). Not many studies have looked at the treatment and results of MCNS that starts in adulthood.

source: We retrospectively studied 125 patients who had MCNS with onset in either adulthood or late adolescence.
target: We looked at 125 patients from other studies that had MCNS appear as a late adolescent or adult.

source: Presenting characteristics, duration of initial treatment and response to treatment, relapse patterns, complications, and long-term outcome were studied.
target: We studied how the disease appeared, how long it was treated, and how patients responded to the treatment. We also studied further problems caused by the disease and treatment, as well as long-term outcomes.

source: Study design: Case series.
target: The type of this study is to look at various cases.

source: Setting & participants: Patients with new-onset nephrotic syndrome 16 years or older and a histologic diagnosis of MCNS in 1985 to 2011 were identified from pathology records of 10 participating centers.
target: We looked at pathology reports from 1985 to 2011 from 10 participating health care centers. From these, we found patients 16 years or older with new NS and a diagnosis of MCNS confirmed by looking at tissue under a microscope.

source: Outcomes: Partial and complete remission, treatment resistance, relapse, complications, renal survival.
target: The cases had a variety of outcomes: partial and complete reversal of the disease, resistance of the disease to treatment, the disease coming back, further problems caused by the disease, and how long the kidneys functioned.

source: Results: Corticosteroids were given as initial treatment in 105 (84%) patients.
target: Eighty-four percent of patients were given corticosteroids as an initial treatment.

source: After 16 weeks of corticosteroid treatment, 92 (88%) of these patients had reached remission.
target: After 16 weeks of corticosteroids, 88% of these patients were cured.

source: Median time to remission was 4 (IQR, 2-7) weeks.
target: On average, time to reversing the disease was around 4 weeks.

source: 7 (6%) patients initially received cyclophosphamide with or without corticosteroids, and all attained remission after a median of 4 (IQR, 3-11) weeks.
target: Six percent of patients at first were given a cancer drug called cyclophosphamide, either with or without corticosteroids. All these patients were cured, in about 4 weeks on average.

source: 13 (10%) patients reached remission without immunosuppressive treatment.
target: Ten percent of patients were cured without drugs that suppress the immune system.

source: One or more relapses were observed in 57 (54%) patients who received initial corticosteroid treatment.
target: The disease returned at least once in 54% of patients that initially were given corticosteroids.

source: Second-line cyclophosphamide resulted in stable remission in 57% of patients with relapsing MCNS.
target: Cyclophosphamide used as a second-choice alternative drug cured 57% of the patients who had returning MCNS.

source: Acute kidney injury was observed in 50 (40%) patients.
target: Forty percent of the patients had serious kidney damage.

source: Recovery of kidney function occurred almost without exception.
target: Kidney function returned in almost every case.

source: Arterial or venous thrombosis occurred in 11 (9%) patients.
target: Nine percent of patients had blood clots.

source: At the last follow-up, 113 (90%) patients were in remission and had preserved kidney function.
target: At the last follow-up, 90% of the patients still had the disease reversed and had functioning kidneys.

source: 3 patients with steroid-resistant MCNS progressed to end-stage renal disease, which was associated with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis lesions on repeat biopsy.
target: Three patients with steroid-resistant MCNS continued to kidney failure. The kidney failure came along with damage to the small structures of the kidney that were seen under a microscope.

source: Limitations: Retrospective design, variable treatment protocols.
target: This study is limited because we only looked at past patients and they were not all given the same treatments and dosages.

source: Conclusions: The large majority of patients who had MCNS with onset in adulthood or late adolescence were treated with corticosteroids and reached remission, but many had relapses.
target: The first conclusion we can make is that most patients with MCNS that appeared as an adult or late adolescent were cured with corticosteroids. However, many had the disease come back.

source: Cyclophosphamide resulted in stable remission in many patients with relapses.
target: In many of the patients for whom the disease came back, treatment with cyclophosphamide kept it from coming back again.

source: Significant morbidity was observed due to acute kidney injury and other complications.
target: Significant health problems were seen from serious damage to the kidneys and other problems caused by the disease.

source: Progression to end-stage renal disease occurred in a few patients and was explained by focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.
target: In a few patients, the disease continued to kidney failure, and this could be explained by damage to the small structures of the kidney.

source: Background: Despite recent advances in immunosuppressive therapy for patients with primary nephrotic syndrome, its effectiveness and safety have not been fully studied in recent nationwide real-world clinical data in Japan.
target: New discoveries in treatments that suppress the immune system have helped patients with Nephrotic Syndrome (NS), a group of symptoms relating to the kidneys. However, the safety and effectiveness of these treatments has not been fully studied for recent cases in Japan.

source: Methods: A 5-year cohort study, the Japan Nephrotic Syndrome Cohort Study, enrolled 374 patients with primary nephrotic syndrome in 55 hospitals in Japan, including 155, 148, 38, and 33 patients with minimal change disease (MCD), membranous nephropathy (MN), focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and other glomerulonephritides, respectively.
target: A study 5 years ago followed 374 patients with NS in Japan, across 55 hospitals. It included 155 patients with Minimal Change Disease (MCD), 148 patients with membranous nephropathy (MN), 38 patients with Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and 33 patients with other types of inflammation of the small filtering structures in the kidney.

source: The incidence rates of remission and relapse of proteinuria, 50% and 100% increases in serum creatinine, end-stage kidney disease (ESKD), all-cause mortality, and other major adverse outcomes were compared among glomerulonephritides using the Log-rank test.
target: Among these diseases, we compared how often proteinuria (too much protein in the urine) was cured and came back. We also compared how often patients died, had kidney failure, or had other serious problems.

source: Incidence of hospitalization for infection, the most common cause of mortality, was compared using a multivariable-adjusted Cox proportional hazard model.
target: The most common cause of death was being hospitalized for an infection. We compared this cause among the different diseases using statistical methods.

source: Results: Immunosuppressive therapy was administered in 339 (90.6%) patients.
target: We found that treatment to suppress the immune system was given to 339 patients.

source: The cumulative probabilities of complete remission within 3 years of the baseline visit was ≥ 0.75 in patients with MCD, MN, and FSGS (0.95, 0.77, and 0.79, respectively).
target: For patients with MCD, MN, FSGS, there was more than a 75% chance the disease was completely cured within 3 years.

source: Diabetes was the most common adverse events associated with immunosuppressive therapy (incidence rate, 71.0 per 1000 person-years).
target: The most common side effect of treatments suppressing the immune system was diabetes. In each year, a person had a 71 in 1000 chance of this happening.

source: All-cause mortality (15.6 per 1000 person-years), mainly infection-related mortality (47.8%), was more common than ESKD (8.9 per 1000 person-years), especially in patients with MCD and MN.
target: Death from any cause, about half of which caused by infections, was more common than kidney failure, especially in patients with MCD and MN.

source: MCD was significantly associated with hospitalization for infection than MN.
target: MCD was more associated with hospitalization for infections than MN.

source: Conclusions: Patients with MCD and MN had a higher mortality, especially infection-related mortality, than ESKD.
target: Patients with MCD and MN were more likely to die, especially from infections, than to have kidney failure.

source: Nephrologists should pay more attention to infections in patients with primary nephrotic syndrome.
target: We also conclude that doctors should pay more attention to infections in patients with NS.

source: Background: Little population-based data exist about adults with primary nephrotic syndrome.
target: Nephrotic Syndrome (NS) is a combination of symptoms relating to the kidneys. There is not much information about how NS exists in the population.

source: Methods: To evaluate kidney, cardiovascular, and mortality outcomes in adults with primary nephrotic syndrome, we identified adults within an integrated health care delivery system (Kaiser Permanente Northern California) with nephrotic-range proteinuria or diagnosed nephrotic syndrome between 1996 and 2012.
target: We looked at kidney health, heart disease, and death in adults within Kaiser Permanente of Northern California that had symptoms of NS between 1996 and 2012.

source: Nephrologists reviewed medical records for clinical presentation, laboratory findings, and biopsy results to confirm primary nephrotic syndrome and assigned etiology.
target: Kidney specialists looked at medical records to confirm NS and determined causes.

source: We identified a 1:100 time-matched cohort of adults without diabetes, diagnosed nephrotic syndrome, or proteinuria as controls to compare rates of ESKD, cardiovascular outcomes, and death through 2014, using multivariable Cox regression.
target: For comparison, we also found a group of adults with no diabetes, NS, or proteinuria (too much protein in the urine) through 2014. This group was to compare rates of kidney failure, heart disease, and death using statistical methods.

source: Results: We confirmed 907 patients with primary nephrotic syndrome (655 definite and 252 presumed patients with FSGS [40%], membranous nephropathy [40%], and minimal change disease [20%]).
target: We confirmed 907 patients with NS. Of these, 655 definitely had either Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), Membranous Nephropathy (MN), or Minimal Change Disease (MCD). Another 252 were presumed to have one of these.

source: Mean age was 49 years; 43% were women.
target: The average age of the patients was 49, and 43% were women.

source: Adults with primary nephrotic syndrome had higher adjusted rates of ESKD (adjusted hazard ratio [aHR], 19.63; 95% confidence interval [95% CI], 12.76 to 30.20), acute coronary syndrome (aHR, 2.58; 95% CI, 1.89 to 3.52), heart failure (aHR, 3.01; 95% CI, 2.16 to 4.19), ischemic stroke (aHR, 1.80; 95% CI, 1.06 to 3.05), venous thromboembolism (aHR, 2.56; 95% CI, 1.35 to 4.85), and death (aHR, 1.34; 95% CI, 1.09 to 1.64) versus controls.
target: Adults with NS had about 20 times the rate of kidney failure compared to people with no NS. They also had about two and a half times the rate of heart-related chest pain, three times the rate of heart failure, almost twice the rate of stroke, two and a half times the rate of blood clots, and 34% higher rate of death.

source: Excess ESKD risk was significantly higher for FSGS and membranous nephropathy than for presumed minimal change disease.
target: The extra risk for kidney failure was much higher for FSGS and MN than for disease presumed to be MCD.

source: The three etiologies of primary nephrotic syndrome did not differ significantly in terms of cardiovascular outcomes and death.
target: The three causes of NS did not have significant differences in heart disease and death.

source: Conclusions: Adults with primary nephrotic syndrome experience higher adjusted rates of ESKD, cardiovascular outcomes, and death, with significant variation by underlying etiology in the risk for developing ESKD.
target: In conclusion, adults with NS have higher rates of kidney failure, heart disease, and death. The risk of kidney failure depended significantly on which underlying disease caused the NS symptoms.

source: Chylothorax is an uncommon and serious clinical condition, typically induced by trauma, either postsurgical or accidental injury, but the mechanism of chylothorax caused by nephrotic syndrome is still unclear.
target: Chylothorax is an uncommon and serious medical condition where fluid from the lymphatic system builds up around the lungs. It is typically caused by serious damage to the body, either after surgery or an accident. However, we don’t know how a cluster of kidney-related symptoms called Nephrotic Syndrome (NS) causes chylothorax.

source: Here, we report a case of primary nephrotic syndrome with membranous nephropathy (MN) in a 66-year-old man who presented with severe chylothorax.
target: In this article we describe a case of NS with a kidney disease called Membranous Nephropathy (MN) in a 66 year old man with severe chylothorax.

source: The chylothorax was managed by intercostal chest tube drainage, subcutaneous injection of enoxaparin, and treatment with anti-inflammatory agents and diuretics.
target: The chylothorax was kept under control with a drainage tube inserted into the chest, injections of a drug called enoxaparin, and treatment with anti-inflammatory drugs and diuretics, which increase urine production.

source: After treatment, the patient's pleural effusion decreased, and the chyle gradually became clear.
target: After treatment, the fluid around the lungs decreased and became clear.

source: We discuss the causes of MN with chylothorax.
target: We will talk about the causes of MN with chylothorax.

source: We considered that the hypoproteinemia changed the permeability of mucous membranes and lymphatic vessels, leading to leakage of chylous particles and chylous pleural effusion formation.
target: We thought that maybe too little protein in the blood allowed fluid from the lymphatic system to leak through tissues and cause the buildup around the lungs.

source: Chylothorax may also have been caused by severe tissue edema, edema of the lymphatic walls, and increased pressure, resulting in increased permeability or rupture of the lymphatic wall, and leakage of chylous fluid into the thoracic cavity.
target: The chylothorax may also have been caused by severe swelling and increased pressure. This could have caused fluid to seep through or rupture the walls of lymph vessels (which are similar to blood vessels) and leak into the chest cavity.

source: Because of its rarity, we hope this case report will improve clinicians' understanding of MN complications in primary nephrotic syndrome and provide suitable treatment options for future clinical reference.
target: Since MN is rare, we hope this case report will help doctors to understand problems associated with MN in patients that have NS, and that it will provide treatment options for doctors in the future.

source: Background: Steroid resistant nephrotic syndrome (SRNS), while uncommon in children, is associated with significant morbidity.
target: Steroid Resistant Nephrotic Syndrome (SRNS) is a group of kidney-related symptoms that cannot be treated with steroids. Though it is not common in children, it is associated with many serious health problems.

source: Calcineurin inhibitors (CNIs) remain the first line recommended therapy for children with non-genetic forms of SRNS, but some children fail to respond to them.
target: Immune system suppressing drugs called Calcineurin Inhibitors (CNIs) are the preferred treatment for children with SRNS that is not inherited. However, some children do not respond to these drugs.

source: Intravenous (IV) cyclophosphamide (CTX) has been shown to be effective in Asian-Indian children with difficult to treat SRNS (SRNS-DTT).
target: Another drug called Cyclophosphamide (CTX), given by IV, has been shown to be effective in Asian-Indian children with SRNS that is difficult to treat (SRNS-DTT).

source: Our study evaluated the outcome of IV CTX treatment in North American children with SRNS-DTT.
target: Our study looked at the outcome of IV CTX treatment in North American children with this disease.

source: Methods: Retrospective review of the medical records of children with SRNS-DTT treated with IV CTX from January 2000 to July 2019 at our center.
target: The study was a review of previous medical records of children with SRNS-DTT treated with IV CTX from January 2000 to July 2019 at our center.

source: Data abstracted included demographics, histopathology on renal biopsy, prior and concomitant use of other immunosuppressive agents and serial clinical/laboratory data.
target: From these records, we looked at demographics, kidney tissue samples, and the use of drugs to suppress the immune system. We also looked at notes from office visits and lab results recorded over time.

source: Primary outcome measure was attainment of complete remission (CR).
target: The main outcome that we measured was complete reversal of the disease.

source: Results: Eight children with SRNS-DTT received monthly doses (median 6; range 4-6) of IV CTX.
target: We found that 8 children with SRNS-DTT were given monthly doses of IV CTX.

source: Four (50%) went into CR, 1 achieved partial remission and 3 did not respond.
target: Four were completely cured, one was partially cured, and three did not respond to the treatment.

source: Three of the 4 responders had minimal change disease (MCD).
target: Three of the four patients that were cured had a particular type of SRNS that was caused by Minimal Change Disease (MCD).

source: Excluding the 1 child who responded after the 4th infusion, the median time to CR was 6.5 (range 0.5-8) months after completion of IV CTX infusions.
target: Except for the one child who responded to treatment after the 4th dose, the average time to reverse the disease was about six and a half months after completing all the IV CTX doses.

source: Three remain in CR at a median of 8.5 years (range: 3.7-10.5 years) after completion of CTX; one child relapsed and became steroid-dependent.
target: Three of the patients are still cured at an average of 8 and a half years after completing the CTX treatment. One child had the disease come back and had to continue taking steroids indefinitely.

source: No infections or life-threatening complications related to IV CTX were observed.
target: No infections or other life-threatening problems relating to IV CTX were seen.

source: Conclusions: IV CXT can induce long term remission in North-American children with MCD who have SRNS-DTT.
target: In conclusion, in North American children, Cyclophosphamide (CTX) given by IV can completely cure SRNS that is difficult to treat and is caused by Minimal Change Disease (MCD).

source: Background: Although venous thromboembolism is a well-known complication of nephrotic syndrome, the long-term absolute and relative risks of arterial thromboembolism, venous thromboembolism, and bleeding in adults with nephrotic syndrome remain unclarified.
target: Blood clots are a well-known problem associated with Nephrotic Syndrome (NS), a group of kidney-related symptoms. However, the risks of blood clots and bleeding in adults with NS is not clear.

source: Methods: In this matched cohort study, we identified every adult with first-time recorded nephrotic syndrome from admissions, outpatient clinics, or emergency department visits in Denmark during 1995-2018.
target: This study looked at groups of similar people with and without disease. In it, we found every adult that had NS recorded for the first time from 1995 to 2018 at admissions, outpatient clinics, and emergency department visits in Denmark.

source: Each patient was matched by age and sex with 10 individuals from the general population.
target: Each patient was matched by age and sex with 10 individuals from the general population.

source: We estimated the 10-year cumulative risks of recorded arterial thromboembolism, venous thromboembolism, and bleeding accounting for the competing risk of death.
target: We estimated the 10-year risk of recorded blood clots and bleeding, accounting for risk of death.

source: Using Cox models, we computed crude and adjusted hazard ratios (HRs) of the outcomes in patients with nephrotic syndrome versus comparators.
target: Using a statistical model, we calculated how likely the patients were to have certain outcomes compared to the general population.

source: Results: Among 3967 adults with first-time nephrotic syndrome, the 1-year risk of arterial thromboembolism was 4.2% (95% confidence interval [CI] 3.6-4.8), of venous thromboembolism was 2.8% (95% CI 2.3-3.3), and of bleeding was 5.2% (95% CI 4.5-5.9).
target: Among 3,967 adults with first-time NS, the risk of having a blood clot in an artery within one year was 4%. The risk of a blood clot in a vein in the same time was 3%, and the risk of bleeding was 5%.

source: The 10-year risk of arterial thromboembolism was 14.0% (95% CI 12.8-15.2), of venous thromboembolism 7.7% (95% CI 6.8-8.6), and of bleeding 17.0% (95% CI 15.7-18.3), with highest risks of ischemic stroke (8.1%), myocardial infarction (6.0%), and gastrointestinal bleeding (8.2%).
target: Within 10 years, the risk of a blood clot in an artery was 14%, the risk of a blood clot in a vein was 8%, and the risk of bleeding was 17%. The highest bleeding risks were of stroke, heart attack, and gastrointestinal bleeding.

source: During the first year, patients with nephrotic syndrome had increased rates of both arterial thromboembolism (adjusted HR [HRadj] = 3.11 [95% CI 2.60-3.73]), venous thromboembolism (HRadj = 7.11 [5.49-9.19]), and bleeding (HRadj = 4.02 [3.40-4.75]) compared with the general population comparators after adjusting for confounders.
target: During the first year, patients with NS had higher rates of blood clots in arteries, blood clots in veins, and bleeding than the people from the general population.

source: Conclusion: Adults with nephrotic syndrome have a high risk of arterial thromboembolism, venous thromboembolism, and bleeding compared with the general population.
target: In conclusion, adults with NS have a higher risk of blood clots, both in arteries and veins, and bleeding compared to the general population.

source: The mechanisms and consequences of this needs to be clarified.
target: We still need to figure out how this happens and what the consequences are.

source: Children with nephrotic syndrome (NS) have a number of potential risk factors for the development of acute kidney injury (AKI) including intravascular volume depletion, infection, exposure to nephrotoxic medication, and renal interstitial edema.
target: Nephrotic Syndrome (NS) is a combination of kidney-related symptoms. Children with NS are at risk for serious kidney damage for a number of reasons. These include too little blood, infection, medications, and swelling in the kidneys.

source: This study was aimed to determine the incidence of AKI in children hospitalized with a relapse of NS and its short-term outcome.
target: The aim of this study was to find out how often serious kidney damage happens in children that had NS come back, and the short-term outcome of that damage.

source: This prospective observational study was conducted from February 2017 to January 2018 at a tertiary care teaching hospital.
target: This study followed people over time and was conducted from February 2017 to January 2018 at a teaching hospital with highly specialized care.

source: A total of 54 children and adolescents (1-18 years) hospitalized with a diagnosis of NS and relapse with/or without other complications were enrolled.
target: We enrolled a total of 54 children and adolescents who were hospitalized with a diagnosis of NS that came back, with or without associated problems.

source: Clinical data and examination were recorded.
target: We recorded health-related information.

source: Children who developed AKI during the first two weeks of hospitalization were followed up till recovery or six weeks whichever was earlier to determine the outcome and factors predisposing to AKI.
target: To figure out what puts children more at risk for serious kidney damage, we followed up with children who had this problem during the first two weeks of hospitalization. We continued following up with them either until they recovered or until six weeks, whichever was earlier.

source: The mean age of the study population was 59.5 months and 35 (64.8%) patients were male.
target: The average age of people in the study was 59.5 months. Thirty-five of the patients (65%) were male.

source: Of the 54 patients hospitalized, 42 (77.8%) were admitted with infection-associated relapses while 22.2% of children had relapse alone.
target: Out of the 54 patients that were hospitalized, 42 had returning NS that was associated with infections. Twenty-two percent of children had returning NS only.

source: Diarrhea and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis were the most common infections (26.1% each) followed by urinary tract infections in 19% and pneumonia in 14.3%.
target: The most common infections were diarrhea and bacterial infections of the peritoneum, the lining of the abdominal cavity. The next most common were urinary tract infections and pneumonia.

source: Twenty-three (42.6%) children developed AKI according to the KDIGO definition and 27 (50%) using the pRIFLE classification.
target: Twenty-three or 27 children developed serious kidney damage, depending on the criteria used.

source: Infections [odds ratio (OR) 1.24] and use of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEI) (OR 2.3) were the most common predisposing factors for AKI.
target: The most common factors that increased risk of serious kidney damage were infections and the use of ACE inhibitors.

source: The mean recovery time for AKI was 7.34 days.
target: The average time for recovery from the serious kidney damage was about 7 days.

source: Development of AKI was associated with prolonged hospital stay (12.57 vs.8.55 days P <0.01) and delayed recovery.
target: Serious kidney damage was associated with longer hospital stays and delayed recovery.

source: At the end of follow-up all children recovered from AKI.
target: At the end of the followup, all children recovered from the kidney damage.

source: The incidence of AKI in children hospitalized with complications of NS is high.
target: Children hospitalized with problems associated with NS have high rates of serious kidney damage.

source: While the occurrence of these AKI episodes may appear transient, a recurrence of such episodes may be detrimental to the long-term outcome of children with NS.
target: These instances of serious kidney damage may seem to come and go, but happening repeatedly may cause long-term problems for children with NS.

source: Infections and the use of ACEI during relapses are risk factor for the occurrence of AKI.
target: Infections and the use of ACE inhibitors increases the risk of serious kidney damage.

source: Nephrotic syndrome (NS) encompasses a variety of disease processes leading to heavy proteinuria and edema.
target: Nephrotic syndrome (NS) includes a variety of underlying diseases that lead to serious proteinuria (too much protein in the blood) and edema (fluid buildup).

source: Minimal change disease (MCD) remains the most common primary cause of NS, as well as the most responsive to pharmacologic treatment with often minimal to no chronic kidney disease.
target: Minimal change disease (MCD) is still the most common cause of NS, and is the most responsive to treatment with drugs, often with little or no chronic kidney disease.

source: Other causes of NS include focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, which follows MCD, and secondary causes, including extrarenal or systemic diseases, infections, and drugs.
target: Other causes of NS include focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, which follows MCD, and secondary causes, including diseases outside the kidneys, infections, and drugs.

source: Although initial diagnosis relies on clinical findings as well as urine and blood chemistries, renal biopsy and genetic testing are important diagnostic tools, especially when considering non-MCD NS.
target: The initial diagnosis relies on signs and symptoms observed by a doctor. However, urine and blood tests, kidney tissue samples, and genetic testing are also important for diagnosis. This is especially true for NS not caused by MCD.

source: Moreover, biomarkers in urine and serum have become important areas for research in this disease.
target: Additionally, signs of disease in blood and urine have become important areas for research in this disease.

source: NS progression and prognosis are variable and depend on etiology, with corticosteroids being the mainstay of treatment.
target: The outcomes of NS and the way it progresses can vary and depend on the underlying disease. Corticosteroids are the main treatment.

source: Other alternative therapies found to be successful in inducing and maintaining remission include calcineurin inhibitors and rituximab.
target: Alternative treatments that have been found to work include calcineurin inhibitors (a class of drugs that suppress the immune system) and rituximab (Rituxan).

source: Disease course can range from recurrent disease relapse with or without acute kidney injury to end-stage renal disease in some cases.
target: The course of the disease can range from continuing to come back, with or without serious kidney damage, to kidney failure in some cases.

source: Given the complex pathogenesis of NS, which remains incompletely understood, complications are numerous and diverse and include infections, electrolyte abnormalities, acute kidney injury, and thrombosis.
target: Since the origin of NS is complicated and not completely understood, there are many associated problems. These include infections, electrolyte problems, serious kidney damage, and blood clots.

source: Pediatricians must be aware of the presentation, complications, and overall long-term implications of NS and its treatment.
target: Pediatricians should know about how NS appears and the associated problems and long-term issues of NS and its treatment.

source: Objective: The objective of this health technology policy analysis was to determine, where, how, and when physiotherapy services are best delivered to optimize functional outcomes for patients after they undergo primary (first-time) total hip replacement or total knee replacement, and to determine the Ontario-specific economic impact of the best delivery strategy.
target: The goal of this health technology policy analysis was to figure out, where, how, and when physiotherapy (PT) can best help patients after knee or hip replacement. We also hope to figure out the financial impact of the best PT strategies for Ontario.

source: The objectives of the systematic review were as follows: To determine the effectiveness of inpatient physiotherapy after discharge from an acute care hospital compared with outpatient physiotherapy delivered in either a clinic-based or home-based setting for primary total joint replacement patients.
target: Specifically, we wanted to figure out how inpatient PT (during a stay at a care facility) compared with outpatient PT, either at a clinic or at home, for patients that had joint replacements.

source: To determine the effectiveness of outpatient physiotherapy delivered by a physiotherapist in either a clinic-based or home-based setting in addition to a home exercise program compared with a home exercise program alone for primary total joint replacement patients.
target: We also wanted to see how effective outpatient PT with a therapist was, either at a clinic or at home, compared with just a home exercise program for patients that had joint replacements.

source: To determine the effectiveness of preoperative exercise for people who are scheduled to receive primary total knee or hip replacement surgery.
target: Finally, we aimed to figure out how effective it is to exercise before surgery for people who are scheduled for knee or hip replacement.

source: Conclusions: Based on the evidence, the Medical Advisory Secretariat reached the following conclusions with respect to physiotherapy rehabilitation and physical functioning 1 year after primary TKR or THR surgery: There is high-quality evidence from 1 large RCT to support the use of home-based physiotherapy instead of inpatient physiotherapy after primary THR or TKR surgery.
target: Based on the evidence, the Medical Advisory Secretariat concluded that there is good evidence to support using home-based PT instead of inpatient PT after hip or knee replacement. This was concluded with regard to function a year after the surgery.

source: There is low-to-moderate quality evidence from 1 large RCT to support the conclusion that receiving a monitoring phone call from a physiotherapist and practicing home exercises is comparable to receiving clinic-based physiotherapy and practicing home exercises for people who have had primary TKR surgery.
target: There is some evidence from one large study that getting phone call from a physiotherapist and practicing home exercises is comparable to having PT at a clinic and practicing home exercises for people who have had knee replacement.

source: However, results may not be generalizable to those who have had THR surgery.
target: However, the same might not apply to those who have hip replacement.

source: There is moderate evidence to suggest that an exercise program beginning 4 to 6 weeks before primary TKR surgery is not effective.
target: There is decent evidence that an exercise program beginning 4 to 6 weeks before knee replacement surgery is not effective.

source: Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is the gold standard treatment for end-stage knee osteoarthritis.
target: Complete knee replacement surgery is the best known treatment for severe arthritis in the knee.

source: Most patients report successful long-term outcomes and reduced pain after TKA, but recovery is variable and the majority of patients continue to demonstrate lower extremity muscle weakness and functional deficits compared to age-matched control subjects.
target: Most patients have successful long-term outcomes and reduced pain after knee replacement. However, recovery varies, and most patients continue to have weakness in the legs and less function compared to peers of the same age.

source: Given the potential positive influence of postoperative rehabilitation and the lack of established standards for prescribing exercise paradigms after TKA, the purpose of this study was to systematically review randomized, controlled studies to determine the effectiveness of postoperative outpatient care on short- and long-term functional recovery.
target: There is a potential positive influence of rehab after surgery and a lack of established standards for prescribing exercise paradigms after knee replacement. With this in mind, our goal in this review article was to look carefully at studies to determine how effective outpatient care is for regaining function short-term and long-term.

source: Nineteen studies were identified as highly relevant for the review and four categories of postoperative intervention were discussed: 1) strengthening exercises; 2) aquatic therapy; 3) balance training; and 4) clinical environment.
target: We found 19 studies that we deemed very relevant for the review. We discussed four categories of treatment after surgery: 1) strengthening exercises; 2) aquatic therapy; 3) balance training; and 4) clinical environment.

source: Optimal outpatient physical therapy protocols should include: strengthening and intensive functional exercises given through land-based or aquatic programs, the intensity of which is increased based on patient progress.
target: The best outpatient physical therapy strategies should include strengthening and intensive functional exercises, either normal or in water. The intensity of these should increase based on patient progress.

source: Due to the highly individualized characteristics of these types of exercises, outpatient physical therapy performed in a clinic under the supervision of a trained physical therapist may provide the best long-term outcomes after the surgery.
target: Since these exercises are very individualized, outpatient physical therapy done in a clinic under with a trained physical therapist may provide the best long-term outcomes after the surgery.

source: Supervised or remotely supervised therapy may be effective at reducing some of the impairments following TKA, but several studies without direct oversight produced poor results.
target: Supervised or remotely supervised therapy may be effective at reducing some of the impairments following knee replacement, but several studies without direct oversight had poor results.

source: Most studies did not accurately describe the "usual care" or control groups and information about the dose, frequency, intensity and duration of the rehabilitation protocols were lacking from several studies.
target: Most studies did not accurately describe what “usual care” was used to compare against the programs they were testing. Information about the amount, frequency, intensity and duration of the rehab protocols was also missing from several studies.

source: Background: Rehabilitation, with an emphasis on physiotherapy and exercise, is widely promoted after total knee replacement.
target: Rehab, with an emphasis on physiotherapy and exercise, is widely promoted after total knee replacement.

source: However, provision of services varies in content and duration.
target: However, how services are given varies in content and duration.

source: The aim of this study is to update the review of Minns Lowe and colleagues 2007 using systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the effectiveness of post-discharge physiotherapy exercise in patients with primary total knee replacement.
target: The aim of this study is to provide an updated summary of the research to evaluate how effective physiotherapy (PT) exercise is for patients that had knee replacements.

source: Methods: We searched MEDLINE, Embase, PsycInfo, CINAHL and Cochrane CENTRAL to October 4(th) 2013 for randomised evaluations of physiotherapy exercise in adults with recent primary knee replacement.
target: We searched several medical literature databases, up to October 4th, 2013, for studies of PT exercise in adults that had knee replacements.

source: Outcomes were: patient-reported pain and function, knee range of motion, and functional performance.
target: The outcomes we looked at were: patient-reported pain and function, knee range of motion, and functional performance.

source: Authors were contacted for missing data and outcomes.
target: We contacted the researchers for missing data and outcomes.

source: Risk of bias and heterogeneity were assessed.
target: We also looked at whether the study results could be misleading.

source: Results: Searches identified 18 randomised trials including 1,739 patients with total knee replacement.
target: Our searches found 18 studies including 1,739 patients with total knee replacement.

source: Interventions compared: physiotherapy exercise and no provision; home and outpatient provision; pool and gym-based provision; walking skills and more general physiotherapy; and general physiotherapy exercise with and without additional balance exercises or ergometer cycling.
target: The treatments that were compared were: PT exercise without care; home and outpatient care; pool and gym-based care; walking skills and more general PT; and general PT exercise with and without additional balance exercises or a stationary bike.

source: Compared with controls receiving minimal physiotherapy, patients receiving physiotherapy exercise had improved physical function at 3-4 months, SMD -0.37 (95% CI -0.62, -0.12), and pain, SMD -0.45 (95% CI -0.85, -0.06).
target: Compared with people with little or no PT, patients that got PT exercise had improved physical function at 3-4 months.

source: Benefit up to 6 months was apparent when considering only higher quality studies.
target: When considering only higher quality studies, there was a clear benefit up to 6 months.

source: There were no differences for outpatient physiotherapy exercise compared with home-based provision in physical function or pain outcomes.
target: There were no differences for outpatient PT exercise compared with home-based care in physical function or pain outcomes.

source: There was a short-term benefit favouring home-based physiotherapy exercise for range of motion flexion.
target: There was a short-term benefit in favor of home-based PT exercise for range of motion.

source: There were no differences in outcomes when the comparator was hydrotherapy, or when additional balancing or cycling components were included.
target: There were no differences in outcomes for pool-based care, or when additional balancing or cycling components were included.

source: In one study, a walking skills intervention was associated with a long-term improvement in walking performance.
target: In one study, a walking skills treatment plan was associated with a long-term improvement in walking performance.

source: However, for all these evaluations studies were under-powered individually and in combination.
target: However, for all these evaluations, studies did not have enough patients to be sure of their significance, even taken altogether.

source: Conclusion: After recent primary total knee replacement, interventions including physiotherapy and exercise show short-term improvements in physical function.
target: In conclusion, after recently having knee replacement, care including PT and exercise improve physical function short-term.

source: However this conclusion is based on meta-analysis of a few small studies and no long-term benefits of physiotherapy exercise interventions were identified.
target: However, this conclusion is based on a few small studies, and we found no long-term benefits of PT exercise.

source: Future research should target improvements to long-term function, pain and performance outcomes in appropriately powered trials.
target: Future research should focus on improving long-term function, pain and performance with larger studies.

source: Purpose: The aim of the present study was to evaluate the efficacy and safety of non-supervised home-based exercise versus individualized and supervised programs delivered in clinic-based settings for the functional recovery immediately after discharge from a primary TKA.
target: The aim of this study was to see how effective and safe different functional recovery strategies were immediately after discharge from knee replacement surgery. It compared non-supervised, home-based exercise to individualized and supervised programs delivered in clinic-based settings.

source: Methods: Medline, Embase, Cochrane, and PEDro databases were screened, from inception to April 2015, in search for randomized clinical trials (RCT) of home-based exercise interventions versus individualized and supervised outpatient physical therapy after primary TKA.
target: We searched several medical literature databases for papers published up until April 2015. We were looking for clinical trials of home-based exercise programs versus individualized and supervised outpatient physical therapy (PT) after knee replacement.

source: Target outcomes were: knee range of motion (ROM), patient-reported pain and function, functional performance, and safety.
target: The outcomes we compared were knee range of motion (ROM), patient-reported pain and function, functional performance, and safety.

source: Results: The search and selection process identified 11 RCT of moderate quality and small sample sizes.
target: Our search and selection process found 11 clinical trials of decent quality but that looked at small numbers of patients.

source: ROM active extension data suitable for meta-analysis was available from seven studies with 707 patients, and ROM active flexion from nine studies with 983 patients.
target: Seven studies, with a total of 707 patients, had data on ROM for extending the leg that could be used. Nine studies, with a total of 983 patients, had data on ROM for bending the leg.

source: Most studies showed no difference between groups.
target: Most studies showed no difference between groups.

source: Pooled differences were within the non-inferiority margin.
target: Even taken altogether, differences between the treatment types were small enough to be by chance.

source: Conclusion: Short-term improvements in physical function and knee ROM do not clearly differ between outpatient physiotherapy and home-based exercise regimes in patients after primary TKA; however, this conclusion is based on a meta-analysis with high heterogeneity.
target: In conclusion, short-term improvements in physical function and knee ROM are not clearly different between outpatient PT and home-based exercise regimes in patients after knee replacement. However, this conclusion is based on combining studies with many differences.

source: Background: Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) alleviates pain, but muscle strength and function is reduced for a long period postoperatively.
target: Complete knee replacement surgery alleviates pain, but muscle strength and function is reduced for a long period after the operation.

source: Aim: To investigate whether maximal strength training (MST) is more effective in improving muscle strength than standard rehabilitation (SR) after TKA.
target: Our aim was to investigate whether Maximal Strength Training (MST) is more effective in improving muscle strength than standard rehab knee replacement.

source: Design: A randomized, controlled study.
target: This study involved two groups who randomly got either the standard care or the experimental care.

source: Setting: Community physical therapy centers and University hospital research department.
target: It was done in community physical therapy centers and a university hospital research department.

source: Population: Forty-one adults <75 years with primary, unilateral osteoarthritis of the knee scheduled for TKA.
target: The study included 41 adults under 75 with arthritis in one knee and who were scheduled for knee replacement surgery.

source: Methods: Participants were randomized to supervised MST of the lower extremities 3 times/week for 8 weeks and physiotherapy session1/week (N.=21) or to SR, including physiotherapy sessions/telephone contact 1/week and writing home exercise logs (N.=20).
target: Participants randomly got either supervised MST for the legs 3 times a week for 8 weeks and one physiotherapy session per week or the standard rehab, including physiotherapy sessions and/or telephone contact once a week and writing home exercise logs. The MST group had 21 patients, while the standard rehab group had 20 patients.

source: Maximal strength in leg press and knee extension, 6-minute walk test, patient-reported functional outcome score and pain were assessed preoperatively, 7 days, 10 weeks and 12 months postoperatively.
target: We measured maximum strength in leg press and knee extension, 6-minute walk test, patient-reported functional outcome score and pain. We looked at these 7 days after the operation, 10 weeks after, and 12 months after.

source: Results: The MST group exceeded preoperative levels of muscle strength in leg press and knee extension by 37% and 43%, respectively at 10 weeks' follow-up, and the increase was higher than in the SR group (P≤0.001).
target: The MST group had 37% more strength in leg press and 43% more strength in knee extension at 10 weeks. These increases were higher than for the group that had standard rehab.

source: Strength differences persisted up to 12-months follow-up.
target: Strength differences continued up to the 12-month follow-ups.

source: At 12 months, both groups recovered to normative levels in the 6-Minute Walk Test, with no statistically significantly difference between the groups.
target: At 12 months, both groups recovered to normal levels in the 6-Minute Walk Test, with no significant difference between the groups.

source: Conclusions: Participants undergoing MST experienced superior increases in leg press and knee extension muscle strength compared with those managed with SR from 7-day to 10-week follow-up.
target: In conclusion, participants getting Maximal Strength Training (MST) had better increases in leg press and knee extension muscle strength compared to those who had standard rehab, as measured at 7-day to 10-week follow-ups after surgery.

source: The difference in muscle strength was maintained at 12-month follow-up.
target: The difference in muscle strength was maintained at 12-month follow-up.

source: No differences in functional performance were found at any time-point.
target: No differences in functional performance were found at any point.

source: Clinical rehabilitation impact: Exercises after TKA should be performed with high intensity and target the operated leg specifically.
target: The impact of this on clinical rehab is that exercises after knee replacement should be done with high intensity and should target the operated leg specifically.

source: This study evaluated the use of telerehabilitation during the postoperative period for patients who underwent total knee arthroplasty (TKA) or unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA).
target: This study looked at using tele-rehab (where providers interact with patients remotely using the internet) after complete or partial knee replacement surgery.

source: Specifically, this study evaluated the following: (1) patient compliance and adherence to the program, (2) time spent performing physical therapy exercises, (3) the usability of the virtual rehabilitation platform, and (4) clinical outcome scores in a selected primary knee arthroplasty cohort.
target: Specifically, the study looked at: (1) how well patients kept up with the program, (2) time spent performing physical therapy exercises, (3) how easy it was to use the virtual rehab software, and (4) outcomes for a certain group of patients that had knee replacement surgery.

source: A total of 157 consecutive patients underwent TKA (n = 18) or UKA (n = 139).
target: A total of 157 patients in a row had complete (18) or partial (139) knee replacement.

source: These patients used a telerehabilitation system with an instructional avatar, three-dimensional motion measurement and analysis software, and real-time televisit capability designed for arthroplasty patients.
target: These patients used a tele-rehab system with an instructional avatar, three-dimensional motion measurement and analysis software, and real-time tele-visit capability designed for joint surgery patients.

source: Compliance was determined by how many times the patients followed prescribed repetitions of exercises.
target: We measured how well patients kept up with the program by how many times the patients followed the recommended repetitions of exercises.

source: The total time spent performing exercises for each patient was collected.
target: We collected the total each patient time spent performing exercises.

source: The usability of the virtual rehabilitation platform (on the patient's end) was evaluated using the system usability scale (SUS) questionnaire.
target: We evaluated how easy it was for patients to use the virtual rehab software using a questionnaire.

source: The number of in-person and televisits was recorded for each patient.
target: We also recorded the number of in-person and virtual visits for each patient.

source: Patient-reported outcomes were collected through the patient and clinician interfaces and included the Knee Society Score (KSS) for pain and functions, the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) score, and Boston University Activity Measure for Post-Acute Care (AM-PAC) score.
target: We collected patient-reported outcomes through patient/doctor portals. Measurements included were the Knee Society Score (KSS) for pain and functions, the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) score, and Boston University Activity Measure for Post-Acute Care (AM-PAC) score.

source: Patients spent an average of 29.5 days partaking in the therapy.
target: Patients spent an average of about 30 days participating in the therapy.

source: TKA and UKA patients had a mean of 3.5 and 3.2 outpatient follow-up visits, each, for in-office therapy with a physical therapist, respectively.
target: Complete knee replacement patients, on average, had 3.5 outpatient follow-up visits each for in-office therapy with a physical therapist. Partial knee replacement patients had, on average, 3.2 of these visits.

source: This figure exceeded the mean number of real-time virtual patient-clinician visits by 0.8 visits per patient in the TKA cohort and by 1 visit per patient in the UKA cohort.
target: For complete knee replacement patients, this was on average 0.8 more visits than for real-time virtual patient-clinician. For partial hip replacement patients, it was 1 more visit.

source: Patients spent on average 26.5 minutes per day conducting an average of 13.5 exercises.
target: Patients spent, on average, 26.5 minutes per day doing an average of 13.5 exercises.

source: By the end of rehabilitation, patients had spent an average of 10.8 hours performing exercises, and of all the exercises performed, approximately 21 exercises were uniquely designed.
target: By the end of rehab, patients had spent an average of 10.8 hours doing exercises, and of all the exercises performed, around 21 were uniquely designed.

source: Following therapy, KSS pain and function scores improved markedly and the improvements were measured at 368% for TKA and 350% for UKA (pain) and 27% for UKA and 33% for TKA (function).
target: After therapy, KSS pain and function scores improved notably. The scores for pain improved by 368% for complete knee replacement and 350% for partial. The scores for function improved by 33% for complete knee replacement 27% for partial.

source: In addition, WOMAC scores improved by 57% and 66% for UKA and TKA patients while the improvement in AM-PAC scores was at 22% and 24%.
target: Additionally, WOMAC scores improved by 57% for partial knee replacement and 66% for compete knee replacement. The improvement in AM-PAC scores was at 22% for partial and 24% for complete.

source: This telerehabilitation platform encouraged clinician-patient interaction beyond the hospital setting and offers the advantage of cost savings, convenience, at-home monitoring, and coordination of care, all of which are geared to improve adherence and overall patient satisfaction.
target: This tele-rehab platform encouraged interaction between doctors and patients beyond the hospital setting. It offers the advantage of cost savings, convenience, at-home monitoring, and coordination of care. All of these are geared to improve how well patients keep up and overall patient satisfaction.

source: Additionally, the biometric data can be used to develop custom physical therapy regimens to assure proper rehabilitation, which is lacking in other telerehabilitation applications that use noninteractive videos that can be watched on mobile devices and tablets.
target: Additionally, the data from the platform can be used to make custom physical therapy regimens to assure proper rehabilitation. This is lacking in other tele-rehab applications that use non-interactive videos that can be watched on mobile devices and tablets.

source: Objective: Although total knee replacement (TKR) surgery is highly prevalent and generally successful, functional outcomes post-TKR vary widely.
target: Although knee replacement surgery is very common and usually turns out well, amount of function after varies widely.

source: Most patients receive some physical therapy (PT) following TKR, but PT practice is variable and associations between specific content and dose of PT interventions and functional outcomes are unknown.
target: Most patients have some physical therapy (PT) after knee replacement, but PT practice varies. We don’t know which amounts and kinds of PT are associated with which outcomes.

source: Research has identified exercise interventions associated with better outcomes but studies have not assessed whether such evidence has been translated into clinical practice.
target: Research has shown that exercise is associated with better outcomes, but studies have not looked at whether this has made its way into clinical practice.

source: We characterized the content, dose, and progression of usual post-acute PT services following TKR, and examined associations of specific details of post-acute PT with patients' 6-month functional outcomes.
target: We looked at the content, amount, and progression of usual PT services after knee replacement. We also examined associations of specific details of PT after surgery with how much function patients had after 6 months.

source: Methods: Post-acute PT data were collected from patients who were undergoing primary unilateral TKR and participating in a clinical trial of a phone-based coaching intervention.
target: We collected PT data from patients who were having knee replacement surgery on one side and participating in a clinical trial of a phone-based coaching program.

source: PT records from the terminal episode of care were reviewed and utilization and exercise content data were extracted.
target: We reviewed PT records from final sessions to find how much patients used the PT and what exercises were done.

source: Descriptive statistics and linear regression models characterized PT treatment factors and identified associations with 6-month outcomes.
target: We used statistical methods to see which parts of PT treatment were associated with 6-month outcomes.

source: Results: We analyzed 112 records from 30 PT sites.
target: In all, we looked at 112 records from 30 PT sites.

source: Content and dose of specific exercises and incidence of progression varied widely.
target: The types and amounts of exercises, and how they progressed, varied widely.

source: Open chain exercises were utilized more frequently than closed chain (median 21 [interquartile range (IQR) 4-49] versus median 13 [IQR 4-28.5]).
target: Exercises that used weights or other resistance were use more than body-weight exercises (on average 21 versus 13).

source: Median (IQR) occurrence of progression of closed and open chain exercise was 0 (0-2) and 1 (0-3), respectively.
target: Progression of exercises was used on average 0 times for exercises with resistance and 1 time for exercises that just used body weight.

source: Shorter timed stair climb was associated with greater total number of PT interventions and use and progression of closed chain exercises.
target: Climbing stairs faster was associated with more PT sessions and the use and progression of body-weight exercises.

source: Discussion: Data suggest that evidence-based interventions are underutilized and dose may be insufficient to obtain optimal outcomes.
target: The evidence suggests that treatments backed by studies are not used as much as they should be and the amount of PT may not be enough to create the best possible outcomes.

source: Background: Early ambulation with physical therapy (PT) following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) has demonstrated benefits in the literature.
target: Research has shown that having patients walk early with physical therapy (PT) after knee replacement has benefits.

source: However, the impact of early PT on rehabilitation performance and opioid consumption has not been elucidated.
target: However, the impact of early PT on rehab performance and opioid use is still unclear.

source: We evaluate the effect of same-day PT on inhospital functional outcomes and opioid consumption.
target: We looked at the effect of same-day PT on how well patients functions and opioid use while in the hospital.

source: Methods: We retrospectively identified 2 cohorts of primary TKA patients from July 2016 to December 2017: PT0 (n = 295) received PT on the day of surgery, and PT1 (n = 392) received PT on postoperative day (POD) 1.
target: To do this, we looked at 2 past groups of knee replacement patients from July 2016 to December 2017. The first group had 295 patients and had PT on the day of surgery. The second group had 392 patients and received PT on the day after surgery.
