target: Vitamin B6 deficiency (not enough vitamin B6 in the body) has been associated with immune system changes in the elderly.Vitamin B6 deficiency also affects people with HIV infections, kidney disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.

source: Future research efforts should focus on establishing the mechanism underlying the effects of vitamin B6 on immunity and should attempt to establish safe intake levels that optimize immune response.
target: Future research studies should focus on explaining exactly  how vitamin B6 affects the immune system and the right amount of vitamin B6 to take for the best immune system function.

source: The vitamins folic acid, B12 and B6 and B2 are the source of coenzymes which participate in one carbon metabolism.
target: There are several B vitamins: folic acid, B12 and B6 and B2. They are important in one carbon metabolism of protein and DNA metabolism.

source: In this metabolism, a carbon unit from serine or glycine is transferred to tetrahydrofolate (THF) to form methylene-THF.
target: Here, one carbon unit is transferred from an amino acid (building blocks of proteins) by a B vitamin, tetrahydrofolate (THF), to form a new chemical, methylene-THF.

source: This is either used as such for the synthesis of thymidine, which is incorporated into DNA, oxidized to formyl-THF which is used for the synthesis of purines, which are building blocks of RNA and DNA, or it is reduced to methyl-THF which used to methylate homocysteine to form methionine, a reaction which is catalyzed by a B12-containing methyltransferase.
target: Methylene-THF is used  in the synthesis of DNA,  RNA, or in the metabolism of important amino acids (such as the reaction that changes one amino acid, homocysteine, to another amino acid, methionine).

source: Much of the methionine which is formed is converted to S-adenosylmethionine (SAM), a universal donor of methyl groups, including DNA, RNA, hormones, neurotransmitters, membrane lipids, proteins and others.
target: Much of the methionine which is formed is made into S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) and then used in the metabolism of DNA, RNA, hormones, neurotransmitters (nerve impulse chemicals), cell lipids (fats), proteins and others.

source: Because of these functions, interest in recent years has been growing particularly in the area of aging and the possibility that certain diseases that afflict the aging population, loss of cognitive function, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, cancer and others, may be in part explained by inadequate intake or inadequate status of these vitamins.
target: Because of these functions, interest in recent years has been growing particularly in the area of aging. The possibility that certain diseases associated with aging  such as the loss of thinking, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, cancer and others, may be in part explained by inadequate intake or inadequate amounts in the body of these vitamins.

source: Homocysteine, a product of methionine metabolism as well as a precursor of methionine synthesis, was shown recently to be a risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke and thrombosis when its concentration in plasma is slightly elevated.
target: Homocysteine, an amino acid that is both needed to make methionine as well as made from methionine metabolism was shown recently to be a risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke and blood clots  when its amount in blood is slightly elevated.

source: There are now data which show association between elevated plasma homocysteine levels and loss of neurocognitive function and Alzheimer's disease.
target: Facts now show association between increased blood levels of homocysteine and loss of brain function and Alzheimer's disease.

source: These associations could be due to a neurotoxic effect of homocysteine or to decreased availability of SAM which results in hypomethylation in the brain tissue.
target: These associations could be due to harmful effects of homocysteine on the nervous system or on the decreased amount of SAM in the brain.

source: Hypomethylation is also thought to exacerbate depressive tendency in people, and for (colorectal) cancer DNA hypomethylation is thought to be the link between the observed relationship between inadequate folate status and cancer.
target: Problems with methionine metabolism are also thought to increase depressive tendency in people.These problems with methionine and DNA metabolism are also thought to be the link between the relationship seen between low levels of vitamin B folate and colorectal cancer.

source: There are many factors that contribute to the fact that the status of these vitamins in the elderly is inadequate.
target: Many factors(reasons) contribute to the fact that the amount of these vitamins in the elderly is inadequate.

source: These factors are in part physiological such as the achlorhydria which affects vitamin B12 absorption and in part socioeconomic and habitual.
target: One factor is not having enough acid in the stomach (achlorhydria). This decreases the amount of  vitamin B12 your stomach can absorb from food. Other factors include socioeconomic and habits.

source: We need more studies to confirm that these vitamins have important functions in the etiology of these diseases.
target: We need more studies to prove that these vitamins have important functions in the causes of these diseases.

source: We also need to establish if these diseases can be prevented or diminished by proper nutrition starting at a younger age.
target: We also need to prove iif these diseases can be prevented or lessened by proper nutrition starting at a younger age.

source: Background: Vitamin B6 is thought to be a most versatile coenzyme that participates in more than 100 biochemical reactions.
target: Vitamin B6 is thought to be a most versatile chemical that participates in more than 100 biochemical reactions in the body.

source: It is involved in amino acid and homocysteine metabolism, glucose and lipid metabolism, neurotransmitter production and DNA/RNA synthesis.
target: It is involved in protein and amino acid (building blocks of protein) metabolism, carbohydrate (sugar) and lipid (fat) metabolism. It is also involved in neurotransmitter (chemicals involved in nerve impulses) production and DNA/RNA synthesis.

source: Vitamin B6 can also be a modulator of gene expression.
target: Vitamin B6 can also be a chemical that regulates gene expression.

source: Nowadays, clinically evident vitamin B6 deficiency is not a common disorder, at least in the general population.
target: Currently, signs and symptoms of vitamin B6 deficiency is not a common disorder in the general population.

source: Nevertheless, a subclinical, undiagnosed deficiency may be present in some subjects, particularly in the elderly.
target: But Vitamin B6 deficiency may be present without signs or symptoms especially in the elderly.

source: Objective: This review gives a complete overview over the metabolism and interactions of vitamin B6.
target: This research give a complete overview of the actions  of vitamin B6 in the body.

source: Further, we show which complications and deficiency symptoms can occur due to a lack of vitamin B6 and possibilities for public health and supplemental interventions.
target: Researchers show which specific problems can occur due to a lack of vitamin B6. Researchers also discuss possibilities for public health and adding supplements to foods.

source: Methods: The database Medline (www.ncvi.nlm.nih.gov) was searched for terms like "vitamin B6", "pyridoxal", "cancer", "homocysteine", etc.
target: The database Medline (www.ncvi.nlm.nih.gov) was searched on the computer for terms like "vitamin B6", "pyridoxal", "cancer", "homocysteine", etc.

source: For a complete understanding, we included studies with early findings from the forties as well as recent results from 2006.
target: Researchers included studies with early findings from the forties as well as recent results from 2006 for a complete understanding.

source: These studies were summarised and compared in different chapters.
target: This research was summarised and compared in different chapters.

source: Results and conclusion: In fact, it has been proposed that suboptimal vitamin B6 status is associated with certain diseases that particularly afflict the elderly population: impaired cognitive function, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and different types of cancer.
target: Researchers have proposed that less than adequate (suboptimal) vitamin B6 levels are associated with certain diseases that particularly trouble the elderly population such as: decreased ability to think clearly, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and different types of cancer.

source: Some of these problems may be related to the elevated homocysteine concentrations associated to vitamin B6 deficiency, but there is also evidence for other mechanisms independent of homocysteine by which a suboptimal vitamin B6 status could increase the risk for these chronic diseases.
target: Some of these problems may be related to an increased homocysteine (amino acid) levels that is associated with vitamin B6 deficiency. There is also evidence for other ways suboptimal vitamin B6 levels could increase the risk for these chronic diseases.

source: Pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP), the bioactive form of vitamin B6, reportedly functions as a prosthetic group for >4% of classified enzymatic activities of the cell.
target: Pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) is the active form of vitamin B6 in the body. PLP is involved in more than 4% of the chemical reactions of proteins in the cell.

source: It is therefore not surprising that alterations of vitamin B6 metabolism have been associated with multiple human diseases.
target: Changes in vitamin B6 metabolism have been associated with multiple human diseases.

source: As a striking example, mutations in the gene coding for antiquitin, an evolutionary old aldehyde dehydrogenase, result in pyridoxine-dependent seizures, owing to the accumulation of a metabolic intermediate that inactivates PLP.
target: For example, mutations in the gene coding for antiquitin, a protein that appeared early in evolution, result in seizures. These seizures are due to an increase in a chemical (made during vitamin B6 metabolism) that stops the function of PLP.

source: In addition, PLP is required for the catabolism of homocysteine by transsulfuration.
target: PLP is also required for the metabolism of homocysteine (an amino acid involved in vitamin B metabolism)..

source: Hence, reduced circulating levels of B6 vitamers (including PLP as well as its major precursor pyridoxine) are frequently paralleled by hyperhomocysteinemia, a condition that has been associated with an increased risk for multiple cardiovascular diseases.
target: Lower blood levels of B6 vitamers (active vitamin B6 chemicals-PLP and pyridoxine) are associated with  hyperhomocysteinemia (excess blood homocysteine). Hyperhomocysteinemia is associated with a higher risk of many cardiovascular diseases.

source: During the past 30 years, an intense wave of clinical investigation has attempted to dissect the putative links between vitamin B6 and cancer.
target: During the past 30 years, a lot of research has tried to explain links between vitamin B6 and cancer.

source: Thus, high circulating levels of vitamin B6, as such or as they reflected reduced amounts of circulating homocysteine, have been associated with improved disease outcome in patients bearing a wide range of hematological and solid neoplasms.
target: High blood levels of vitamin B6, or the related lower blood levels of homocysteine, have been associated with improved disease outcome in patients with blood and solid organ neoplasms.

source: More recently, the proficiency of vitamin B6 metabolism has been shown to modulate the adaptive response of tumor cells to a plethora of physical and chemical stress conditions.
target: Recently, vitamin B6 metabolism has been shown to control tumor cells' ability to adjust to many different types of stress.

source: Moreover, elevated levels of pyridoxal kinase (PDXK), the enzyme that converts pyridoxine and other vitamin B6 precursors into PLP, have been shown to constitute a good, therapy-independent prognostic marker in patients affected by non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC).
target: Also, increased levels of pyridoxal kinase (PDXK-a protein involved in producing PLP), have been shown to predict good  outcomes in patients with non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC).

source: Here, we will discuss the clinical relevance of vitamin B6 metabolism as a prognostic factor in cancer patients.
target: This research discusses the importance of vitamin B6 metabolism in the disease outcome of cancer patients.

source: Background: Higher plasma concentrations of the vitamin B-6 marker pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) have been associated with reduced colorectal cancer (CRC) risk.
target: Higher blood levels of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) have been linked to lower risk of colorectal cancer (CRC). The level of PLP in the blood is a sign of the level of vitamin B6 in the blood.

source: Inflammatory processes, including vitamin B-6 catabolism, could explain such findings.
target: This could be explained by Inflammation, including types of vitamin B6 metabolism.

source: Objective: We investigated 3 biomarkers of vitamin B-6 status in relation to CRC risk.
target: Researchers measured chemicals that are signs (biomarkers) of the amount of vitamin B6 in blood. They  studied these 3 biomarkers In relation to the risk of CRC.

source: Design: This was a prospective case-control study of 613 CRC cases and 1190 matched controls nested within the Northern Sweden Health and Disease Study (n = 114,679).
target: This large study was done in Sweden and compared 613 participants with CRC to people without CRC.

source: Participants were followed from 1985 to 2009, and the median follow-up from baseline to CRC diagnosis was 8.2 y. PLP, pyridoxal, pyridoxic acid (PA), 3-hydroxykynurenine, and xanthurenic acids (XAs) were measured in plasma with the use of liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry.
target: The participants were followed  from 1985 to 2009. From the start of the study to the time of the CRC diagnosis was about 8 years. The  biomarkers measured in the blood were PLP, pyridoxal, pyridoxic acid (PA), 3-hydroxykynurenine (HK), and xanthurenic acids (XAs)

source: We calculated relative and absolute risks of CRC for PLP and the ratios 3-hydroxykynurenine:XA (HK:XA), an inverse marker of functional vitamin B-6 status, and PA:(PLP + pyridoxal) (PAr), a marker of inflammation and oxidative stress and an inverse marker of vitamin B-6 status.
target: Researchers calculated the risks of CRC and biomarkers for vitamin B6 status, inflammation, and oxidative stress. Oxidative stress occurs when the level of free radicals is too high compared to the level of antioxidants in the body. These biomarkers included PLP, HK:XA, and PAr (PA: (PLP + pyridoxal)).

source: Results: Plasma PLP concentrations were associated with a reduced CRC risk for the third compared with the first quartile and for PLP sufficiency compared with deficiency [OR: 0.60 (95% CI: 0.44, 0.81) and OR: 0.55 (95% CI: 0.37, 0.81), respectively].
target: Higher blood PLP levels were linked to lower CRC risk for many participants. PLP sufficiency (enough) compared to deficiency (not enough) was also linked to lower CRC risk.

source: HK: XA and PAr were both associated with increased CRC risk [OR: 1.48 (95% CI: 1.08, 2.02) and OR: 1.50 (95% CI: 1.10, 2.04), respectively] for the fourth compared with the first quartile.
target: The calculated biomarkers, HK: XA and PAr were both associated with an increased risk for getting CRC.

source: For HK:XA and PAr, the findings were mainly observed in study participants with <10.5 y of follow-up between sampling and diagnosis.
target: For HK:XA and PAr, this risk was mainly seen in participants with less than 10.5 years of follow-up between blood samples taken at the start of the study and the CRC diagnosis.

source: Conclusions: Vitamin B-6 deficiency as measured by plasma PLP is associated with a clear increase in CRC risk.
target: Vitamin B6 deficiency (blood PLP) is associated with a clear increase in CRC risk.

source: Furthermore, our analyses of novel markers of functional vitamin B-6 status and vitamin B-6-associated oxidative stress and inflammation suggest a role in tumor progression rather than initiation.
target: The other biomarkers studied (showing vitamin B6 status and vitamin B6-associated oxidative stress and inflammation), may play a role in cancer development rather than starting it.

source: The immune system is critical in preventing infection and cancer, and malnutrition can weaken different aspects of the immune system to undermine immunity.
target: The immune system is critical in preventing infection and cancer, and malnutrition can weaken different aspects of the immune system to decrease immunity.

source: Previous studies suggested that vitamin B6 deficiency could decrease serum antibody production with concomitant increase in IL4 expression.
target: Previous research suggested that vitamin B6 deficiency could decrease blood antibody production resulting in an increase in IL4 (cytokine-chemical marker of inflammation) production.

source: However, evidence on whether vitamin B6 deficiency would impair immune cell differentiation, cytokines secretion, and signal molecule expression involved in JAK/STAT signaling pathway to regulate immune response remains largely unknown.
target: Facts about whether vitamin B6 deficiency would impair immune cell function, cytokines secretion, and production of chemicals involved in (JAK/STAT signaling) a pathway that helps control the immune response are largely unknown.

source: The aim of this study is to investigate the effects of vitamin B6 deficiency on the immune system through analysis of T lymphocyte differentiation, IL-2, IL-4, and INF-Î³ secretion, and SOCS-1 and T-bet gene transcription.
target: Researchers study the effects of vitamin B6 deficiency on the immune system by studying T lymphocyte (type of white blood cell) function, the secretion of immune chemicals, and the function of certain genes.

source: We generated a vitamin B6-deficient mouse model via vitamin B6-depletion diet.
target: We made a vitamin B6-deficient mouse model by supplying a diet without enough vitamin B6.

source: The results showed that vitamin B6 deficiency retards growth, inhibits lymphocyte proliferation, and interferes with its differentiation.
target: The results showed that vitamin B6 deficiency slows down growth and inhibits lymphocyte cell production and functions.

source: After ConA stimulation, vitamin B6 deficiency led to decrease in IL-2 and increase in IL-4 but had no influence on IFN-Î³.
target: When T lymphocytes were activated,  vitamin B6 deficiency influenced the secretion of some immune chemicals.

source: Real-time PCR analysis showed that vitamin B6 deficiency downregulated T-bet and upregulated SOCS-1 transcription.
target: This study showed that vitamin B6 deficiency affected the function of certain genes.

source: Meanwhile, the appropriate supplement of vitamin B6 could benefit immunity of the organism.
target: The right amount of supplement of vitamin B6 could benefit immunity of the organism.

source: Vitamin B6 is one of the central molecules in the cells of living organisms.
target: Vitamin B6 is one of the most important molecules in the cells of living organisms.

source: Water-soluble vitamin B6 is widely present in many foods, including meat, fish, nuts, beans, grains, fruits, and vegetables.
target: Vitamin B6 dissolves in water. It is widely present in many foods, including meat, fish, nuts, beans, grains, fruits, and vegetables.

source: Additionally, B6 is present in many multivitamin preparations for adults and children and is added as a supplement to foods, power bars, and powders.
target: Vitamin B6 is present in many multivitamins for adults and children. It is added to foods, power bars, and powders.

source: There are several active compounds or vitamers which fall under the generic B6.
target: There are several active chemicals (vitamers) which fall under the types of vitamin B6.

source: These include pyridoxine alcohol, pyridoxal an aldehyde, pyridoxamine, which differs from the first two with an amine group, and a 2,5' phosphate esters.
target: These vitamin B6 vitamers include pyridoxine alcohol, pyridoxal, and pyridoxamine.

source: The major esters are the active coenzyme form and are pyridoxal 5'phosphate (PLP) and pyridoxamine 5'phosphate (PMP).
target: Other vitamin B6 vitamers include  pyridoxal 5'phosphate (PLP) and pyridoxamine 5'phosphate (PMP).

source: The primary form of B6 in meats are the esters, and the dominant plant source is pyridoxine, which is less bioavailable.
target: The main vitamers of vitamin B6 are different in meats and plants. The main vitamer in plants, pyridoxine, is less bioavailable (the amount of the substance taken up by the living organism).

source: Pyridoxine is the most common form found in multivitamins.
target: Pyridoxine is the most common form of vitamin B6 found in multivitamins.

source: As a coenzyme, B6 is involved as a co-factor in over 100 enzymatic reactions, including carbohydrate metabolism, amino acid metabolism, particularly homocysteine, gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, and lipid metabolism.
target: Vitamin B6 is involved in over 100 processes in the body including carbohydrate (sugar) metabolism, amino acid (protein) metabolism, , and lipid (fat) metabolism.

source: Vitamin B6 is also involved in the critical functioning of cells.
target: Vitamin B6 is also involved in the critical functioning of cells.

source: It plays a significant role in transamination, decarboxylation, initial steps of porphyrin synthesis.
target: It plays a significant role in many important chemical reactions in the body.

source: Pyridoxine has a role in cognitive development through neurotransmitter synthesis, immune function with interleukin-2 (IL-2) production, and hemoglobin formation.
target: Pyridoxine has a role in the development of the brain, immune function, and hemoglobin formation by helping produce important chemicals the body uses.

source: Fetal brain development requires adequate B6, and this continues throughout infancy.
target: During pregnancy, fetal brain development requires adequate B6, and this continues throughout infancy.

source: Vitamin B6 recommendations are made in accordance with age and life stage with pregnancy and breastfeeding, involving the highest recommended daily allowance.
target: The amount of vitamin B6 doctors recommend is made according to age and life stage. The highest recommended daily allowance is during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

source: Background: a large number of studies have linked vitamin B6 to inflammation and cardiovascular disease in the general population.
target: A large number of studies have linked vitamin B6 to inflammation and cardiovascular disease in the general population.

source: However, it remains uncertain whether vitamin B6 is associated with cardiovascular outcome independent of inflammation.
target: It is not known if vitamin B6 is linked to future cardiovascular disease without inflammation.

source: Methods: we measured plasma pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP), as an indicator of vitamin B6 status, at baseline in a population-based prospective cohort of 6249 participants of the Prevention of Renal and Vascular End-stage Disease (PREVEND) study who were free of cardiovascular disease.
target: Researchers measured the amount of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) in the blood at the start of a prevention study in patients without cardiovascular disease.  Levels of PLP in blood can show how much vitamin B6 is present in the body.

source: As indicators of low-grade systemic inflammation, we measured high-sensitivity C-reactive protein and GlycA; Results: median plasma PLP was 37.2 (interquartile range, 25.1-57.0) nmol/L. During median follow-up for 8.3 (interquartile range, 7.8-8.9) years, 409 non-fatal and fatal cardiovascular events (composite outcome) occurred.
target: Low-level widespread inflammation in the body was measured by blood levels of high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP) and GlyA. The blood level of PLP was about    37.2 nmol/L. These patients were followed up for about 8 years. During this time, 409 cardiovascular diseases events occurred, some were fatal.

source: In the overall cohort, log transformed plasma PLP was associated with the composite outcome, independent of adjustment for age, sex, smoking, alcohol consumption, body mass index (BMI), estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), total cholesterol:high-density lipoprotein (HDL)-cholesterol ratio, and blood pressure (adjusted hazard ratio per increment of log plasma PLP, 0.66; 95% confidence interval (CI), 0.47-0.93).
target: In these patients, the amount of PLP in the blood was linked to the cardiovascular events that occurred. This link did not depend on other risk factors like age, sex, smoking, drinking alcohol, body weight (BMI), kidney function (eGFR), cholesterol levels, or blood pressure.

source: However, adjustment for high-sensitivity C-reactive protein and GlycA increased the hazard ratio by 9% and 12% respectively, to non-significant hazard ratios of 0.72 (95% confidence interval, 0.51-1.01) and 0.74 (95% confidence interval, 0.53-1.05).
target: But, the amount of hsCRP and GlyA in the blood was linked to an increase in the risk of having these cardiovascular events occurring.

source: The association of plasma PLP with cardiovascular risk was modified by gender (adjusted Pinteraction = 0.04).
target: Also, this link of the amount of PLP in the blood and the risk of getting cardiovascular disease was affected by gender.

source: When stratified according to gender, in women the prospective association with cardiovascular outcome was independent of age, smoking, alcohol consumption, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, and GlycA (adjusted hazard ratio, 0.50, 95% confidence interval, 0.27-0.94), while it was not in men (adjusted hazard, 0.99, 95% confidence interval, 0.65-1.51).
target: In women, the link between the amount of PLP in the blood and getting cardiovascular disease did not depend on the other risk factors like age, smoking, drinking alcohol, or blood levels of hsCRP and GlyA. In men, the link was not as independent of these other risk factors.

source: Conclusions: in this population-based cohort, plasma PLP was associated with cardiovascular outcome, but this association was confounded by traditional risk factors and parameters of inflammation.
target: In these patients, the level of PLP in blood was linked to getting cardiovascular disease. This link was affected by the usual risk factors and measures of inflammation.

source: Notably, the association of low plasma PLP with high risk of adverse cardiovascular outcome was modified by gender, with a stronger and independent association in women.
target: The association of low blood levels of PLP with high risk of harmful cardiovascular outcomes wasaffected by gender. This association was stronger and independent in women.

source: We have reported that a subpopulation of patients with schizophrenia have lower levels of vitamin B6 (VB6) in peripheral blood than do healthy controls.
target: Research has shown that some patients with schizophrenia  have lower levels of vitamin B6 in the blood than healthy people.

source: In a previous study, we found that VB6 level was inversely proportional to the patient's positive and negative symptom scale (PANSS) score for measuring symptom severity, suggesting that the loss of VB6 might contribute to the development of schizophrenia symptoms.
target: A study showed that lower vitamin B6 levels increased symptoms of schizophrenia. Higher vitamin B6 levels decreased symptoms of schizophrenia.When measuring the symptoms of schizophrenia, the loss of vitamin B6 might help cause the symptom of schizophrenia.

source: In the present study, to clarify the relationship between VB6 deficiency and schizophrenia, we generated VB6-deficient (VB6(-)) mice through feeding with a VB6-lacking diet as a mouse model for the subpopulation of schizophrenia patients with VB6 deficiency.
target: To study the relationship between vitamin B6 and schizophrenia, research was done on mice fed a diet without vitamin B6 . This made a mouse model of vitamin B6 deficiency.

source: After feeding for 4 weeks, plasma VB6 level in VB6(-) mice decreased to 3% of that in control mice.
target: After 4 weeks of that diet, blood levels of vitamin B6  in these mice decreased to 3% of the healthy mice.

source: The VB6(-) mice showed social deficits and cognitive impairment.
target: The vitamin B6 deficiency mice showed social and thinking (behavior) problems.

source: Furthermore, the VB6(-) mice showed a marked increase in 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG) in the brain, suggesting enhanced noradrenaline (NA) metabolism in VB6(-) mice.
target: Also in the vitamin B6 deficiency mouse model, mice showed a high increase in a chemical that suggests increased metabolism of a hormone (noradrenaline (NA)) for flight-or-flight in the brain.

source: We confirmed the increased NA release in the prefrontal cortex (PFC) and the striatum (STR) of VB6(-) mice through in vivo microdialysis.
target: We proved that certain parts of the brain released this increased NA in vitamin B6 deficiency mice.

source: Moreover, inhibiting the excessive NA release by treatment with VB6 supplementation into the brain and Î±2A adrenoreceptor agonist guanfacine (GFC) suppressed the increased NA metabolism and ameliorated the behavioral deficits.
target: Feeding the mice vitamin B6 supplements decreases the excess NA released by the brain. Treating the mice with a  specific drug, guanfacine (GFC), decreased the NA metabolism. Doing these things helped to correct the behavior problems in the vitamin B6 deficiency mice.

source: These findings suggest that the behavioral deficits shown in VB6(-) mice are caused by enhancement of the noradrenergic (NAergic) system.
target: This research suggests that the behavior problems in the vitamin B6 deficiency mice are caused by an increase in the noradrenergic (NAergic) system.

source: There is little evidence regarding the association between serum vitamin B6 concentration and subsequent mortality.
target: There is little evidence about the connection beween blood levels of vitamin B6 and later mortality (death rate).

source: We aimed to evaluate the association of serum vitamin B6 concentration with all-cause, cardiovascular disease (CVD), and cancer mortality in the general population using data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES).
target: Researchers studied the connection between blood levels of vitamin B6 levels and mortality from all causes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer using facts collected from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES).

source: Our study examined 12,190 adults participating in NHANES from 2005 to 2010 in the United States.
target: Our study examined 12,190 adults participating in NHANES from 2005 to 2010 in the United States.

source: The mortality status was linked to National Death Index (NDI) records up to 31 December 2015.
target: Mortality was linked to National Death Index (NDI) records up to 31 December 2015.

source: Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) is the biologically active form of vitamin B6.
target: The chemical, pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP), is the active form of vitamin B6 in the body.

source: Vitamin B6 status was defined as deficient (PLP < 20 nmol/L), insufficient (PLP â‰¥ 20.0 and <30.0 nmol/L), and sufficient (PLP â‰¥ 30.0 nmol/L).
target: Vitamin B6 blood levels were defined as deficient (not enough), insufficient (low), and sufficient (enough) depending on the level of PLP in the blood..

source: We established Cox proportional-hazards models to estimate the associations of categorized vitamin B6 concentration and log-transformed PLP concentration with all-cause and cause-specific mortality by calculating hazard ratios (HRs) and 95% confidence intervals (95%CIs).
target: Researchers created a model to accurately estimate the risk of death (Cox model) between blood levels of vitamin B6 and PLP and mortality from all causes and from specific causes.

source: In our study, serum vitamin B6 was sufficient in 70.6% of participants, while 12.8% of the subjects were deficient in vitamin B6.
target: This study showed vitamin B6 was sufficient in 70.6% of participants. But, 12.8% of participants had deficient vitamin B6 blood levels.

source: During follow-up, a total of 1244 deaths were recorded, including 294 cancer deaths and 235 CVD deaths.
target: During follow-up, a total of 1244 deaths were recorded, including 294 cancer deaths and 235 cardiovascular disease deaths.

source: After multivariate adjustment in Cox regression, participants with higher serum vitamin B6 had a 15% (HR = 0.85, 95% CI = 0.77, 0.93) reduced risk of all-cause mortality and a 19% (HR = 0.81, 95%CI = 0.68, 0.98) reduced risk for CVD mortality for each unit increment in natural log-transformed PLP.
target: Participants with higher blood vitamin B6 had a 15% lower risk of all-cause mortality and a 19% lower risk of cardiovascular disease mortality for each unit level of PLP (using the Cox model).

source: A higher log-transformed PLP was not significantly associated with a lower risk for cancer mortality.
target: Higher PLP blood levels were not significantly associated with a lower risk of cancer mortality (using the Cox model).

source: Compared with sufficient vitamin B6, deficient (HR = 1.37, 95% CI = 1.17, 1.60) and insufficient (HR = 1.19, 95%CI = 1.02, 1.38) vitamin B6 level were significantly associated with a higher risk for all-cause mortality.
target: Compared with sufficient vitamin B6 levels, deficient and insufficient vitamin B6 levels were significantly associated with a higher risk for all-cause mortality (Cox model).

source: There was no significant association for cause-specific mortality.
target: There was no significant association for cause-specific mortality.

source: Participants with higher levels of vitamin B6 had a lower risk for all-cause mortality.
target: Participants with higher levels of vitamin B6 had a lower risk for all-cause mortality.

source: These findings suggest that maintaining a sufficient level of serum vitamin B6 may lower the all-cause mortality risk in the general population.
target: These findings suggest that maintaining a sufficient level of  vitamin B6 in the blood may lower the all-cause mortality risk in the general population.

source: Achondroplasia, the most common form of human dwarfism is a rare condition that occurs in approximately 1:20,000 births.
target: Achondroplasia, the most common form of human dwarfism is a sporadic autosomal dominant condition that occurs in approximately 1:20,000 births.

source: The major clinical outcome of Achondroplasia is attenuated growth, rhizomelic shortening of the long bones and craniofacial abnormalities.
target: The major health impact of Achondroplasia is reduced growth, shortening of the long bones and abnormalitiesin the bones of the head and face.

source: As of today there is no pharmacological treatment for Achondroplasia.
target: As of today there is no drug treatment for Achondroplasia.

source: Some improvement in the patients well being and daily function can be achieved by a surgical limb lengthening procedure.
target: Some improvement in the patients well being and daily function can be achieved by a surgical limb lengthening procedure.

source: Growth hormone treatment seems to have only modest short term success and to lack long term benefits.
target: Growth hormone treatment seems to have only modest short term success and to lack long term benefits.

source: Achondroplasia results from a single point mutation in Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor 3 (FGFR3).
target: Achondroplasia results from a mutation in Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor 3 (FGFR3).

source: In 97% of the patients, there is a Glycine to Arginine substitution at position 380 within the FGFR-3 transmembrane domain leading to receptor overactivation.
target: In 97% of the patients, the mutation leads to overactivation of the protein coded for by the FGFR3 gene.

source: This FGF receptor tyrosine kinase is expressed by chondrocytes in the growth plate of developing long bones and plays a crucial role in bone growth.
target: This protein is found in cells in the growth area of developing long bones and plays a crucial role in bone growth.

source: Genetic disruption of the FGFR-3 gene in mice leads to a remarkable increase in the length of the vertebral column and long bones.
target: Disruption of the FGFR-3 gene in mice leads to a remarkable increase in the length of the vertebral column and long bones.

source: This suggests that overaction of FGFR3 signaling may specifically impair chondrocyte function within the epiphyseal growth plates and cause Achondroplasia.
target: This suggests that overaction of FGFR3 impairs cell function in the growth regions of the bone and causes Achondroplasia.

source: Reconstituted normal bone growth may therefore be achieved by attenuation of FGFR3 signaling in the appropriate cells within the growth plate.
target: Normal bone growth may therefore be achieved by reducing FGFR3 activity in the appropriate cells within the growth region of the bones.

source: It is highly conceivable that drug development strategies aimed either towards blocking extracellular ligand binding or towards intracellular checkpoints along the FGF signal transduction cascade, may prove successful in the treatment of Achondroplasia.
target: It is highly conceivable that drug development strategies aimed either towards blocking overaction of the FGFR3 and related activities may prove successful in the treatment of Achondroplasia.

source: This review focuses on the possible approaches for developing a drug for Achondroplasia and related skeletal disorders, using chemical, biochemical and molecular strategies.
target: This review focuses on the possible approaches for developing a drug for Achondroplasia and related bone diseases.

source: Purpose of review: A summary of management and current research in achondroplasia (OMIM 100800).
target: The purpose of this review is to summarize current research and care for people with achondoplasia (a form of dwarfism).

source: The most common nonlethal skeletal dysplasia, achondroplasia presents a distinct clinical picture evident at birth.
target: Achondroplasia is the most common nonlethal bone growth impairment and its characteristics can be seen at birth.

source: Substantial information is available concerning the natural history of this dwarfing disorder.
target: A lot of information is available about how achondroplasia progresses over time.

source: Diagnosis is made by clinical findings and radiographic features.
target: Achondroplasia is diagnosed by a medical exam and X-rays.

source: Characteristic features include short limbs, a relatively large head with frontal bossing and midface hypoplasia, trident hands, muscular hypotonia, and thoracolumbar kyphosis.
target: Achondroplasia is characterised by features such as short limbs and a relatively large head.

source: Children commonly have recurrent ear infections, delayed motor milestones, and eventually develop bowed legs and lumbar lordosis.
target: Children commonly have ear infections, delayed walking, and eventually develop bowed legs and a curve in the back.

source: People with achondroplasia are generally of normal intelligence.
target: People with achondroplasia are generally of normal intelligence.

source: Recent findings: The genetic cause of achondroplasia was discovered in 1994.
target: The genetic cause of achondroplasia was discovered in 1994.

source: Subsequent research efforts are designed to better characterize the underlying possible biochemical mechanisms responsible for the clinical findings of achondroplasia as well as to develop possible new therapies and/or improve intervention.
target: Since then, research efforts aim to understand the mechanisms that lead to achondroplasia as well as to develop and improve treatments.

source: Summary: Establishing a diagnosis of achondroplasia allows families and clinicians to provide anticipatory care for affected children.
target: Diagnosis for achondroplasia allows families and doctors to plan future care for affected children.

source: Although the primary features of achondroplasia affect the skeleton, a multidisciplinary approach to care for children with achondroplasia helps families and clinicians understand the clinical findings and the natural history of achondroplasia in order to improve the outcome for each patient.
target: Coordinating care between professionals from different disciplines helps each child with achodroplasia get the best treatment.

source: The most frequent type of rhizomelic dwarfism, achondroplasia (ACH), is caused by mutations in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) gene.
target: The most frequent type of dwarfism, achondroplasia (ACH), is caused by mutations in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) gene.

source: Mutations in FGFR3 result in skeletal dysplasias of variable severity, including mild phenotypic effects in hypochondroplasia (HCH), severe phenotypic effects in thanatophoric dysplasia types I (TDI) and II (TDII), and severe but survivable phenotypic effects in severe achondroplasia with developmental delay and acanthosis nigricans (SADDAN).
target: Mutations in the FGFR3 result in bone growth impairments of variable severity  including hypochondroplasia (HCH), thanatophoric dysplasia types I (TDI) and II (TDII and severe achondroplasia with developmental delay and acanthosis nigricans (SADDAN).

source: To explore the molecular mechanisms that result in the different phenotypes, we investigated the kinetics of mutated versions of FGFR3.
target: To explore the molecular mechanisms that result in the different bone growth impairments and with varying severity, the authors investigated the properties of mutated versions of FGFR3.

source: First, we assayed the phosphorylation states of the mutated FGFR3s and found that the level of phosphorylation in TDI-FGFR3 was lower than in ACH-FGFR3, although the other mutants were phosphorylated according to phenotypic severity.
target: First, the authors checked for addition of a phosphate group to FGFR3. Attachement of phosphate groups can change the properites of the receptor and where it is located in the cell. They found that different parts of the receptor had phosphate groups depending on the type of mutation.

source: Second, we analyzed the duration of the phosphorylation.
target: Second, the authors looked for how long the phosphate groups were present in the mutated FGFR3 receptor.

source: TDI-FGFR3 was not highly phosphorylated under ligand-free conditions, but the peak phosphorylation levels of TDI-FGFR3 and ACH-FGFR3 were maintained for 30 min after stimulation with FGF-1.
Moreover, ligand-dependent phosphorylation of TDI-FGFR3, but not ACH-FGFR3, lasted for more than 8 h after FGF-1 administration.
The other mutant proteins showed sustained phosphorylation independent of ligand presence.
target: Generally, the mutated proteins showed longer presence of the phosphate group on FGFR3.

source: Third, we investigated the intracellular localization of the mutant proteins.
target: Third, the authors investigated where in the cell the mutated proteins were located.

source: Immunofluorescence analysis showed accumulations of TDII-FGFR3, SADDAN-FGFR3, and a portion of TDI-FGFR3 in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER).
target: Image analysis of the cells showed that mutated FGFR3 was in the part of the cell where proteins are made.

source: Based on these data, we concluded that sustained phosphorylation of FGFR3 causes chondrodysplasia, and the phenotypic severity depends on the proportion of ER-localized mutant FGFR3.
target: Based on these data, the authors conclude that sustained presence of the phosphate group of FGFR3 causes bone growth impairment and the severity depends on the proportion of the mutated FGFR3 present in the region of the cell involved in synthesizing proteins.

source: In FGFR3 signaling, the transcription factor, signal transducer and activator of transcription 1 (STAT1) inhibit proliferation and induce apoptosis of chondrocytes.
target: FGFR3 signaling, activates another protein called STAT1 and causes cartilage cells to stop dividing and dying.

source: Here we reveal that phospholipase C gamma (PLCgamma) mediates FGFR3-induced STAT1 activation.
target: Phospholipase gamma (PLCgamma) mediates the activation of STAT1 by FGFR3.

source: Both PLCgamma and STAT1 were activated by FGFR3 signaling, but a dominant-negative form of PLCgamma (DN-PLCgamma) remarkably reduced STAT1 phosphorylation.
Apoptosis assays revealed that the constitutively active forms of FGFR3 (TDII-FGFR3) and STAT1 (STAT1-C) induce apoptosis of chondrogenic ATDC5 cells via caspase activity.
DN-PLCgamma reduced the apoptosis of ATDC5 cells expressing TDII-FGFR3, but over-expression of both DN-PLCgamma and STAT1-C induced apoptosis.
Therefore, we conclude that a PLCgamma-STAT1 pathway mediates apoptotic signaling by FGFR3.
target: The authors conclude that a  FGFR3 and two proteins, PLCgamma and STAT1 are responsible for cell death of cartiliage cells.

source: This review focuses on the rheumatological features of achondroplasia, which is the most common skeletal dysplasia and the most frequent cause of short-limbed dwarfism.
target: This review focuses on the muscle and bone-related features of achondroplasia, which is the most common bone growth impairment and the most frequent cause of short-limbed dwarfism.

source: It is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner but results in the majority of cases of de novo mutations.
target: The gene that causes achondroplasia affects many members of a family but in the majority of cases is due to a new mutation.

source: The disease is related to a mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor-3 (FGFR3) gene encoding one member of the FGFR subfamily of tyrosine kinase receptors, which results in constitutive activation of the receptor.
target: The diseases is related to a mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor-3 (FGFR3) gene and results in a continuously active receptor.

source: Biochemical studies of FGFR3 combined with experiments in knock-out mice have demonstrated that FGFR3 is a negative regulator of chondrocytes proliferation and differentiation in growth plate.
target: Experiments show that FGFR3 prevents bone growth by preventing cartilage cells in the bone growth region from growing and maturing.

source: This mutation induces a disturbance of endochondral bone formation.
target: The mutation responsible for achondroplasia prevents growth of long bones.

source: The diagnosis of achondroplasia is based on typical clinical and radiological features including short stature, macrocephaly with frontal bossing, midface hypoplasia and rhizomelic shortening of the limbs.
target: The diagnosis of achondroplasia is based on physical and X-ray features including short stature, large head with a prominent forehead, underdevelopment of the middle part of the face and shortening of the limbs.

source: The most common rheumatological complications of achondroplasia are medullar and radicular compressions due to spinal stenosis and deformities of the lower limbs.
target: The most common bone-related complications of achondroplasia are  shortening of the spine and deformities of the lower limbs.

source: Current treatment and future therapies are discussed.
target: Current treatment and future therapies are discussed.

source: Achondroplasia (MIM 100800) is the most common non-lethal skeletal dysplasia.
target: Achondroplasia is a type of dwarfism and the most common nonlethal bone growth impairment.

source: Its incidence is between one in 10,000 and one in 30,000.
target: Achondroplasia affects between one in 10,000 and 30,000 people.

source: The phenotype is characterized by rhizomelic disproportionate short stature, enlarged head, midface hypoplasia, short hands and lordotic lumbar spine, associated with normal cognitive development.
target: Achondroplasia is characterized by short stature, shortened limbs, enlarged head, disproportionate face, curvature in the lower back and is associated with normal cognitive development.

source: This autosomal-dominant disorder is caused by a gain-of-function mutation in the gene encoding the type 3 receptor for fibroblast growth factor (FGFR3); in more than 95% of cases, the mutation is G380R.
target: A mutation in a gene called type 3 receptor for fibroblast growth factor (FGFR3) causes achondroplasia.

source: The diagnosis is suspected on physical examination and confirmed by different age-related radiological features.
target: Achondroplasia is diagnosed by physical examination and confirmed by X-ray.

source: Anticipatory and management care by a multidisciplinary team will prevent and treat complications, including cervical cord compression, conductive hearing loss and thoracolumbar gibbosity.
target: A person with achondroplasia is cared for by a multidisciplinary team that will prevent and treat complications, including spinal cord squeezing, hearing loss and development of a hump in the back.

source: Weight counselling, psychosocial guidance and professional integration programmes play an important role in the global quality of life of these patients and their families.
target: Weight counselling, psychosocial guidance and professional integration programmes play an important role in the global quality of life of these patients and their families.

source: Fibroblast growth factors (FGFs) and their receptors (FGFRs) play significant roles in vertebrate organogenesis and morphogenesis.
target: Fibroblast growth factors (FGFs) and their receptors (FGFRs) play significant roles in vertebrate organ development.

source: FGFR3 is a negative regulator of chondrogenesis and multiple mutations with constitutive activity of FGFR3 result in achondroplasia, one of the most common dwarfisms in humans, but the molecular mechanism remains elusive.
target: FGFR3 prevents cartilage formation and some mutations with continuously active FGFR3 result in achondroplasia, a common dwarfism in humans, but the molecular mechanisms remain elusive.

source: In this study, we found that chondrocyte-specific deletion of BMP type I receptor a (Bmpr1a) rescued the bone overgrowth phenotype observed in Fgfr3 deficient mice by reducing chondrocyte differentiation.
target: In this study, the authors found that removing BMP type I receptor a (Bmpr1a) from cartilage cells rescued the bone overgrowth observed in Fgfr3 deficient mice by reducing differentiation of cartilage cells.

source: Consistently, using in vitro chondrogenic differentiation assay system, we demonstrated that FGFR3 inhibited BMPR1a-mediated chondrogenic differentiation.
target: Consistently, using a cartilage cell differentiation assay system, the authors demonstrated that FGFR3 inhibited BMPR1a-mediated maturation of cartilage cells.

source: Furthermore, we showed that FGFR3 hyper-activation resulted in impaired BMP signaling in chondrocytes of mouse growth plates.
target: Furthermore, they showed that FGFR3 hyper-activation resulted in impaired BMP signaling in cartilage cells of mouse growth plates.

source: We also found that FGFR3 inhibited BMP-2- or constitutively activated BMPR1-induced phosphorylation of Smads through a mechanism independent of its tyrosine kinase activity.
target: The authors also found that FGFR3 inhibited BMP-2- or constitutively activated BMPR1-induced phosphate group addition of Smads through a mechanism independent of its enzyme activity.

source: We found that FGFR3 facilitates BMPR1a to degradation through Smurf1-mediated ubiquitination pathway.
target: They found that FGFR3 facilitates BMPR1a to through a common protein breakdown pathway called the ubiquitination pathway. Smurf1 played a role in this protein breakdown pathway.

source: We demonstrated that down-regulation of BMP signaling by BMPR1 inhibitor dorsomorphin led to the retardation of chondrogenic differentiation, which mimics the effect of FGF-2 on chondrocytes and BMP-2 treatment partially rescued the retarded growth of cultured bone rudiments from thanatophoric dysplasia type II mice.
target: They demonstrated that decreasing of BMP signaling by BMPR1 inhibitor dorsomorphin led to the retardation of cartilage cell maturation, which mimics the effect of FGF-2 on cartilage cells. BMP-2 treatment partially rescued the retarded growth of cultured bone rudiments from thanatophoric dysplasia type II mice.

source: Our findings reveal that FGFR3 promotes the degradation of BMPR1a, which plays an important role in the pathogenesis of FGFR3-related skeletal dysplasia.
target: Their findings reveal that FGFR3 promotes the degradation of BMPR1a, and this plays an important role in the development of FGFR3-related bone growth impairment.

source: Achondroplasia is a human bone genetic disorder of the growth plate and is the most common form of inherited disproportionate short stature.
target: Achondroplasia is a human bone growth impairment and is the most common form of inherited disproportionate short stature.

source: It is inherited as an autosomal dominant disease with essentially complete penetrance.
target: Achondroplasia tends to run in families, an affected parent has a 50% chance of passing it to their child.

source: Of these most have the same point mutation in the gene for fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) which is a negative regulator of bone growth.
target: Most people with achondroplasia have a mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) gene.

source: The clinical and radiological features of achondroplasia can easily be identified; they include disproportionate short stature with rhizomelic shortening, macrocephaly with frontal bossing, midface hypoplasia, lumbar hyperlordosis, and a trident hand configuration.
target: Characteristics of achodroplasia can be easily identified from physical exams and X-rays; they include disproportionate short stature with limb shortening, large head with prominence of forehead, underdevelopment of the middle part of the face and a short stubby hands with a separation betweem the middle and ring fingers.

source: The majority of achondroplasts have a normal intelligence, but many social and medical complications may compromise a full and productive life.
target: The majority of people with achondroplasia have normal intelligence, but many social and medical complications may compromise a full and productive life.

source: Some of them have serious health consequences related to fluid build up in the brain, head and neck region shortening, or blockage in the upper breathing passage..
target: Some of them have serious health consequences related to hydrocephalus, craniocervical junction compression, or upper-airway obstruction.

source: In this article, we discuss a number of treatments from the surgical limb lengthening approach and the Recombinant Growth Hormone (rhGH) treatment, to future treatments, which include the Natriuretic Peptide C-type (CNP).
target: In this article, we discuss a number of treatments from surgical limb lengthening and Growth Hormone treatment, to future treatments, including therapy using a bone formation stimulator called C-type natriuretic peptide.

source: The discussion is a comparative study of the complications and drawbacks of various experiments using numerous strategies.
target: The discussion is a comparative study of the complications and drawbacks of various experiments using numerous strategies.

source: Autosomal dominant mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) cause achondroplasia (Ach), the most common form of dwarfism in humans, and related chondrodysplasia syndromes that include hypochondroplasia (Hch), severe achondroplasia with developmental delay and acanthosis nigricans (SADDAN), and thanatophoric dysplasia (TD).
target: Mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) cause achondroplasia (Ach), the most common form of dwarfism in humans and related chondrodysplasia syndromes that include hypochondroplasia (Hch), severe achondroplasia with developmental delay and acanthosis nigricans (SADDAN), and thanatophoric dysplasia (TD). The mutations are passed on nonsex chromosomes and causes the child that inherits the mutation from an affected parent to have a 50% chance to be affected.

source: FGFR3 is expressed in chondrocytes and mature osteoblasts where it functions to regulate bone growth.
target: FGFR3 is expressed in cartilage cells and mature bone-forming cells where it functions to regulate bone growth.

source: Analysis of the mutations in FGFR3 revealed increased signaling through a combination of mechanisms that include stabilization of the receptor, enhanced dimerization, and enhanced tyrosine kinase activity.
target: Analysis of the mutations in FGFR3 revealed increased signaling through a combination of mechanisms that include stabilization of the receptor, enhanced binding of two receptors to each other, and enhanced enzyme activity of the receptor.

source: Paradoxically, increased FGFR3 signaling profoundly suppresses proliferation and maturation of growth plate chondrocytes resulting in decreased growth plate size, reduced trabecular bone volume, and resulting decreased bone elongation.
target: Paradoxically, increased FGFR3 signaling profoundly suppresses cell multiplication and maturation of growth plate cartilage cells resulting in decreased growth region size, reduced volume of the ends of long bones, and resulting decreased bone elongation.

source: In this review, we discuss the molecular mechanisms that regulate growth plate chondrocytes, the pathogenesis of Ach, and therapeutic approaches that are being evaluated to improve endochondral bone growth in people with Ach and related conditions.
target: In this review, authors discuss the molecular mechanisms that regulate growth region cartilage cells, development of Achondroplasia (Ach), and therapeutic approaches that are being evaluated to improve bone growth in people with Ach and related conditions.

source: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3)  mutations cause dwarfisms, including achondroplasia (ACH) and thanatophoric dysplasia (TD).
target: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) gain-of-function mutations cause dwarfisms, including achondroplasia (ACH) and thanatophoric dysplasia (TD).

source: The constitutive activation of FGFR3 disrupts the normal process of skeletal growth.
target: The continuously active state of FGFR3 disrupts normal process of bone growth.

source: Bone-growth anomalies have been identified in skeletal ciliopathies, in which primary cilia (PC) function is disrupted.
target: Bone-growth abnormalities have been identified in which primary cilia (PC) function is disrupted. Primary cilia are long protrusions on cells that act as an antenna and are important during development.

source: In human ACH and TD, the impact of FGFR3 mutations on PC in growth plate cartilage remains unknown.
target: In human ACH and TD, the impact of FGFR3 mutations on PC in growth plate cartilage remains unknown.

source: Here we showed that in chondrocytes from human (ACH, TD) and mouse Fgfr3Y367C/+ cartilage, the constitutively active FGFR3 perturbed PC length and the sorting and trafficking of intraflagellar transport (IFT) 20 to the PC.
target: Here, the authors showed that in cartilage cells from ACH and TD patients and mice with Fgfr3 mutation, the FGFR3 is overactive and it perturbed PC length and formation.

source: We demonstrated that inhibiting FGFR3 with FGFR inhibitor, PD173074, rescued both PC length and IFT20 trafficking.
target: They demonstrated that inhibiting FGFR3, using a drug inhibitor, rescued both PC length and formation.

source: We also studied the impact of rapamycin, an inhibitor of mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway.
target: The authors also studied the impact of a drug inhibitor of a signaling pathway.

source: Interestingly, mTOR inhibition also rescued PC length and IFT20 trafficking.
target: Interestingly, the drug also rescued PC length and formation.

source: Together, we provide evidence that the growth plate defects ascribed to FGFR3-related dwarfisms are potentially due to loss of PC function, and these dwarfisms may represent a novel type of skeletal disorders with defective ciliogenesis.
target: Together, we provide evidence that the growth plate defects ascribed to FGFR3-related dwarfisms are potentially due to loss of PC function, and these dwarfisms may represent a novel type of bone growth disorders with defective cilia formation.

source: Cilia project from almost every cell integrating extracellular cues with signaling pathways.
target: Cilia are projections present in almost every cell. Cilia integrate signals from outside the cells and those within the cells.

source: Constitutive activation of FGFR3 signaling produces the skeletal disorders achondroplasia (ACH) and thanatophoric dysplasia (TD), but many of the molecular mechanisms underlying these phenotypes remain unresolved.
target: Continuous activation of FGFR3 signaling produces the bone growth disorders achondroplasia (ACH) and thanatophoric dysplasia (TD), but many of the molecular mechanisms underlying these remain unresolved.

source: Here, we report in vivo evidence for significantly shortened primary cilia in ACH and TD cartilage growth plates.
target: Here, the authors report that primary cilia in ACH and TD cartilage growth regions are significantly shorter.

source: Using in vivo and in vitro methodologies, our data demonstrate that transient versus sustained activation of FGF signaling correlated with different cilia consequences.
target: Data demonstrate that transient versus continuous activation of fibrblast growth factor (FGF) signaling correlates with size of the cilia.

source: Transient FGF pathway activation elongated cilia, while sustained activity shortened cilia.
target: Transient FGF pathway activation elongated cilia, while continuous activity shortened cilia.

source: FGF signaling extended primary cilia via ERK MAP kinase and mTORC2 signaling, but not through mTORC1.
target: FGF signaling extended primary cilia via  a pathway involving ERK MAP kinase and mTORC2, but not through mTORC1.

source: Employing a GFP-tagged IFT20 construct to measure intraflagellar (IFT) speed in cilia, we showed that FGF signaling affected IFT velocities, as well as modulating cilia-based Hedgehog signaling.

Our data integrate primary cilia into canonical FGF signal transduction and uncover a FGF-cilia pathway that needs consideration when elucidating the mechanisms of physiological and pathological FGFR function, or in the development of FGFR therapeutics.
target: Authors uncover a FGF-cilia pathway that is involved in the mechanisms of FGFR function, and should be considered in the development of therapies for FGFR function.

source: OBJECTIVES. To establish a database of literature and other evidence on neonatal screening programmes and technologies for inborn errors of metabolism.
target: Our first goal in this article was to establish a database of literature and other evidence on newborn screening programmes and technology for genetic disorders related to metabolism.

source: To undertake a systematic review of the data as a basis for evaluation of newborn screening for inborn errors of metabolism.
target: We also aimed to rigorously review data to evaluate newborn screening for genetic disorders related to metabolism.

source: To prepare an objective summary of the evidence on the appropriateness and need for various existing and possible neonatal screening programmes for inborn errors of metabolism in relation to the natural history of these diseases.
target: We aimed to summarize the evidence for whether various newborn screening programs for genetic disorders related to metabolism are appropriate and necessary. We aimed to make this summary in relation to the natural history of these diseases, and for both existing and possible programs.

source: To identify gaps in existing knowledge and make recommendations for required primary research.
target: We further aimed to identify gaps in knowledge and make recommendations for further research that is needed.

source: To make recommendations for the future development and organisation of neonatal screening for inborn errors of metabolism in the UK.
target: Finally, we aimed to make recommendations for the future development and organisation of newborn screening for genetic disorders related to metabolism in the UK.

source: HOW THE RESEARCH WAS CONDUCTED. There were three parts to the research.
target: There were three parts to this research.

source: A systematic review of the literature on inborn errors of metabolism, neonatal screening programmes, new technologies for screening and economic factors.
target: We rigorously reviewed research papers on genetic disorders related to metabolism, newborn screening programs, new technologies for screening, and economic factors.

source: Inclusion and exclusion criteria were applied, and a working database of relevant papers was established.
target: We decided how we would include or exclude papers, and made a working database of relevant papers.

source: All selected papers were read by two or three experts and were critically appraised using a standard format.
target: All the papers we selected were read by two or three experts and were critically assessed using a standard format.

source: Seven criteria for a screening programme, based on the principles formulated by Wilson and Jungner (WHO, 1968), were used to summarise the evidence.
target: Seven criteria for a screening programme, based on the principles formulated by Wilson and Jungner (WHO, 1968), were used to summarise the evidence.

source: These were as follows.
target: These were the following:

source: Clinically and biochemically well-defined disorder.
target: The disorder is well defined by signs and symptoms.

source: Known incidence in populations relevant to the UK.
target: The disease occurs in populations relevant to the UK.

source: Disorder associated with significant morbidity or mortality.
target: The disorder is associated with serious health problems or death.

source: Effective treatment available.
target: Effective treatment is available for the disease.

source: Period before onset during which intervention improves outcome.
target: The disorder can be helped by addressing it in the time before it appears.

source: Ethical, safe, simple and robust screening test.
target: There is an ethical, safe, simple and robust screening test for the disorder.

source: Cost-effectiveness of screening.
target: Screening for the disorder is cost-effective.

source: A questionnaire which was sent to all newborn screening laboratories in the UK.
target: A questionnaire was sent to all newborn screening laboratories in the UK.

source: Site visits to assess new methodologies for newborn screening.
target: There are site visits to assess new methodologies for newborn screening.

source: The classical definition of an inborn error of metabolism was used (i.e., a monogenic disease resulting in deficient activity in a single enzyme in a pathway of intermediary metabolism).
target: We used the traditional way of defining a genetic disorder related to metabolism. This was that the disorder was caused by a single mutated gene that affected a single type of enzyme needed for metabolism.

source: RESEARCH FINDINGS. INBORN ERRORS OF METABOLISM. Phenylketonuria (PKU) (incidence 1:12,000) fulfilled all the screening criteria and could be used as the 'gold standard' against which to review other disorders despite significant variation in methodologies, sample collection and timing of screening and inadequacies in the infrastructure for notification and continued care of identified patients.
target: We found that phenylketonuria matched all the                                  criteria. This disorder could be used as a gold standard when comparing other disorders, even though the screening process can vary and systems for notifying patients are not good enough.

source: Of the many disorders of organic acid and fatty acid metabolism, a case can only be made for the introduction of newborn screening for glutaric aciduria type 1 (GA1; estimated incidence 1:40,000) and medium-chain acyl CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency (estimated incidence 1:8000-1:15,000).
target: Of the many disorders of metabolism of organic and fatty acids, we can only make the case for newborn screening of Glutaric Aciduria type 1 (GA1) and Medium-Chain Acy-CoA Dehydrogenase (MCAD deficiency).

source: Therapeutic advances for GA1 offer prevention of neurological damage but further investigation is required into the costs and benefits of screening for this disorder.
target: Advancements in therapy for GA1 can prevent damage to the brain and nerves but more research into the costs and benefits of screening for this disorder is needed.

source: MCAD deficiency is simply and cheaply treatable, preventing possible early death and neurological handicap.
target: MCAD deficiency can be treated simply and cheaply, preventing possible early death and brain handicap.

source: Neonatal screening for these diseases is dependent upon the introduction of tandem mass spectrometry (tandem MS).
target: Newborn screening for these diseases depends on the introduction of tandem Mass Spectrometry (tandem MS) technology.

source: This screening could however also simultaneously detect some other commonly-encountered disorders of organic acid metabolism with a collective incidence of 1:15,000.
target: But, this screening could also detect some other disorders at the same time. These other disorders are related to organic acid metabolism and happen to one in 15,000 people.

source: Neonatal screening for congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency (incidence 1:17,000) has been shown to be beneficial in other countries and similar benefits should accrue in the UK.
target: Newborn screening for congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) has worked well in other countries and the UK should see similar benefits. This disease occurs in one in 17,000 people.

source: A national programme of neonatal screening for CAH would be justified, with reassessment after an agreed period.
target: A national programme of newborn screening for CAH would be justified, and could be looked at again after an agreed period of time.

source: Biotinidase deficiency is of low incidence in the UK (estimated 1:100,000), but this may be outweighed by the simplicity of the screening methodology and the benefits in prevention.
target: Another disorder, biotinidase deficiency, is rare in the UK (estimated at one in 100,000), but that may be outweighed by how simple the screening process is and the benefits of preventing the disease.

source: The aim of newborn screening is to identify presymptomatic healthy infants that will develop significant metabolic or endocrine derangements if left undiagnosed and untreated.
target: The aim of newborn screening is to identify healthy infants that will develop significant metabolic or endocrine disorders if left undiagnosed and untreated.

source: The goal of ultimately reducing or eliminating irreversible sequelae is reached by maximizing test sensitivity of the primary newborn screening that measures specific analytes by a number of methodologies.
target: Newborn screening measures specific substances in various ways. Using the most sensitive screening tests can ultimately reduce or eliminate permanent consequences.

source: Differentiation of true from false negatives is accomplished by the test specificity.
target: Specificity of a test distinguishes true negative results from false negative results (when the test is negative but the patient actually has the disease).

source: This review discusses disorders for which, in general, there are available therapies and that are detected by routine and expanded newborn screening.
target: In general, this article discusses disorders that have treatments and that can be detected by routine and expanded newborn screening.

source: Recommendations are presented for evaluation by a primary care physician, with confirmation by a metabolic or endocrinology specialist.
target: We give recommendations for testing by a general practice doctor, with confirmation by a metabolic or endocrine specialist.

source: Disorders are organized in tabular format by class of pathway or analyte, with attention to typical clinical presentations, confirmatory biochemical and molecular tests, and therapies.
target: We organize disorders in a table by the physical process they affect in the body or by the substance that is tested. In doing this we also pay attention to how the disease first appears, lab tests that can confirm the disorders, and treatments available.

source: There are numerous challenges in clinical follow-up, including diagnosis and appropriate understanding of the consequences of the disorders.
target: There are many challenges for following up with patients, including diagnosis and appropriate understanding of the consequences of the disorders.

source: The data required to meet these challenges can be acquired only by large scale longitudinal comprehensive studies of outcome in children identified by newborn screening.
target: Meeting these challenges will require information. This can only be gained with large-scale studies that follow children identified by newborn screening over time to see their outcomes.

source: Only with such data can newborn screening fully serve families.
target: Newborn screening can only fully serve families once we have this knowledge.

source: The aim of newborn screening is to detect newborns with serious, treatable disorders so as to facilitate appropriate interventions to avoid or ameliorate adverse outcomes.
target: The goal of newborn screening is to detect newborns with serious, treatable disorders. This allows appropriate action to be taken to prevent or lessen bad outcomes.

source: Mass biochemical testing of newborn babies was pioneered in the 1960s with the introduction of screening for phenylketonuria, a rare inborn error of metabolism, tested by using a dried blood spot sample.
target: Large-scale lab testing of newborn babies was pioneered in the 1960s with the introduction of screening for phenylketonuria, a rare, inherited metabolic disorder. This testing was done by using dried blood spot samples.

source: The next disorder introduced into screening programs was congenital hypothyroidism and a few more much rarer disorders were gradually included.
target: The next disorder introduced into screening programs was congenital hypothyroidism, which is an inherited disorder of the thyroid gland, which controls metabolism. A few more much rarer disorders were gradually included.

source: Two recent advances have greatly changed the pace: modification of tandem mass spectrometry and DNA extraction and analysis from newborn screening dried blood spot.
target: Two recent advances have greatly change the pace. These are modification of tandem mass spectrometry (an advanced chemical detection technique, also known as MS/MS) and DNA analysis from newborn screening dried blood spots.

source: These two technologies make the future possibilities of newborn screening seem almost unlimited.
target: These two technologies make the future possibilities of newborn screening seem almost unlimited.

source: Newborn screening tests are usually carried out on a dried blood spot sample, for which there are special analytical considerations.
target: Newborn screening test are usually done with a dried blood spot sample, which needs to be considered when analyzing.

source: Dried blood spot calibrators and controls, prepared on the same lot number of filter paper, are needed.
target: Extra dried blood spots need to be prepared on the same filter paper to calibrate the tests.

source: Methods have a co-efficient of variation of about 10% due to the increased variability of a dried filter paper sample compared with other biochemical samples.
target: Since dried filter paper samples have more variability than other types of samples, the results of these tests can vary by about 10% or more.

source: The haematocrit is an additional variable not able to be measured.
target: Additionally, the amount of red blood cells cannot be measured.

source: Also of importance is obtaining a balance between the sensitivity and specificity of each assay.
target: It is also important to balance how sensitive the test is (how good it is at finding a problem) with how specific it is (how sure you can be when a problem is found).

source: Fixing cut-off points for action needs consideration of what is an acceptable percentage of the population to recall for further testing.
target: In deciding how to balance these, we need to consider how many people it is acceptable to bring back for more testing.

source: Few assays are 100% discriminatory.
target: Not many tests are perfectly accurate.

source: Programs in Australasia currently screen for at least 30 disorders.
target: Programs in Australasia currently screen for at least 30 disorders.

source: Detection of these requires not only the assay of a primary marker but often determination of a ratio of that marker with another, or possibly an alternative assay, for example a DNA mutation.
target: Detecting these disorders often requires more than just knowing one amount. It can require knowing relative amounts of two different things, or could require an additional alternative test, like a genetic test.

source: The most important disorders screened for are described briefly: phenylketonuria, primary congenital hypothyroidism, cystic fibrosis, the galactosaemias, medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency, glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency and congenital adrenal hyperplasia, together with several other disorders detectable by tandem mass spectrometry.
target: In this article we will briefly describe the most important disorders that are screened for. These are phenylketonuria, hypothyroidism, Cystic Fibrosis (CF), galactosaemias, Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency, Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase (GCDH) deficiency and Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH), along with several other disorders detectable by MS/MS.

source: Newborn screening deals with rare disorders and benefit cannot be shown easily without very large pilot studies.
target: Newborn screening deals with rare disorders and it is hard to see the benefit without very large studies.

source: There have been randomised controlled trials of screening for cystic fibrosis, and now several studies are beginning to establish the benefit of tandem mass spectrometry screening for disorders of fatty acid and amino acid metabolism.
